# Business Requirements Document (BRD)
## Gemini 8-Metre Telescopes Control System Software
### Project ID: 1995-GEMINI
### Version 1.0 | Classification: Internal — Pre-Requirements Baseline

---

## 1. PROBLEM STATEMENT

The Gemini Observatory is commissioning two 8-metre class telescopes (one on Mauna Kea, Hawaii, and one on Cerro Pachón, Chile) that will operate as a shared international facility serving a geographically dispersed community of astronomers. The observatory faces a fundamental operational challenge: how to maximise the scientific return from two of the world's most powerful ground-based telescopes while serving a user community that ranges from occasional visiting observers to highly experienced resident staff, across multiple time zones and institutional affiliations.

The core business problem has several measurable dimensions:

**Telescope utilisation inefficiency.** Traditional interactive observing — where a single astronomer manually controls the telescope in real time — is inherently wasteful. Weather changes, instrument reconfiguration delays, and the inability to match observing conditions to the optimal science programme mean that a significant fraction of available dark time is lost. The observatory requires a control system that enables queue-based and flexible scheduling so that the telescope is always executing the highest-priority science compatible with current conditions.

**Downtime risk.** The observatory's General System Requirements (GSR) mandate that total system downtime due to failures must not exceed 2% of available observing time (approximately 15 minutes per night, or one night per month). No existing control system architecture has been designed to this specification for a facility of this complexity. Without a purpose-built, fault-tolerant software system, the observatory cannot meet its contractual obligations to partner institutions and time-allocation committees.

**Multi-site operational complexity.** The observatory must support simultaneous operations from the telescope summit, a base facility, a remote operations facility, and home institutions of visiting astronomers. No single existing telescope control system supports this level of distributed, multi-user, multi-mode operation. Without a unified software architecture, coordination between on-site operators, remote observers, and support staff will be error-prone and operationally unsafe.

**Instrument proliferation.** The Gemini telescopes will routinely carry three scientific instruments simultaneously at the Cassegrain focus, with additional visitor instruments brought by observers. Managing parallel instrument access, calibration, and standby operations while only one instrument has the telescope beam at any given time requires a level of software coordination that exceeds the capability of conventional telescope control systems.

**Lack of automation for service and queue observing.** The observatory's science model depends heavily on service observing — where Gemini staff execute programmes on behalf of remote astronomers — and queue-based scheduling. Neither mode is feasible without a rich, automated observing programme environment. Currently, no such environment exists that is tailored to the Gemini operational model.

---

## 2. BUSINESS OBJECTIVES & SUCCESS CRITERIA

The Gemini Control System software must achieve the following business objectives, each accompanied by measurable success criteria derived from the operational requirements.

### Objective 1: Maximise Telescope Scientific Productivity
The system must enable the observatory to execute the maximum number of high-quality science observations per available night by supporting queue-based, service, and flexible scheduling modes.

**Success Criteria:**
- Queue-based observing shall be the default operational mode, with manual interactive operation available only as an exception enabled by operations staff.
- The system shall support flexible scheduling with instrument and mode switching at least twice per night without loss of observing time beyond defined overhead limits.
- All possible concurrencies in telescope and instrument operation shall be exploited; the Sequencer shall be the primary mechanism for achieving this.
- Telescope start-up from cold state shall complete within approximately 5 minutes (software) plus 4 minutes (telescope) plus 2 minutes (instrument) = approximately 11 minutes total.
- Warm restart (excluding software download) shall complete within approximately 1 minute.

### Objective 2: Meet System Availability and Reliability Targets
The control system must not be a significant source of observatory downtime.

**Success Criteria:**
- Total system downtime attributable to software and control failures shall not exceed 2% of available observing time (requirement), with a goal of 1%.
- Recovery from any single non-fatal subsystem failure shall be achieved within 5 minutes of onset of the error condition, returning the telescope to an observing state.
- Command handshaking between IOCs shall occur within 100–200 milliseconds.
- Command acknowledgement timeouts shall be supported at approximately 500 milliseconds.
- Peak control information throughput shall support at least 100 transactions per second (TPS) across the system.
- The system shall support continuous monitoring of all active and inactive subsystems to detect degradation before failure.

### Objective 3: Support Distributed, Multi-User Operations
The system must enable simultaneous, coordinated operation from multiple physical locations without compromising safety or data integrity.

**Success Criteria:**
- The system shall support simultaneous operation of up to six active control nodes and two monitoring nodes (one local, one remote) without appreciable performance degradation.
- The system shall be capable of handling up to ten active nodes under peak load conditions.
- Any authorised user shall be able to access any part of the system from any node via a single logon and configuration operation, subject to privilege controls.
- Remote operations shall provide all capabilities available in on-site operations, with only performance degradation (not functional loss) resulting from WAN bandwidth limitations.

### Objective 4: Ensure Data Integrity and Throughput
Astronomical data must be captured, stored, and made available for quality assessment without becoming a bottleneck to observations.

**Success Criteria:**
- The LAN shall support data transfer rates of 20–40 Mbits/second to accommodate concurrent multi-user data access.
- Detector readout for focusing activities shall complete within approximately 0.1 seconds (partial readout).
- Full readout of large mosaicked optical detectors shall complete within 2–3 minutes.
- The system shall retain at least 7 days of data from the largest instrument, with the most recent 3 days accessible interactively from hard disk.
- All astronomical data shall be archived automatically during observing and maintenance operations in a format compatible with the Gemini Archive and FITS standard.
- Quick-look data quality assessment shall be available on-line during observations.

### Objective 5: Provide a Safe Operating Environment
The system must protect personnel and equipment at all times.

**Success Criteria:**
- The system shall implement a layered safety architecture (software limits → soft limit switches → software watchdogs → hardware watchdogs → hard limit switches → hardware interlocks → mechanical hard stops) with no bypass possible at the hardware interlock level.
- Safety monitoring shall be self-executing; the system shall bring itself to a safe state without requiring human intervention when danger is detected.
- Range and validity checking shall be applied to all input commands before execution.

### Objective 6: Deliver a Maintainable, Evolvable Software Platform
Given that the software will be developed over multiple years and must accommodate evolving technology and new instruments, the architecture must support long-term maintainability.

**Success Criteria:**
- Addition of a new instrument shall require no modification to existing operational software; changes shall be confined to operational procedures and configuration.
- All software modules shall carry version labels retrievable at runtime via control commands.
- On-line version control shall be available at all times to recover or restore any software version.
- Every software module shall have corresponding test specifications usable for both acceptance testing and on-line verification.
- All equipment shall support a programmed adjustment and maintenance interval of 30 days or longer without requiring unscheduled intervention.

---

## 3. STAKEHOLDERS

### 3.1 Observing Astronomers (Primary End Users)
**Role:** Science programme owners and on-site or remote observers who use the telescope to acquire astronomical data.

**Range:** From occasional visiting observers with limited system familiarity to experienced staff astronomers with deep knowledge of the facility.

**Primary Need:** To concentrate on science — target acquisition, exposure management, and on-line data quality assessment — without needing to understand the details of telescope and instrument control. They require a simple, secure, and learnable interface that abstracts away low-level control complexity.

**Key Constraint:** Observing astronomers shall have no direct telescope control privileges. They interact with the system through the scheduler and observing programme environment. They do, however, typically have direct control access to instruments.

---

### 3.2 Operations Staff (Night Assistants, Telescope Operators, Operations Supervisors, Instrument Operators)
**Role:** Responsible for the safe and efficient nightly operation of the telescope. They supervise telescope operation, advise observing astronomers, monitor system performance and safety, and intervene when problems arise.

**Primary Need:** Full privileged access to all commands, maintenance procedures, and direct control of physical units when required. Ability to update operational tables (e.g. filter lists), change the operational status of subsystems, and manage the observing queue.

**Key Constraint:** Direct access to subsystems is only permitted when those subsystems are not in normal operation; appropriate reconfiguration procedures must be followed to gain access during normal operations.

---

### 3.3 Software Development and Maintenance Staff
**Role:** Responsible for developing, testing, installing, and maintaining the Gemini control system software. May be located at the telescope site, base facility, remote operations facility, or at contracted external organisations.

**Primary Need:** Highest-level system privileges to modify any component of the system. Access to test-level operation modes, integration test environments, and the virtual telescope simulator. Must follow strict configuration control guidelines to avoid interfering with ongoing operations.

**Key Constraint:** Work is typically performed at the test operation level; other parts of the system may need to run in test mode concurrently to support integration testing.

---

### 3.4 Observatory Directorate and Science Operations Management
**Role:** Sets the policies governing telescope scheduling, programme priorities, and operational rules (e.g. preemption limits, overhead tolerances for queue resorting).

**Primary Need:** Confidence that the scheduling software enforces observatory policy, that science time is maximised, and that the system supports the observatory's commitments to partner institutions and time-allocation bodies.

---

### 3.5 Remote and Service Observers (Gemini Staff Acting on Behalf of Astronomers)
**Role:** Gemini staff who execute observing programmes on behalf of client astronomers who are not present at the telescope.

**Primary Need:** Access to the astronomer's observing programme, including any special notes or instructions from the astronomer; ability to review, adjust, and execute the programme; and ability to communicate with the remote client astronomer.

---

### 3.6 Remote Monitoring Users (Supervisors, Support Staff, Expert Advisors)
**Role:** Users who need to observe system status and ongoing operations without taking control, either for supervisory purposes or to provide expert diagnostic support.

**Primary Need:** Read-only access to all relevant telescope and instrument status information from any location, without affecting the performance of ongoing observations.

---

### 3.7 Visitor Instrument Teams
**Role:** External groups who bring specialised instruments to the telescope for limited observing runs.

**Primary Need:** A standardised, well-defined interface that allows their instrument to participate in the Gemini operational environment — receiving status queries, accepting preprogrammed observing sequences, and requesting telescope offsets and focus adjustments — without requiring full integration into the Gemini software environment.

---

### 3.8 Archive and Data Management (Gemini Archive Subsystem)
**Role:** Receives, stores, and provides access to all astronomical data produced by the telescope.

**Primary Need:** Data delivered in a consistent, standards-compliant format (FITS) with complete header information, automatically and reliably, during all observing and maintenance operations.

---

## 4. CURRENT STATE vs FUTURE STATE

### 4.1 Current State — Limitations

**Traditional Interactive Observing Model.** Existing telescope control systems at comparable observatories are designed around the model of a single astronomer interactively controlling the telescope in real time. This model is fundamentally incompatible with the Gemini operational concept of service observing, queue scheduling, and flexible scheduling. It requires the astronomer to be physically present and to manage low-level telescope and instrument commands directly, which is both inefficient and error-prone.

**No Queue or Flexible Scheduling Infrastructure.** There is no existing software infrastructure capable of managing a dynamic queue of observing programmes, resorting that queue based on current conditions, and executing programmes automatically with minimal human interaction. Scheduling decisions are made manually by the night assistant, with no software support for optimising against weather conditions, instrument configurations, and programme priorities simultaneously.

**Single-Site, Single-User Architecture.** Existing telescope control systems are designed for operation from a single control room by a small, co-located team. There is no provision for distributed multi-site access, remote observing, or multi-point monitoring at the level required by Gemini's international partnership model.

**No Virtual Telescope or Simulation Environment.** Astronomers currently have no means to test and validate their observing programmes before arriving at the telescope. Programme errors are discovered during the observing run itself, wasting precious telescope time.

**Fragmented Instrument Control.** Instruments at existing facilities are typically controlled as independent systems with their own interfaces, conventions, and software environments. There is no unified framework for managing multiple simultaneously mounted instruments, supporting hot-standby configurations, or enabling parallel calibration activities on inactive instruments.

**Limited Fault Recovery.** Existing systems typically require manual intervention and significant downtime to recover from subsystem failures. There are no automated reconfiguration procedures to allow continued observing with degraded equipment.

**No Standardised Visitor Instrument Interface.** Visitor instruments are accommodated on an ad hoc basis, requiring significant custom integration work for each visit and providing no guarantee of consistent behaviour within the control system.

### 4.2 Future State — Capabilities Enabled by the New System

**Queue-Based Observing as the Default Mode.** The new system will make queue-based observing the standard operational mode. Observing programmes will be submitted in advance, validated against a full telescope simulator, and executed automatically by a Sequencer with minimal human interaction. The existence of the queue will be transparent to on-site observers during initial operations, easing the transition.

**Flexible Scheduling with Software Decision Support.** A scheduling software component will advise operations staff on the optimal programme to execute given current conditions, instrument configurations, and programme priorities. The queue will be dynamically resortable, and instrument/mode switching will be achievable at least twice per night.

**Fully Distributed, Multi-Site Operations.** Any authorised user will be able to access any part of the system from any node — on-site, at the base facility, at the remote operations facility, or at a home institution — via a single logon. Remote operations will provide full functional equivalence to on-site operations, subject only to WAN bandwidth constraints.

**Virtual Telescope and Full Simulation Environment.** Every hardware subsystem will provide a software simulation module. Astronomers will be able to test and validate their observing programmes completely within the virtual telescope environment before the observing run, eliminating programme errors as a source of telescope time loss.

**Unified Multi-Instrument Management.** The system will provide parallel access to all mounted instruments simultaneously, with one instrument active on the telescope beam and others able to perform calibrations, prepare for observations, or operate independently at any level. A standardised visitor instrument interface will allow external instruments to participate in the Gemini environment with a defined, minimal integration effort.

**Automated Fault Detection, Recovery, and Reconfiguration.** The system will continuously monitor all subsystems, automatically attempt recovery from failures, reconfigure the observing environment to continue with remaining equipment where possible, and bring the system to a safe state when required — all within a target recovery time of 5 minutes.

**Homogeneous, Network-Transparent User Interface.** A single, consistent user interface framework — portable across hardware platforms and network-transparent — will present a uniform look and feel regardless of which subsystem is being operated or from which location the user is working.

---

## 5. BUSINESS RULES

The following domain-specific rules govern system behaviour and must be enforced by the software at all times.

### 5.1 Access Control and Privilege Rules

**BR-AC-01:** The system shall maintain a minimum of three distinct privilege levels: Observing Astronomer, Operations Staff, and Software Development/Maintenance Staff. Privileges shall be determined at login and enforced throughout the session.

**BR-AC-02:** Observing astronomers shall have no privilege to send direct control commands to the telescope mount or telescope subsystems. They may query the status of any subsystem at any time. Observing programmes (as automated entities) may request telescope control functions; individual astronomers may not issue such commands interactively.

**BR-AC-03:** Observing astronomers shall have read-only access to operational tables (e.g. the list of filters mounted on an instrument). Operations staff shall have update access to these tables.

**BR-AC-04:** Observing astronomers shall normally have direct control access to instruments. This is distinct from telescope control.

**BR-AC-05:** Operations staff shall have full privileged access to all commands and maintenance procedures, including direct control of physical units. However, they shall not access subsystems that are in normal operation without first executing the appropriate reconfiguration procedure.

**BR-AC-06:** Software development and maintenance staff shall have the highest privilege level, enabling modification of any system component. All such work must comply with configuration control guidelines and must not interfere with ongoing normal operations.

**BR-AC-07:** All users wishing to monitor Gemini operations (beyond the designated supervisor) must obtain permission through procedures established by Operations.

**BR-AC-08:** Remote access to system features may be restricted to specific authorised remote sites (e.g. Hale Pohaku, Hilo, Cerro Tololo). Security controls shall enforce these site-based restrictions.

**BR-AC-09:** Critical resources that can support only a restricted number of simultaneous uses shall be assigned exclusively through the Access Mode Allocation system. The allocation system must be deadlock-free.

### 5.2 Operational Mode Rules

**BR-OM-01:** Queue-based automatic operation shall be the normal observing mode. Direct interactive operation shall be available only when explicitly enabled by operations staff and shall not be considered the standard mode.

**BR-OM-02:** The existence of the scheduling queue shall be transparent to on-site observers during the initial phases of telescope operation.

**BR-OM-03:** The system shall operate at three defined levels: Observing, Maintenance, and Test. The operational software shall clearly indicate the current operation level to all users at all times.

**BR-OM-04:** The system shall check and display the compatibility between subsystems operating in different modes. Incompatible mode combinations shall be flagged and prevented.

**BR-OM-05:** Test-level operation shall permit installation and testing of new packages, execution of any low-level test, and update of all non-protected parameter values. Access to test level shall be extremely limited.

**BR-OM-06:** Interactive operation shall be available from automatic operation to handle exceptional conditions. It is the normal mode at maintenance and test levels.

**BR-OM-07:** Inactive instruments (those not currently receiving the telescope beam) shall be permitted to: (a) take calibration or flat-field exposures in parallel; (b) prepare for an exposure in hot-standby mode; and (c) operate at all foreseen operation levels independently.

### 5.3 Instrument and Configuration Rules

**BR-IC-01:** Only one instrument shall have access to the telescope beam at any given time (the active instrument). An instrument feeding two detectors via beam splitters or field splitters is considered a single active instrument.

**BR-IC-02:** Instrument start-up and shutdown shall be possible independently of the telescope and without affecting telescope operation.

**BR-IC-03:** The definition of the observing environment (instrument combination, light path) shall be dynamic — changeable during operations without restarting the telescope or other instruments.

**BR-IC-04:** Operations staff shall have exclusive privilege to change the observing environment (i.e. select the active instrument combination).

**BR-IC-05:** The operational software shall know which subsystems are installed and operational at all times.

**BR-IC-06:** Visitor instruments must operate as servers responding to commands from upper system levels. They must support, at minimum: status acquisition by other systems, entry of preprogrammed observing sequences, and telescope position and focus offset requests.

**BR-IC-07:** Telescope software at both Gemini telescopes shall be maintained as identical in the upper software layers, even if the underlying hardware differs.

### 5.4 Scheduling and Queue Rules

**BR-SQ-01:** The observing queue shall be resortable at any time. Resorting shall be based on properties of individual observing sequences, current site conditions, and rules established by the observatory directorate.

**BR-SQ-02:** Scheduling software shall advise operations staff on the optimal programme selection but shall not make autonomous scheduling decisions without human confirmation. The software shall have an operational mode that supports the decision-making process by presenting relevant information without enforcing a choice.

**BR-SQ-03:** Flexible scheduling shall support switching between telescope modes and instruments at least twice per night.

**BR-SQ-04:** It shall be possible to queue all observations possible with currently available instruments in the form of preprogrammed observing sequences.

### 5.5 Data Management Rules

**BR-DM-01:** All astronomical data shall be stored in a Gemini-standard format used across all instruments. Data shall be transmitted between Gemini and home institutions in FITS format with complete header information.

**BR-DM-02:** There shall always be at least two copies of acquired data: one secured copy as acquired, and one copy for on-line quality assessment (preferably on removable media).

**BR-DM-03:** Archiving of data shall occur automatically during observing and maintenance level operations. Data output format shall be compatible with Gemini Archive requirements.

**BR-DM-04:** The system shall retain at least 7 days of data from the largest instrument, with the most recent 3 days accessible interactively.

**BR-DM-05:** Data may be compressed using loss-less techniques for LAN/WAN transmission and storage. Loss-based compression (e.g. JPEG, MPEG) is permitted only for rough video images transmitted every 0.5 seconds. High-quality image transmission must use loss-less compression and complete within 20 seconds.

**BR-DM-06:** For detector data requiring preprocessing (e.g. infrared), only the preprocessed data shall be stored.

### 5.6 Safety Rules

**BR-SF-01:** Safety protection shall be applied whenever control software actions could endanger personnel or damage any subsystem (e.g. driving beyond mechanical limits, overexposing detectors).

**BR-SF-02:** Safety protection shall be implemented independently of the control software. The layered safety architecture (software limits, soft limit switches, software watchdogs, hardware watchdogs, hard limit switches, hardware interlocks, mechanical hard stops) shall be enforced in that order of precedence.

**BR-SF-03:** Hardware interlocks shall have no bypass. No software or hardware action shall be capable of overriding a hardware interlock.

**BR-SF-04:** The system shall bring itself to a safe state automatically upon detection of a safety-relevant condition, without requiring human intervention.

### 5.7 Fault and Error Management Rules

**BR-FE-01:** All errors shall be classified into one of three categories: Fatal (no acceptable recovery; system falls to safe backup state requiring human restart), Serious (operation cannot complete; human intervention required to restart full operation), or Warning (unexpected condition logged; operation continues, possibly with reduced efficiency).

**BR-FE-02:** Alarms shall be formally distinguished from errors. Automatically monitored alarms shall clear automatically when the error condition resolves. Alarms requiring human acknowledgement shall persist until acknowledged.

**BR-FE-03:** Failure of one subsystem shall not cascade to affect other working subsystems or communication links.

**BR-FE-04:** All fault notifications shall identify the origin and nature of the problem specifically. All notifications shall be capable of electronic logging. Multiple verbosity levels shall be supported.

**BR-FE-05:** Command retries shall be implemented automatically for common timeout and no-response conditions, to avoid unnecessary error escalation.

### 5.8 Monitoring Rules

**BR-MO-01:** Monitoring shall not affect the performance of ongoing observations under any circumstances.

**BR-MO-02:** Status information shall be available both as automatic displays at defined locations and as explicit on-demand queries from any point in the system.

**BR-MO-03:** Control information on all controlled variables shall be provided by all subsystems on request. No status request shall cause a delay in control activities or produce a locking condition, even if the corresponding equipment is unavailable or faulty.

**BR-MO-04:** Meteorological information from the weather station shall be available centrally to all users.

**BR-MO-05:** Engineering data shall be loggable at up to 200 Hz for short periods and at up to 1 Hz for long-term logging, into a common format (baselined as SYBASE).

---

## 6. SCOPE

### 6.1 In Scope

The following capabilities and components are within the scope of the Gemini Control System software:

- **Telescope Control Software:** All software for controlling telescope mount, enclosure, active optics, guiding, and associated subsystems at both Gemini telescope sites.
- **Instrument Control Software:** Control software for all facility instruments mounted at the Cassegrain focus, including parallel management of up to three simultaneously mounted instruments.
- **Visitor Instrument Interface:** A standardised subset interface enabling visitor instruments to participate in the Gemini operational environment.
- **Observing Programme Environment:** A visually oriented, rich programming environment for astronomers to create, test, and submit automated observing programmes, including a full telescope simulator operating within the virtual telescope environment.
- **Sequencer and Queue Management:** Software to manage the observing queue, execute programmes automatically, support resorting and resequencing, and provide the scheduling decision-support tool.
- **User Interface Framework:** A homogeneous, network-transparent, portable user interface toolkit providing a consistent look and feel across all subsystems and all user locations.
- **Data Acquisition and Storage:** All software for detector readout, data formatting, on-site storage, and automatic archiving to the Gemini Archive subsystem.
- **Quick-Look Data Quality Assessment:** On-line image and pixel analysis tools for assessing data quality during observations, including standard reduction procedures for basic calibrations.
- **On-Line Database:** A memory-resident, distributed database holding all telescope, instrument, and detector control parameters, accessible at all operation levels with defined access times.
- **Access Control and Privilege Management:** The Access Mode Allocation system, login-based privilege determination, and all associated security enforcement mechanisms.
- **Fault Detection, Logging, and Recovery:** Built-in test facilities, self-test sequences, error classification and logging, alarm management, and automated recovery and reconfiguration procedures.
- **Remote Operations Infrastructure:** All software enabling remote observing, remote monitoring, remote diagnostics, and remote maintenance from base, support, and remote operations facilities, including WAN interface software.
- **Multi-Point Monitoring:** Software enabling simultaneous read-only monitoring of all telescope and instrument status from multiple locations.
- **Communication Infrastructure Software:** Software protocols, LAN management, time distribution interfaces, and WAN connectivity software (TCP/IP, ARPA services, NFS, RPC, IPC).
- **IOC Software Layer:** All Instrument/Input-Output Controller software including EPICS-based database, record support, device support, device drivers, and scanning mechanisms.
- **Simulation Modules:** Software simulation modules for every hardware subsystem, enabling virtual telescope operation independent of physical hardware.
- **Version Control and Configuration Management:** On-line version control, boot-time consistency checking, formal release system, and change logging.
- **Test and Verification Infrastructure:** Test specifications for all modules, regression test suites, self-test sequences, and the Software Test Plan framework.
- **Startup, Shutdown, and Reconfiguration Procedures:** Automated procedures for telescope and instrument startup/shutdown, dynamic reconfiguration, and environment switching.
- **Interface Routines to External Packages:** Interface routines connecting the Gemini system to external packages (IRAF, MIDAS, IDL, ADAM, Khoros, STARCAT, SYBASE/DBMS), which are considered part of the Gemini software system.
- **Star Catalogue Access:** Software providing computer access to star catalogues for automatic selection of guide stars and standard stars.
- **Voice and Video Infrastructure Software:** Software interfaces for site monitoring video, voice connectivity, autoguider cameras, and sky monitoring devices (cloud monitors, seeing monitors).
- **Human Engineering Compliance:** All user interface and workflow design elements addressing fatigue, stress, feedback, and team interaction requirements.

### 6.2 Out of Scope

The following are explicitly outside the scope of the Gemini Control System software:

- **Off-Line Data Reduction Software:** Packages such as IRAF, MIDAS, IDL, ADAM, and Khoros used for off-line science data reduction are not part of the Gemini software. (Interface routines connecting these packages to the Gemini system are in scope.)
- **Physical Hardware Design:** Mechanical, optical, and electronic hardware design for the telescope, instruments, enclosure, and infrastructure is not part of the software scope.
- **Visitor Instrument Hardware and Internal Software:** The internal software of visitor instruments is the responsibility of the visiting team. Only the standardised interface layer is in scope.
- **Central Archive Infrastructure:** The Gemini Archive subsystem itself (beyond the interface and automatic data delivery to it) is managed separately.
- **WAN Physical Infrastructure:** Physical cabling, satellite links, and telecommunications infrastructure between sites are not part of the software scope, though the software must operate over the defined WAN.
- **Science Data Analysis and Publication:** Post-observation science analysis, publication workflows, and proposal management systems are out of scope.
- **Full Hardware Redundancy Implementation:** Full hardware redundancy is not a system requirement. Redundancy decisions for specific subsystems are made on a cost-effectiveness basis and are not a general software requirement.
- **Point-to-Point Video Between Facilities:** Point-to-point video conferencing between Gemini operations facilities is not a requirement (though voice connectivity is in scope).

---

## 7. CONSTRAINTS

### 7.1 Technical Constraints

**TC-01 — Standards Compliance:** All software must use commercial packages, off-the-shelf public domain software, and industry standards wherever feasible. Proprietary, non-standard solutions are to be avoided.

**TC-02 — Programming Language and Portability:** All software that does not directly control specific hardware must be written as machine-independent, portable code. Even microprocessor-level software should be hardware-independent to allow later target processor selection.

**TC-03 — Operating Environment:** The IOC layer shall use VxWorks as the real-time operating system, EPICS as the IOC toolkit, and VME crate/CPU hardware. Workstations shall support Ethernet IEEE-802.3 and FDDI interfaces. Internal data formats must be compatible across all development and operational workstations.

**TC-04 — Network Architecture:** The control LAN shall be based on Ethernet IEEE 802.3. The backbone LAN shall use FDDI due to bandwidth requirements. The system shall include a Local Time Bus for time distribution and both a digital reflective memory bus and an analogue event-based bus for signals not satisfiable by LAN. WAN connectivity shall use TCP/IP (DARPA protocol) and must support standard ARPA services (telnet, FTP, SMTP), NFS, RPC, IPC, and Unix socket interfaces.

**TC-05 — WAN Bandwidth:** There shall be at least one dedicated WAN link of T1 bandwidth for remote support, development, and testing. All operational capability must function over this link, with performance degradation (not functional loss) acceptable.

**TC-06 — Database Access Performance:** The on-line database shall provide access times in the range of 2–3 milliseconds per access. Time-critical information shall be held in memory.

**TC-07 — Real-Time Control:** Strict real-time control is restricted to the IOC layer. Upper-layer software shall not impose real-time constraints on the IOC layer beyond defined interface specifications.

**TC-08 — Version Control System:** All Gemini software shall be developed using the CVS version control system. Version information must be retrievable from executing software via control commands.

**TC-09 — Simulation Requirement:** Every hardware subsystem must provide a software simulation module that responds in a reasonable fashion to commands directed at that hardware. Simulation modules may require a standard environment (VxWorks, EPICS, VME) but must not require any application-specific hardware.

**TC-10 — Hardware Platform Minimisation:** The number of distinct computer hardware platforms shall be kept to the minimum practical, to reduce maintenance burden and facilitate future migration.

**TC-11 — SCSI Peripherals:** SCSI-interface peripherals shall be available on development and operational workstations.

### 7.2 Operational Constraints

**OC-01 — Maintenance Interval:** All equipment shall support a programmed adjustment and maintenance interval of 30 days or longer. The software must not require more frequent mandatory maintenance interventions.

**OC-02 — No Automatic Failover for Hardware Redundancy:** Where hardware redundancy is implemented, switching to the backup system shall require a manual procedure. There is no requirement for automatic hardware failover.

**OC-03 — IOC Failure Non-Transferability:** In the event of IOC failure, control cannot be transferred to another IOC due to local hardware connections. Recovery requires physical replacement of faulty cards or assemblies.

**OC-04 — Configuration Control During Development:** Software development and maintenance staff must follow strict configuration control guidelines. Modifications to the live system must not interfere with ongoing normal operations.

**OC-05 — Telescope Software Identity:** The upper-layer telescope control software at both Gemini sites must be maintained as identical, even if lower-level hardware differs between the two telescopes.

**OC-06 — Instrument Independence:** Individual instruments must be able to run fully independently. Addition of a new instrument must not, as a goal, require modification to already operational software components.

### 7.3 Regulatory and Policy Constraints

**RC-01 — Observatory Directorate Scheduling Rules:** The scheduling software must enforce rules established by the observatory directorate regarding programme preemption limits, overhead tolerances, and priority weighting. These rules are subject to change and must be configurable without software recompilation (table-driven).

**RC-02 — Data Proprietary Periods:** The types of data available from the archive, the granularity of access (header only, averaged, full raw), and the time frames for availability (same night, weekly, after proprietary period) shall be governed by the Gemini Archiving Requirements document, which is an external policy constraint on this system.

**RC-03 — Partner Institution Access Rights:** Remote access restrictions to specific authorised sites reflect the observatory's international partnership agreements. The security system must be configurable to enforce site-based access controls.

### 7.4 Development Process Constraints

**DC-01 — Evolutionary Development:** Software shall be developed in evolutionary fashion, with incremental releases. The CVS version control system is mandated.

**DC-02 — Documentation Standards:** All software must be fully documented internally (code comments) and externally. External documentation must include Unix-style man pages.

**DC-03 — Error Reporting Standards:** All developers must adhere to a standard method for reporting and recording errors from both internal and external sources.

**DC-04 — Change Logging:** Developers must maintain accurate change logs showing all software modifications as applied to the system.

**DC-05 — Test Plan Requirement:** Test plans must be written for all individual software packages and modules. A formal release system must exist at package and module level, checkable on-line for consistency.

---

## 8. ASSUMPTIONS & DEPENDENCIES

### 8.1 Assumptions

**A-01:** The Gemini Observatory will operate two 8-metre telescopes, one in Hawaii and one in Chile, and both will use the same upper-layer control software architecture. Hardware differences between the two sites will be accommodated at the lower software layers without affecting the upper-layer software.

**A-02:** Queue-based observing will become the dominant operational mode once the system is commissioned and operational experience has been gained. The transition from traditional interactive observing will be managed operationally, with the queue initially transparent to on-site observers.

**A-03:** The WAN links between the telescope sites, base facilities, and remote operations facilities will provide at minimum T1-equivalent bandwidth for the dedicated remote support link. The software is designed to function with performance degradation (not functional loss) over constrained WAN links.

**A-04:** The EPICS toolkit, VxWorks real-time operating system, and VME crate/CPU hardware are the selected IOC platform. Changes to this selection would require reassessment of IOC-layer software requirements.

**A-05:** Workstations used for development and operations will be state-of-the-art systems at the time of procurement, supporting Ethernet IEEE-802.3 and FDDI interfaces, and will be from a scaleable family to allow technology migration.

**A-06:** The Gemini Archive subsystem will be developed and operated as a separate system. The control system software is responsible only for automatic delivery of data to the archive in the required format; the archive's internal operation is outside this system's scope.

**A-07:** Star catalogues required for guide star and standard star selection will be available on-line within the Gemini system. The specific catalogues and their access requirements will be defined in a separate catalogue specification document (referenced as TBD in the requirements).

**A-08:** The observatory directorate's scheduling rules (preemption limits, overhead tolerances, priority weighting) will be provided to the software team in a form suitable for implementation as configurable table-driven parameters. These rules are not yet fully defined and will evolve during the project.

**A-09:** Expert scheduling software capable of fully autonomous programme selection does not currently exist. The scheduling component will initially operate in a decision-support mode, presenting information to operations staff rather than making autonomous decisions.

**A-10:** Voice connectivity between all Gemini operations facilities will be provided on a permanent connection. The software system assumes this infrastructure is in place and does not need to provision it.

**A-11:** The human engineering requirements (fatigue minimisation, feedback design, team interaction support) will be addressed through the user interface design process and will be validated with representative users during acceptance testing.

**A-12:** The SYBASE database system is the baseline for long-term engineering data logging. This selection may be revisited as part of the standards definition process.

### 8.2 Dependencies

**D-01 — Gemini General System Requirements (GSR):** The reliability targets (2% downtime requirement, 1% goal) and other system-level performance requirements are derived from the GSR. Changes to the GSR may require reassessment of software reliability requirements.

**D-02 — Software Management Plan (SMP):** The phasing and scheduling of software development activities, including the sequencing of integration tests, is governed by the SMP. The BRD assumes the SMP is in place and that the phasing described therein is achievable.

**D-03 — Software Test Plan (STP):** Test procedure methods are to be defined in the STP. The BRD assumes the STP will be developed concurrently with the software and will cover all packages and modules.

**D-04 — Electronic Design Specification:** Cabling requirements, router and gateway placement, interlock connections, and WAN link specifications are defined in the Electronic Design Specification. The software system depends on the physical infrastructure described therein being implemented as specified.

**D-05 — Instrument Functional Requirements Specifications:** The specific functionality that each instrument control software module must expose is defined in per-instrument Functional Requirements Specifications. The control system software depends on these specifications being available and stable before instrument control software development begins.

**D-06 — Instrument Control System Infrastructure Work Package Definition:** The software interface to the interlock system is defined in this document. The safety layer implementation depends on this interface being defined and stable.

**D-07 — Gemini Archiving Requirements:** The data format, data types, access granularity, and proprietary period policies governing the archive interface are defined externally. The data management software depends on these requirements being finalised before the archive interface is implemented.

**D-08 — EPICS Community and APS Experience:** The system architecture draws on the experience of the EPICS community, including the Collaborative Access Teams at Argonne's Advanced Photon Source. Continued availability of EPICS as an open, community-supported toolkit is assumed. The Gemini system's use of Tcl/Tk and IDL for high-level sequencing and data reduction is consistent with this community's practice.

**D-09 — Time Reference System:** Several subsystems require synchronisation with the site Time Reference System. The Time Reference System is assumed to be a separate infrastructure component providing absolute and relative time signals via the Local Time Bus. The accuracy requirements for this synchronisation will be defined in the General Description document (Chapter 2 of the system specification).

**D-10 — CVS Version Control Infrastructure:** The CVS version control system must be operational and accessible to all development staff before software development commences. On-line version control must be available at all times during operations.

ground_truth_requirements.text: Definition Observing astronomers are the end users of the system. They range greatly in experience from occasional users of the system to very experienced users such as staff astronomers at the Gemini 8m Telescopes site and service observers. Purpose of work Whatever their level of experience, observing astronomers will wish to concentrate on the efficient acquisition of astronomical data and on-line assessment of data quality, rather than on the details of controlling the telescope and instruments. To allow observing astronomers to achieve this objective, the system must offer to a user an interface which, while fulfilling the various operational requirements in the different modes and offering status information both automatically and on request at any required level, is still simple to learn and secure in its use. Privileges Observing astronomers shall have no privileges as far as the direct control of the telescope is concerned. They shall not be able to send control commands directly but they must be able to enquire about the status of the telescope or any subsystem at any time. The intent is not to restrict the capabilities of the observing astronomer in any way but rather to establish a single point of control and responsibility. Programs, as opposed to observers, may have the capability of direct control of the telescope. This would allow the observer to create an observing program which requested a telescope control function but would not allow the observer to enter (for instance) a command to slew the mount. Astronomers are typically given control access to instruments, however. Operation mode Traditional interactive operation shall normally be replaced by operation via an automatic sequencer. This is essential to support operational requirements such as service observing and flexible scheduling (as defined later in Section 2.7 on page 2-12). A certain degree of interaction shall be provided, meaning in this case that the user will interact with the scheduler program, rather than with the control programs directly. Observing commands will normally be submitted via the User interface to a queue for later execution. It must also be possible to break and resequence this queue. e.g. as a result of the quality assessment of previous data. In particular, Operations staff will be able to enable direct interactive operation, but this shall not be considered as the normal operation mode for the reasons explained in Section 2.7 on page 2-12. It is evident that, for some functions (such as adjustment of spectrograph slit width for seeing conditions) it must be necessary to include interactive capability. However each instance of such a function should be examined as a candidate for automation - such as focussing. The existence of the scheduling queue shall be transparent to the on-site observer during the initial phases of telescope operation. Only after experience has been gained with the system will the existence of the queue become evident to the on site observer.
ground_truth_requirements.label: FR

ground_truth_requirements.text: Definition Currently, operations staff includes 'night assistants' and staff of the 'operations group'. Depending on the operations implemented on the Gemini 8m Telescopes, they may become telescope operators, operations supervisors of the Gemini 8m Telescopes telescope, and instrument operators and have to overview the Gemini 8m Telescopes operation. These users will be very experienced users of the system. They will be very familiar with the Gemini 8m Telescopes and/or its instruments. Purpose of work Operations staff will control the Gemini 8m Telescopes indirectly via a scheduler program or directly via commands. They will supervise telescope operation and will be able to advise observing astronomers on what they have to do to use telescope and instruments efficiently. They shall also monitor general performance and system safety. Privileges Operations staff shall have privileges to access all commands and maintenance procedures in case of problems. This includes direct control of physical units. However, they shall not have access to subsystems while these are in normal operation. If they need to access other parts of the system appropriate reconfiguration procedures have to be run. Operation mode Operations staff shall have access to operation tables in update mode, while observing astronomers will have access to them only in read mode (for example the list of filters mounted on an instrument). Again, the intent is not to restrict the capabilities of the observing astronomer in any way but rather to establish a single point of control and responsibility. At the present time it is not clear how to handle visitor instrument requirements in this area. They shall be able to change the operational status of units according to the results of tests performed on such units (e.g. to see if a faulty unit can be declared as operational again and redefined as part of the environment in use).
ground_truth_requirements.label: FR

ground_truth_requirements.text: Definition Software development and maintenance are staff: •based either at the Gemini 8m Telescopes site or base facility, or •based at the Gemini 8m Telescopes remote operations facility, or •non Gemini staff from a contract company or from an associated Institute. These users will have a deep understanding of the way the Gemini 8m Telescopes software works. Purpose of work Software development and maintenance staff intervene when there is a major problem to be solved or an upgrade to be installed. They also perform system generation and installation of new software packages or new releases, according to established test and validation procedures. Privileges Software development and maintenance staff need the highest privilege in order to be able to modify everything in the system. Nevertheless strict configuration control guidelines must be followed to prevent interference with ongoing normal operation. Operation mode Software development and maintenance staff usually work at the test level (see Section 3.2.1.3 on page 3-4) for the part of the software under test. Other parts of the Gemini 8m Telescopes may also need to run in test mode to support integration tests. This means that a number of commands are available which allow the hardware concerned to be tested (test commands) and are not accessible to observing astronomers.
ground_truth_requirements.label: FR

ground_truth_requirements.text: Normally three scientiﬁc instruments are mounted on the cassegrain focus of the telescope. The Gemini 8m Telescopes are thus characterized all the time as in a multi-instrument context. Parallel access to all the mounted instruments shall be provided, though only one instrument has access to the telescope beam (active instrument).Instruments which feed two detectors by using beam splitters or ﬁeld splitters is considered as a single active instrument. Feeding two separate instruments via beam splitters or ﬁeld splitters and providing separate control nodes is not a requirement. Various situations are possible for the other (inactive) instruments: Normally three scientiﬁc instruments are mounted on the cassegrain focus of the telescope. The Gemini 8m Telescopes are thus characterized all the time as in a multi-instrument context. Parallel access to all the mounted instruments shall be provided, though only one instrument has access to the telescope beam (active instrument).Instruments which feed two detectors by using beam splitters or ﬁeld splitters is considered as a single active instrument. Feeding two separate instruments via beam splitters or ﬁeld splitters and providing separate control nodes is not a requirement. Various situations are possible for the other (inactive) instruments: they shall be able to take calibration or ﬂat ﬁeld exposures in parallel they shall be able to prepare for an exposure to start as soon as the telescope beam is switched back to them (in this case, they are in a hot standby situation) they shall be able to work; at all foreseen operation levels (observing, maintenance, test) (see Section 3.2.1 on page 3-3).
ground_truth_requirements.label: FR

ground_truth_requirements.text: The requirement to provide for support, installation, and operation of outside instruments brought by the observer has several implications for the system. Due to the specialized nature of visitor instrumentation it is unlikely that complete integration into the Gemini environment is either feasible or warranted. In this case a subset of the available functionality must be made available through a standardized interface. The Gemini Telescopes view all instruments as operating as servers, responding to commands from the upper levels of the system. Visitor instruments must be capable of operating in this mode to be adequately supported. It should be the goal of this interface that it be a subset of the existing instrumentation interface (rather than a separate system). At a minimum this interface should support: The requirement to provide for support, installation, and operation of outside instruments brought by the observer has several implications for the system. Due to the specialized nature of visitor instrumentation it is unlikely that complete integration into the Gemini environment is either feasible or warranted. In this case a subset of the available functionality must be made available through a standardized interface. The Gemini Telescopes view all instruments as operating as servers, responding to commands from the upper levels of the system. Visitor instruments must be capable of operating in this mode to be adequately supported. It should be the goal of this interface that it be a subset of the existing instrumentation interface (rather than a separate system). At a minimum this interface should support: acquisition of status information of instrument by other systems capability to enter preprogrammed observing sequences capability to offset the telescope position and focus
ground_truth_requirements.label: FR

ground_truth_requirements.text: The requirements to have instruments operated as single units imply that several user stations will be active at the same time on the Gemini 8m Telescopes. On these user stations, different kinds of users may be working at the same time with the Gemini 8m Telescopes software. Independently of the location of users at the telescope site, they shall be able to access (according to their privileges) any part of the whole setup with a simple logon and conﬁguration operation. In other words. any subsection of the whole Gemini 8m Telescopes system should be accessible and controllable from any single point (but of course with protection ensuring security and safety). A particularly simple case of multi-use of the system is multi-point monitoring. By this it is meant that, while some (active) user is in control of the Gemini 8m Telescopes, someone else can follow what they are doing by monitoring all the relevant data from telescope and instruments. This will typically be needed by the operation supervisor. All other users wishing to monitor Gemini 8m Telescopes operations have to go through the procedures set up by Operations and get permission to do so. The multi-point monitoring mode might also be important when certain difﬁcult or rare problems occur, when expert advice is needed and can only be obtained from colleagues situated remotely. Multi-point monitoring allows them to follow directly the results of tests performed and investigate how the system is working (e.g. by selecting different display pages with the up-to-date status information on different parts of the system). Multi-point monitoring also allows a local observer to be monitored and advised by a remote supervisor. Monitoring shall exist both in the form of automatic displays of status information at different locations, and in the form of explicit access to the required status information from any point. Monitoring shall not affect the performance of ongoing observations.
