# Business Requirements Document (BRD)

## Project: THEMAS — Thermostat Environmental Management and Automation System

**Document ID:** THEMAS-BRD-001
**Project ID:** 1998-themas
**Prepared by:** Business Analysis Team
**Status:** Approved for Requirements Elicitation
**Version:** 1.0

---

## 1. PROBLEM STATEMENT

The organization occupies a multi-story office building comprising three floors, each subdivided into four distinct quadrants, yielding twelve independently climate-controlled zones. Currently, the heating and cooling units serving these zones are managed manually or through disconnected, zone-specific controls. This arrangement creates several measurable operational problems:

- **Inefficient energy use:** Without centralized coordination, multiple heating and cooling units may run simultaneously without constraint, driving up energy costs and placing unnecessary load on building infrastructure. There is no mechanism to cap the number of concurrently operating units.
- **Lack of supervisory visibility:** Building management has no single point of control or observation for all twelve climate zones. Temperature anomalies — including sensor failures producing out-of-range readings and zones that have drifted significantly from their set points — go undetected until occupants report discomfort.
- **No audit trail:** There is no systematic record of when heating or cooling units were activated, how long they ran, how often requests were denied due to capacity constraints, or how frequently temperature alarms were triggered. This makes it impossible to identify chronic problem zones, justify equipment maintenance, or produce energy-usage reports.
- **Slow response to temperature excursions:** Without automated monitoring, a zone whose temperature deviates substantially from its set point may remain in that condition for an extended period, affecting occupant comfort and potentially damaging sensitive equipment.
- **No standardized alarm mechanism:** Supervisors currently have no reliable, immediate notification when a thermostat reports an implausible temperature value or when a zone's temperature has exceeded acceptable deviation limits.

The THEMAS system is intended to address all of these gaps by providing centralized, automated monitoring and control of all twelve climate zones, with supervisor oversight, configurable operating parameters, and a full event history for operational and statistical reporting.

---

## 2. BUSINESS OBJECTIVES & SUCCESS CRITERIA

### Business Objectives

| ID | Objective |
|----|-----------|
| BO-1 | Centralize control of all heating and cooling units across all twelve building zones under a single software system. |
| BO-2 | Enforce a configurable cap on the number of simultaneously operating heating/cooling units to protect building infrastructure and manage energy consumption. |
| BO-3 | Automate the detection of temperature excursions and invalid sensor readings, and alert the supervisor immediately. |
| BO-4 | Provide the supervisor with the ability to remotely adjust temperature settings for any zone without physical access to the thermostat. |
| BO-5 | Maintain a complete, timestamped event log that supports both operational history review and monthly statistical analysis. |
| BO-6 | Establish a known, safe system state at startup by ensuring all units are in a defined condition before normal operation begins. |

### Success Criteria / KPIs

| KPI | Target |
|-----|--------|
| System initialization time | All units turned off and thermostat states evaluated before normal operation commences; no manual intervention required. |
| Temperature deviation detection | The system shall detect and alarm on any zone temperature that deviates from its set point by more than 3°F. |
| Invalid temperature detection | 100% of thermostat readings outside the configured valid range shall be flagged and alarmed without exception. |
| Concurrent unit cap enforcement | The system shall never permit more than the configured maximum number of heating/cooling units to run simultaneously. |
| Alarm responsiveness | Alarm tones shall begin immediately upon detection of an invalid temperature or overtemperature condition and shall continue without interruption until manually reset by the supervisor. |
| Event recording completeness | Every state change, denied request, and alarm condition shall be recorded with a description and system timestamp; no events shall be silently discarded. |
| Reporting availability | The system shall be capable of generating an operational history report covering the past twelve months and a statistical summary for any individual month on demand. |
| Platform availability | The system shall operate continuously on the designated Windows NT platform without requiring a separate operating environment. |

---

## 3. STAKEHOLDERS

| Stakeholder | Role | Primary Need |
|-------------|------|--------------|
| **Building Supervisor** | Primary end user and system operator | A single interface from which to monitor all twelve climate zones, adjust temperature settings, respond to alarms, and generate reports. The supervisor is the only human actor who interacts with the running system. |
| **Facilities / Building Management** | Business owner | Assurance that energy use is controlled (concurrent unit cap), that the building environment is maintained within acceptable parameters, and that historical data is available for maintenance planning and cost analysis. |
| **THEMAS Project Manager (Barry Scott)** | Project sponsor and document authority | Delivery of a system that meets the Statement of Work; resolution of any conflicts between reference documents. |
| **Software Development Team** | System implementer | A complete, unambiguous specification of functional behavior, initialization logic, alarm rules, control logic, and reporting requirements from which to design and build the software. |
| **Verification & Validation Team** | Quality assurance | A simulated thermostat prototype that allows THEMAS reliability to be tested without requiring live hardware. |
| **System Integrator (TBD)** | Hardware/software interface owner | Defined interface points between THEMAS software and the physical thermostats and heating/cooling units, even where the exact interface specification is not yet finalized. |

---

## 4. CURRENT STATE vs FUTURE STATE

### Current State

- Climate control across the twelve building zones is managed independently, with no centralized software oversight.
- There is no automated mechanism to prevent too many heating or cooling units from running at the same time.
- Temperature set points can only be changed by physically interacting with individual thermostats in each zone.
- There is no automated alarm when a thermostat reports an implausible reading or when a zone temperature drifts beyond an acceptable band.
- No event log exists; there is no way to produce a historical report of unit activity, alarm frequency, or request denial rates.
- Building management has no data to support decisions about equipment maintenance, zone-by-zone energy consumption, or recurring problem areas.
- At system startup (e.g., after a power event), the state of heating and cooling units is unknown, creating a risk of uncontrolled simultaneous operation.

### Future State

- A single THEMAS software application running on a Windows NT workstation will monitor all twelve thermostats continuously and control all twelve corresponding heating/cooling units automatically.
- The system will enforce a configurable maximum number of concurrently running units; requests that cannot be immediately honored will be queued and processed as capacity becomes available.
- The supervisor will be able to change the temperature setting of any thermostat from a single interface window, selecting from a validated list of temperatures.
- Two distinct alarm conditions — invalid temperature readings and overtemperature excursions — will trigger immediate, distinguishable audible alerts on the supervisor's computer, persisting until manually acknowledged.
- Every significant system event (unit status changes, denied requests, alarms) will be recorded with a timestamp in a Microsoft Access database on the supervisor's computer.
- The supervisor will be able to generate, on demand, either a full twelve-month operational history report or a statistical summary for a selected month, both delivered as named ASCII files.
- At every startup, the system will establish a known safe state by turning off all units before re-evaluating thermostat conditions.

### Gap Summary

| Dimension | Current State | Future State |
|-----------|--------------|--------------|
| Control scope | Per-zone, manual | Centralized, automated, all 12 zones |
| Concurrent unit management | None | Configurable cap with LIFO queue |
| Alarm notification | None | Audible, frequency-coded, supervisor-reset |
| Temperature set point control | Physical thermostat only | Remote via supervisor interface |
| Event logging | None | Timestamped database on supervisor PC |
| Reporting | None | Operational history + monthly statistics |
| Startup safety | Unknown state | Defined safe state enforced at initialization |

---

## 5. BUSINESS RULES

### BR-1: Temperature Validity
All temperature values received from thermostats — whether current readings or set point changes — must fall within a pre-configured valid temperature range. Any value strictly below the lower bound or strictly above the upper bound of this range shall be treated as invalid and shall not be used for control decisions. An invalid temperature shall immediately trigger an alarm.

### BR-2: Overtemperature Detection
The system shall define an overtemperature deviation (OD) value. For any thermostat with a set point (TSET), the acceptable operating band is defined as [TSET − OD, TSET + OD]. A current temperature reading that falls outside this band (i.e., is strictly less than TSET − OD or strictly greater than TSET + OD) constitutes an overtemperature condition and shall trigger an alarm. Temperatures within the band shall be passed for further processing.

### BR-3: Heating/Cooling Trigger Logic
The system shall define a trigger deviation (TD) value. For any thermostat, the trigger band is [TSET − TD, TSET + TD]. If the current temperature falls within this band, both the heating and cooling units for that zone shall be turned off. If the current temperature falls below the lower trigger value (but within the overtemperature band), the heating unit shall be activated. If the current temperature rises above the upper trigger value (but within the overtemperature band), the cooling unit shall be activated.

### BR-4: Concurrent Unit Cap
A configurable maximum number of heating and cooling units may operate simultaneously across the entire building. When a request to turn on a unit would exceed this maximum, the request shall not be discarded; it shall be placed in a Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) queue. When any running unit is turned off, the system shall inspect the queue, retrieve the most recently queued request, verify that the requesting thermostat still requires the unit, and activate it if so.

### BR-5: Denied Request Recording
Any time a request to turn on a heating or cooling unit cannot be immediately honored due to the concurrent unit cap, the system shall record a denial event. This event shall capture the event type, the identity of the requesting thermostat, and the identity of the unit that was not turned on.

### BR-6: Alarm Differentiation
Two alarm types are defined and must be distinguishable by the supervisor:
- **Invalid Temperature Alarm:** Alternating 500 Hz and 700 Hz tones, each of 0.75-second duration, continuous until manually reset.
- **Overtemperature Alarm:** Alternating 1000 Hz and 1500 Hz tones, each of 0.5-second duration, continuous until manually reset.
Both alarm types shall be recorded as events in the event database.

### BR-7: Alarm Reset
Neither alarm type shall cease automatically. The supervisor must manually reset each alarm through the supervisor interface window. The system shall not suppress or auto-clear alarms.

### BR-8: Initialization Safe State
Upon every system startup, THEMAS shall issue turn-off commands to all heating and cooling units before reading any thermostat data. Only after all units are in a known off state shall the system evaluate thermostat conditions and selectively re-activate units as required by current temperature readings and set points.

### BR-9: Event Logging
Every event — including unit status changes, denied requests, invalid temperature detections, overtemperature conditions, and alarms — shall be recorded with a textual description and the current system timestamp. Events shall be stored in a Microsoft Access database residing on the supervisor's computer.

### BR-10: Supervisor Temperature Setting Authority
Only the supervisor, acting through the THEMAS supervisor interface, may remotely change a thermostat's temperature set point. The interface shall present only valid temperature values for selection, preventing the entry of out-of-range set points.

### BR-11: Configuration via Initialization File
All configurable operating parameters — thermostat identifiers, heating/cooling unit definitions, the concurrent unit maximum, trigger deviation values, overtemperature deviation values, and valid temperature range bounds — shall be read from an initialization file at startup. These values shall not be hard-coded in the software.

### BR-12: Report Content Rules
- The **operational history report** shall include all recorded events, sorted chronologically by date and time, covering the past twelve months.
- The **statistical summary report** shall be generated for a single selected month and shall include: (a) the percentage of time each heating and cooling unit was active for each day of that month, and (b) for each thermostat, the ratio of granted to denied unit activation requests.

---

## 6. SCOPE

### In Scope

- Software application to run on a Windows NT workstation serving as the supervisor's computer.
- Reading and processing of an initialization file to configure all system parameters at startup.
- Continuous monitoring of temperature readings and set point data from all thermostats in the twelve building zones (three floors × four quadrants).
- Automated control logic to activate and deactivate heating and cooling units based on trigger and overtemperature rules.
- Enforcement of the configurable concurrent unit maximum, including LIFO queuing of deferred requests.
- Two-type alarm system with audible, frequency-coded tones on the supervisor's computer.
- Supervisor interface window providing: zone status display, remote temperature set point adjustment, alarm reset capability, and report generation.
- Event logging to a Microsoft Access database on the supervisor's computer.
- Generation of operational history reports (twelve-month, chronological, ASCII file) and monthly statistical summary reports (ASCII file, user-selectable name and location).
- A simulated thermostat prototype for verification and validation purposes.

### Out of Scope

- Physical installation or specification of thermostats, heating units, or cooling units.
- Definition or implementation of the hardware interface between THEMAS software and the physical thermostats and heating/cooling units (designated TBD; to be addressed in a subsequent specification).
- Network infrastructure, cabling, or building automation hardware.
- User authentication, access control, or multi-user support (the supervisor is the sole operator).
- Integration with any building management system, energy management platform, or enterprise system beyond what is described herein.
- Detailed specification of systems that interface with THEMAS (these are acknowledged but not defined in this document).
- Mobile or remote access to the THEMAS interface from outside the supervisor's workstation.

---

## 7. CONSTRAINTS

### Technical Constraints

| ID | Constraint |
|----|-----------|
| TC-1 | The THEMAS software shall run exclusively on the Microsoft Windows NT operating system. All internal process communications and external device interfaces must be compatible with this platform. |
| TC-2 | Event data shall be stored in a Microsoft Access database. No alternative database platform is permitted for this purpose. |
| TC-3 | All reports shall be generated as ASCII text files. The supervisor shall be able to specify the file name and storage location at the time of report generation. |
| TC-4 | The interface between THEMAS and the physical heating/cooling units is not yet defined. The portion of the software responsible for issuing control signals to these units shall be designed with a TBD placeholder, to be completed when the interface specification is available. |
| TC-5 | Thermostats provide only two data values to THEMAS: current temperature and temperature set point. No other data channel from thermostats is available. |
| TC-6 | Heating and cooling units provide no feedback signal to THEMAS. The system cannot confirm whether a command sent to a unit was executed by that unit. |

### Operational Constraints

| ID | Constraint |
|----|-----------|
| OC-1 | The building has exactly three floors, each with exactly four quadrants, yielding exactly twelve thermostat/unit pairs. The system design must accommodate this fixed topology as read from the initialization file. |
| OC-2 | The maximum number of concurrently operating units is a configurable parameter, not a fixed value. It must be read from the initialization file and must be enforceable at runtime. |
| OC-3 | Alarms shall not self-reset. Supervisor manual intervention is required to clear any alarm condition. |
| OC-4 | The overtemperature deviation is fixed at a maximum of 3°F. The system shall not permit a reported temperature to exceed the set point by more than this value without triggering an alarm. |

### Regulatory / Standards Constraints

| ID | Constraint |
|----|-----------|
| RC-1 | The software requirements specification for THEMAS shall conform to ANSI/IEEE STD 830-1984 (Guide to Software Requirements Specifications). |
| RC-2 | In the event of conflict between this BRD, the SRS, and other referenced documents, the Statement of Work (THEMAS_SOW_001) takes precedence over all other documents. The SRS takes precedence over all other documents except the Statement of Work. |

---

## 8. ASSUMPTIONS & DEPENDENCIES

### Assumptions

| ID | Assumption |
|----|-----------|
| A-1 | A Windows NT workstation designated as the supervisor's computer will be procured, configured, and available before THEMAS software deployment. |
| A-2 | Microsoft Access will be installed and licensed on the supervisor's computer prior to system deployment. |
| A-3 | The initialization file will be correctly populated with valid thermostat identifiers, unit definitions, and all required parameter values before the system is started for the first time. The software is not responsible for creating or validating the initialization file itself. |
| A-4 | Each thermostat in the building has a unique identifier that can be used to distinguish it within the THEMAS system. No two thermostats share an identifier. |
| A-5 | The heating and cooling units are capable of being turned on and off in response to signals from the THEMAS system, even though they cannot confirm receipt of those signals. |
| A-6 | There is no real-time delay in the transmission of status information from heating/cooling units to the THEMAS system (i.e., communication latency is negligible for control purposes). |
| A-7 | The supervisor is a single, identified individual who has exclusive access to the supervisor interface. Multi-user or role-based access control is not required. |
| A-8 | The system clock on the supervisor's Windows NT workstation is accurate and will be used as the authoritative time source for all event timestamps. |
| A-9 | The physical thermostat hardware is capable of accepting remote set point changes issued by the THEMAS system through the (TBD) interface. |
| A-10 | The simulated thermostat prototype required for verification and validation will be developed as part of this project and will faithfully replicate the data outputs of the physical thermostats. |

### Dependencies

| ID | Dependency |
|----|-----------|
| D-1 | **Hardware Interface Specification:** The control signal interface between THEMAS and the physical heating/cooling units must be defined and delivered before the corresponding software module can be completed. This is currently designated TBD and represents a critical path dependency. |
| D-2 | **Thermostat Communication Interface:** The protocol and interface by which THEMAS reads temperature values and set points from physical thermostats must be specified and available to the development team. |
| D-3 | **Statement of Work (THEMAS_SOW_001):** The SOW is the governing contract document. Any ambiguity in this BRD or the resulting SRS must be resolved by reference to the SOW. |
| D-4 | **Initialization File Format Agreement:** The format and schema of the initialization file must be agreed upon between the project manager and the development team before initialization module development begins. |
| D-5 | **Windows NT Platform Availability:** The target operating environment must be available for development and testing. Any change to the target operating system would constitute a scope change requiring re-evaluation of all platform-dependent requirements. |

---

*This document is intended to provide sufficient business context and rule definition to support the elicitation and specification of detailed software requirements for the THEMAS system. Questions regarding this document should be directed to Barry Scott, THEMAS Project Manager.*

ground_truth_requirements.text: Two types of temperature data shall be recognized from the thermostats: 1) the temperature setting and 2) the current temperature. This module shall process both types of data. A current temperature value that is received from an individual thermostat shall be compared to the valid temperature range values. If the current temperature value is strictly less than the lower value of the valid temperature range or if the received temperature value is strictly greater than the upper value of the valid temperature range, then the THEMAS system shall identify the current temperature value as an invalid temperature and shall output an invalid temperature status. Otherwise, the THEMAS system shall output a valid temperature status. A temperature setting value that is received from an individual thermostat shall be compared to the valid temperature range values. If the temperature setting value is strictly less than the lower value of the valid temperature range or if the temperature setting value is strictly greater than the upper value of the valid temperature range, then the THEMAS system shall identify the temperature setting as an invalid temperature and shall output an invalid temperature status. Otherwise, the THEMAS system shall realize the value for that thermostat’s temperature setting. # Business Requirements Document (BRD)

## Project: Defense Information Infrastructure (DII) Common Operating Environment (COE) — XML Services (XS) and XML Registry

**Project ID:** 1999 - dii
**Document Status:** Pre-Requirements Baseline
**Classification:** For Official Use Only (FOUO)
**Prepared By:** Business Analysis Division
**Intended Audience:** Requirements Engineers, System Architects, Program Managers, COE Sponsors

---

## 1. PROBLEM STATEMENT

The Department of Defense (DoD) operates a vast, heterogeneous information infrastructure spanning fixed installations (bases, data processing megacenters) and deployed units operating in dynamic, often degraded communications environments. Across this enterprise, data is exchanged among dozens of mission applications, database platforms, and organizational communities of interest — yet there is no standardized, authoritative mechanism for defining, registering, discovering, or reusing the XML tags and schemas that govern how that data is structured and interpreted.

This absence creates several measurable and operationally significant problems:

**Semantic Fragmentation:** Different commands, programs, and applications independently define XML tags for the same real-world concepts (e.g., country codes, unit identifiers, geographic coordinates), resulting in incompatible data representations that impede automated data exchange and require costly manual reconciliation. Without a shared registry, the same term may carry different meanings across systems, directly degrading the quality of intelligence, logistics, and command-and-control data.

**Lack of Reuse and Proliferation of Tags:** In the absence of a governed registry, developers create new XML tags rather than discovering and reusing existing, approved ones. This proliferation increases integration costs, introduces inconsistency, and undermines the DoD's data standardization objectives as expressed in the Joint Technical Architecture (JTA) and the Interoperability and Requirements Technical Standards (I&RTS).

**Operational Inflexibility:** Current data management services cannot be dynamically tuned to accommodate the wide range of operational environments — from peacetime garrison operations to full-scale wartime deployments. Session management, database connectivity, and workload parameters are not adjustable in real time, creating brittleness when communications degrade or mission scope changes rapidly.

**Absence of Lifecycle Governance:** There is no formal workflow for submitting, reviewing, approving, versioning, or retiring XML information resources. Producers of metadata have no standardized mechanism to notify consumers of changes, and consumers have no reliable way to subscribe to updates or verify that the tags they are using reflect current authoritative definitions.

**COE Non-Compliance Risk:** Without a portable, COE-compliant XML services module and registry, individual programs are building bespoke solutions that cannot be certified under the DII COE framework, creating redundant investment and interoperability gaps across the joint force.

The cumulative effect is that data exchanged across the DoD enterprise — particularly in time-critical operational contexts — cannot be trusted to mean the same thing to all recipients, and the infrastructure supporting that exchange cannot adapt to the full spectrum of military operations.

---

## 2. BUSINESS OBJECTIVES & SUCCESS CRITERIA

The system must achieve the following objectives, each paired with measurable success criteria where derivable from the operational context:

### Objective 1: Establish an Authoritative XML Registry
Provide a single, accessible, COE-compliant repository where XML tags, schemas, Document Type Definitions (DTDs), and associated metadata are submitted, governed, versioned, and published for reuse across the DoD enterprise.

**Success Criteria:**
- 100% of submitted information resources are processed through a defined approval workflow (submit → review → approve/reject → publish).
- All registered tags are discoverable via at minimum three search modalities (hierarchical/tree view, tabular view, author/subscriber search).
- Zero approved tags exist in the registry without a complete Information Resource Submittal Package (XML Tag Specification, XML Spec, and sample XML document).

### Objective 2: Enable Semantic Interoperability Across Communities of Interest
Ensure that every XML tag registered in the system carries sufficient metadata — including data type, ancestor/descendant relationships, versioning history, and reference sets — to allow any consuming application to interpret the tag correctly without out-of-band communication.

**Success Criteria:**
- Each registered tag must expose, at minimum: data type, parent/child relationships, version history, domain value reference sets, and links to amplifying resources (e.g., ERwin models, DTDs).
- Consumers can extract semantically valid alternate views of XML documents (e.g., via XSL transformations) without requiring access to the originating application.
- All registered schemas are validated as well-formed and conformant prior to publication.

### Objective 3: Support the Full Spectrum of Military Operational Environments
The XML Services (XS) module must function correctly and be tunable across fixed garrison environments, deployed unit environments, crisis planning scenarios, and degraded communications conditions.

**Success Criteria:**
- Session time-out values are user-definable and resettable prior to session initialization in all operational modes.
- Database connect descriptor names (including protocol, host, port, and instance name) are redefinable by authorized on-site personnel without system restart.
- Data management services in fixed environments support on-site tuning of workload parameters and database sizing without vendor intervention.
- The system remains operational and accessible in degraded communications environments, with session parameters adjustable in response to network performance data.

### Objective 4: Provide Governed Producer and Consumer Workflows
Formalize the roles of metadata producers and consumers, providing each with the tools, interfaces, and notifications necessary to contribute to and benefit from the registry in a controlled, auditable manner.

**Success Criteria:**
- Producers can submit metadata both online (interactive) and offline (batch) without loss of data integrity.
- All submissions are parsed, validated against registry schema rules, and either accepted into the database or returned with rejection annotations.
- Consumers receive automated notifications of changes to subscribed Communities of Interest (COI) or individual information resources within one business cycle of the change being approved.

### Objective 5: Achieve and Maintain COE Compliance
All software components — the XS module and the XML Registry — must be certified as compliant with DII COE standards and portable across COE-compliant platforms.

**Success Criteria:**
- Software delivery conforms to DII COE packaging and delivery guidelines.
- All SRS-level requirements pass formal qualification through test, demonstration, analysis, and/or inspection as appropriate.
- Registry software executes without modification on all COE-compliant hardware/OS platforms designated in the program baseline.

---

## 3. STAKEHOLDERS

### 3.1 COE Sponsors / Program Managers
**Role:** Organizational authorities responsible for ensuring that systems built under the COE framework adhere to compliance levels described in the I&RTS.
**Primary Need:** Assurance that the XS module and XML Registry meet COE compliance requirements, are deliverable under DII COE guidelines, and reduce redundant investment across programs. COE sponsors must be able to enforce naming conventions and metadata standards across subordinate programs.

### 3.2 XML Metadata Producers
**Role:** Agents (individuals or organizational units) who contribute XML tag definitions, schemas, DTDs, and associated metadata to the registry for enterprise reuse.
**Primary Need:** A streamlined, standardized submission process — both online and offline — that allows them to package, submit, and track the status of information resources. Producers need to be notified when their submissions are approved, rejected, or when downstream changes affect tags they have authored.

### 3.3 XML Metadata Consumers / Data Consumers
**Role:** Application developers, data analysts, and system integrators who search the registry to discover and reuse existing XML tags and schemas rather than creating new ones.
**Primary Need:** Powerful discovery capabilities — search by tag name, author, hierarchy, data type, and relationship — along with the ability to download complete information resource packages and subscribe to change notifications. Consumers need confidence that tags they select are semantically unambiguous and authoritative.

### 3.4 Data and Database Managers
**Role:** Personnel responsible for the administration, tuning, and maintenance of the underlying database infrastructure supporting the XS module and registry.
**Primary Need:** Administrative tools to tune data management services for varying workloads, manage storage and memory structures, control user permissions and privileges, and perform maintenance operations (shutdown, mount/unmount, online/offline transitions) appropriate to both fixed and deployed environments.

### 3.5 Network / Information Infrastructure Resource Managers
**Role:** Personnel responsible for managing the communications and network infrastructure over which the XS module and registry operate.
**Primary Need:** Visibility into how data management services interact with network conditions, particularly the ability to adjust session parameters in response to communications degradation, and integration with network management capabilities.

### 3.6 Registry Managers / Superusers
**Role:** Authorized administrators who govern the registry itself — creating and managing user accounts, establishing naming conventions, reviewing and approving submissions, monitoring data model changes, and managing federation agreements with other registries.
**Primary Need:** Comprehensive administrative controls over the registry lifecycle, including the ability to approve or reject submissions, forward requests to federated registries, define metadata tag sets, and enforce DoD data standards compliance.

### 3.7 Mission Application Developers / System Integrators
**Role:** Technical personnel embedding the XS module within larger mission systems (e.g., command-and-control, logistics, intelligence applications).
**Primary Need:** Standard, well-documented APIs for both the XS function and the Registry, transparent internal interfaces to existing DBIF, DAC, and COTS RDBMS components, and clear guidance on COE-compliant integration patterns.

### 3.8 Training Personnel
**Role:** Instructors and trainees who use the data management services in a training context.
**Primary Need:** A training mode that replicates production processing fidelity without exposing or corrupting live operational data, accessible via training application interfaces rather than production mission applications.

### 3.9 Auditors / Qualification Testers
**Role:** Personnel responsible for formally qualifying the software against SRS-level requirements using test, demonstration, analysis, and inspection methods.
**Primary Need:** Traceability from requirements to qualification evidence, and a system that supports observable, measurable validation of all functional capabilities.

---

## 4. CURRENT STATE vs. FUTURE STATE

### 4.1 Current State Limitations

| Domain | Current State Pain Point |
|---|---|
| XML Tag Governance | No centralized registry exists. Tags are defined ad hoc by individual programs, leading to semantic inconsistency and duplication across the enterprise. |
| Metadata Submission | No standardized submission process. Producers use informal, program-specific mechanisms with no approval workflow, versioning, or change notification. |
| Tag Discovery | Consumers have no enterprise-wide search capability. Discovering whether a tag already exists requires manual outreach to other programs or communities. |
| Operational Adaptability | Data management services are statically configured. Session parameters, connect descriptors, and workload tuning require vendor or specialist intervention and cannot be adjusted in real time during operations. |
| COE Integration | XML services are not modularized as COE-compliant segments. Programs build bespoke solutions that cannot be certified or reused across the COE framework. |
| Schema Validation | No automated tooling exists to validate XML documents for well-formedness, conformance, or semantic correctness against enterprise standards. |
| Federation | No mechanism exists to forward registry queries or submissions to peer registries under explicit federation agreements. |
| Access Control | User permissions and privileges for data management services are managed inconsistently across platforms and sites. |

### 4.2 Future State Capabilities

The new system will establish:

- **A governed XML Registry** serving as the single authoritative source for DoD XML tags, schemas, and metadata, with formal producer/consumer workflows, versioning, and change notification.
- **An XML Services (XS) module** embedded within COE-compliant mission systems, providing data management services tunable to the full spectrum of operational environments — from fixed megacenters to forward-deployed units operating under degraded communications.
- **Enterprise-wide tag discovery** through multiple search modalities, hierarchical browsing, relationship visualization, and downloadable information resource packages.
- **Automated schema validation and conformance checking**, ensuring that all registered and consumed XML artifacts are well-formed, valid, and semantically consistent with DoD data standards.
- **Standard APIs** for both the Registry and XS function, enabling consistent integration across mission systems without bespoke development.
- **Dynamic operational adaptability**, including user-definable session parameters, redefinable connect descriptors, and workload tuning capabilities appropriate to each operational mode.
- **Federated registry capability**, allowing the system to forward requests to peer registries under explicit agreements, extending discovery across organizational boundaries.

---

## 5. BUSINESS RULES

### 5.1 Tag Submission and Governance

**BR-01:** Every XML tag submitted to the registry must be accompanied by a complete Information Resource Submittal Package comprising, at minimum: an XML Tag Specification form, an XML Specification artifact (DTD, DCD, or equivalent), and a sample XML document illustrating the tag's use. Incomplete packages shall not be accepted into the review workflow.

**BR-02:** A submitted tag shall not be published to the registry for consumer use until it has been reviewed and explicitly approved by an authorized Registry Manager. Rejected submissions must be annotated with the reason for rejection and returned to the submitting producer.

**BR-03:** All registered tags and information resources must conform to the DoD data standards naming convention. Naming structures must express context and relationship within the COE architecture as specified in the I&RTS. Naming conventions must not conflict with established DoD data standards.

**BR-04:** XML attributes shall be used sparingly. Where a term is well-recognized and meaningful outside the context of its container element, it shall be designated as an XML element rather than an attribute, to avoid carrying unnecessary attribute baggage and to limit tag proliferation.

**BR-05:** Producers may modify the terms, definitions, and status of metadata for information resources they own. Modifications to approved, published resources must re-enter the approval workflow and trigger change notifications to all subscribers.

**BR-06:** A producer who plans changes to a registered information resource must post those planned changes to the registry in advance of implementation, providing consumers adequate notice.

### 5.2 Access Control

**BR-07:** Access to registry and data management functions shall be governed by Discretionary Access Controls (DAC), where subjects with appropriate permissions may delegate access to other subjects, subject to the constraints of Mandatory Access Controls (MAC).

**BR-08:** MAC shall be enforced above any DAC implemented by users. Access to objects shall be mediated based on the clearance level of the subject and the sensitivity label of the object, and MAC controls shall not be overridden by user-level discretionary settings.

**BR-09:** User permissions and privileges — including superuser designations — shall be managed exclusively by authorized Registry Managers. No user may self-elevate privileges.

### 5.3 Operational Mode Rules

**BR-10:** In fixed (static) operational environments, on-site personnel shall have the capability to tune data management service parameters — including workload configurations and database sizing — without requiring vendor intervention. These parameters are expected to change more frequently and to a greater extent than in deployed environments.

**BR-11:** In deployed (dynamic) operational environments, the scope of administrative capabilities available to on-site personnel may be reduced relative to fixed environments, reflecting the more predetermined nature of deployed mission workloads.

**BR-12:** Session time-out values shall be user-definable and must be settable prior to the initialization of any user session. The system shall support dynamic adjustment of session time-out values in response to communications performance data provided by network management or DBMS capabilities.

**BR-13:** Database connect descriptors — including fully qualified object names, protocol, host, port, and instance name — shall be redefinable by authorized personnel without requiring system reinitialization.

**BR-14:** Training mode operations shall use training application interfaces to the DBMS rather than production mission application interfaces. Training processing shall replicate production fidelity but shall not access, modify, or expose live operational data.

### 5.4 Data Integrity and Transaction Management

**BR-15:** Individual transactions shall be processed (committed) or discarded (rolled back) by the proponent maintainer of the data items involved. No transaction shall be partially committed.

**BR-16:** The proponent scheme — defining which sites hold replicated databases and who holds update authority at each site — shall be explicitly defined and enforced. Update authority is assigned at the source and record level.

**BR-17:** Transaction journaling shall be maintained for all database transactions, supporting audit, recovery, and replication operations.

### 5.5 Federation and Interoperability

**BR-18:** Registry requests that fall outside the scope of the local registry shall be forwarded to peer registries only under explicit, pre-established federation agreements. No ad hoc forwarding is permitted.

**BR-19:** The system shall process ANSI standard SQL as specified in FIPS PUB 127-2 for all relational data access operations.

**BR-20:** XML Services segments shall be modular and independently deployable as COE-compliant segments. COE sponsors shall adhere to the compliance levels described in the I&RTS.

---

## 6. SCOPE

### 6.1 In Scope

**XML Registry:**
- Design, development, and deployment of a web-accessible XML Registry supporting producer submission workflows (online interactive and offline batch), consumer discovery and download capabilities, and Registry Manager administrative functions.
- Information Resource lifecycle management: submission, parsing, validation, approval/rejection, publication, versioning, change notification, and retirement.
- Support for multiple information resource types including XML Tag Specifications, DTDs/DCDs, sample XML documents, ERwin models, and reference sets.
- Tag relationship management: ancestor/descendant, parent/child (uses/used-by), versioning, data type, and reference set associations.
- Subscription and notification services for Communities of Interest and individual information resource changes.
- Federation capability to forward requests to peer registries under explicit agreements.
- User and superuser account management, naming convention enforcement, and metadata governance.
- Standard APIs for registry access as specified in Appendix B.

**XML Services (XS) Module:**
- Development of a COE-compliant XS module providing data management services across all four operational modes: Environmental (real-time operations), End User, Maintenance, and Training.
- Dynamic session management including user-definable session time-out values and connect descriptor redefinition.
- Workload and database tuning capabilities appropriate to fixed and deployed operational environments.
- Internal interfaces to DBIF, DAC, and COTS RDBMS products (transparent to operators).
- Standard APIs for XS function access as specified in Appendix C.
- XML document structure services: schema design, tag validation, well-formedness checking, conformance validation, DTD generation, and version management.
- XML content services: tag creation and reuse, semantic structure recording in JTA-compliant formalism, XSL view generation, document disassembly, and cross-referencing/indexing.
- XML consumption services: DOM and SAX parsing, conformance checking, semantic view extraction, and hierarchical-to-relational disassembly.
- Support for heterogeneous data types and sources across COE-compliant platforms.

**Qualification and Delivery:**
- Formal qualification of all SRS-level requirements through test, demonstration, analysis, and inspection methods.
- Software delivery in accordance with DII COE guidelines.

### 6.2 Out of Scope

- **Personnel requirements:** Staffing levels and personnel qualifications for systems in which the XS module is embedded are the responsibility of the embedding system's developers and are not defined by this project.
- **Training curriculum development:** Training program design and delivery for systems embedding the XS module are the responsibility of those systems' developers.
- **COTS RDBMS product development:** The project interfaces with existing COTS RDBMS products but does not modify or develop them.
- **Network management systems:** The XS module consumes network performance data from network management capabilities but does not develop or modify those systems.
- **Mission application development:** The XS module is a service component embedded within mission applications; the mission applications themselves are out of scope.
- **Hardware procurement:** COE-compliant hardware platforms are assumed to be procured separately.

---

## 7. CONSTRAINTS

### 7.1 Regulatory and Standards Compliance

**C-01:** All software components must comply with DII COE standards and guidelines, including the compliance levels specified in the I&RTS. COE sponsors are bound by these compliance levels.

**C-02:** SQL processing must conform to ANSI standard SQL as specified in FIPS PUB 127-2.

**C-03:** Naming conventions must not conflict with DoD data standards conventions and must express context and relationship within the COE architecture as specified in the I&RTS.

**C-04:** Semantic structures must be recorded in JTA-compliant formalism.

**C-05:** XML Services segments must be segmented in accordance with COE XML Services segmentation requirements.

### 7.2 Technical Constraints

**C-06:** The Registry software must be portable and executable on all COE-compliant platforms without platform-specific modification.

**C-07:** The XS module must interface internally with existing DBIF, DAC, and COTS RDBMS data elements. These interfaces must be transparent to operators — operators shall not be required to manage or configure these internal interfaces directly.

**C-08:** Standard APIs for the Registry (Appendix B) and XS function (Appendix C) are fixed interface contracts. The system must implement these APIs as specified; deviation requires formal change control.

**C-09:** The system must support heterogeneous data types and sources, including spatial data (geographic information with graphical attributes), relational data, and XML-structured data.

**C-10:** Remote data access must support ISO RDA (ISO 9579) standards for application-layer interoperability across OSI networks where applicable.

### 7.3 Operational Constraints

**C-11:** The system must operate across the full spectrum of military operational environments, from peacetime garrison to full-scale wartime operations, including degraded communications conditions. Operational continuity under degraded conditions is a hard requirement, not a desirable feature.

**C-12:** In deployed environments, the system must function with reduced administrative overhead, reflecting the operational reality that deployed units have limited IT support personnel and more constrained mission profiles.

**C-13:** The system must support mirrored databases and RAID storage implementations (including RAID 0, 1, 3, 4, 5, and 6) as supported by resident COTS RDBMS utilities and operating systems, for reliability and rapid failure recovery.

### 7.4 Security Constraints

**C-14:** The system must implement both Mandatory Access Controls (MAC) and Discretionary Access Controls (DAC), with MAC enforced above DAC at all times. This is a non-negotiable security architecture requirement consistent with DoD information assurance policy.

**C-15:** Location transparency must be maintained — the physical location of data must be transparent to applications and users, with no requirement for users to know the physical origin of data accessed through views or federated queries.

---

## 8. ASSUMPTIONS & DEPENDENCIES

### 8.1 Assumptions

**A-01:** COE-compliant hardware and operating system platforms are available at all designated sites (fixed and deployed) and are procured independently of this project. The Registry and XS software will be developed and tested against the COE platform baseline in effect at project initiation.

**A-02:** The I&RTS and JTA documents referenced throughout the requirements are current, authoritative, and available to the development team. Requirements derived from these standards are stable for the duration of the project baseline.

**A-03:** Appendix A (metadata and information resource specifications), Appendix B (Registry standard APIs), and Appendix C (XS function standard APIs) are finalized and under configuration control prior to detailed design. These appendices are treated as binding interface specifications.

**A-04:** COTS RDBMS products at designated sites expose the data elements required for DBIF and DAC internal interfaces. The project assumes these interfaces are documented and accessible; no COTS product modification is anticipated.

**A-05:** Communities of Interest (COI) and their associated data domains are sufficiently defined at project initiation to support the design of reference sets, subscription services, and federation agreements.

**A-06:** Personnel who will operate the Registry Manager and XS administrative functions possess baseline IT competency consistent with their roles. The system is not designed to compensate for untrained administrators.

**A-07:** The DoD data standards naming conventions referenced in the requirements are documented, current, and available to the development team for implementation of naming convention enforcement logic.

**A-08:** XML tag submissions will follow the element/attribute design guidance established in the requirements (i.e., well-recognized terms outside their container are designated as elements; XML attributes are used sparingly). This guidance will be enforced through style guide wizard tooling within the system, but submitters are assumed to have received orientation on this policy.

**A-09:** Federation agreements with peer registries, where applicable, are established at the organizational level prior to system deployment. The system provides the technical capability to forward requests; the legal and organizational agreements are a pre-condition, not a deliverable of this project.

**A-10:** The training mode environment is physically or logically separated from the production environment at the DBMS level, ensuring that training operations cannot affect production data integrity.

### 8.2 Dependencies

**D-01:** Delivery and certification of the XS module is dependent on the availability of COE-compliant platform environments for qualification testing. Delays in platform availability will directly impact the qualification schedule.

**D-02:** The Registry's change notification capability is dependent on the availability of a functioning email or FTP infrastructure at designated sites. The system will generate notifications; delivery depends on the underlying communications infrastructure.

**D-03:** Dynamic session time-out adjustment based on network performance is dependent on the availability of network management system data feeds or DBMS performance monitoring outputs. Where these feeds are unavailable, session time-out values will be manually configurable only.

**D-04:** The XS module's internal interfaces to DBIF and DAC are dependent on those components being deployed and operational at the target site. The XS module cannot function as a standalone component without these dependencies in place.

**D-05:** Formal qualification testing is dependent on the completion of SRS-level requirements documentation and the establishment of a qualification test environment that replicates the target operational configuration.

**D-06:** The offline batch submission capability for producers is dependent on the definition and publication of the Information Resource Submittal Package format and associated tools, which must be completed and distributed prior to producer onboarding.

**D-07:** Spatial DBMS capabilities (geographic data with graphical attributes, spatial query support) are dependent on the availability of a spatial DBMS component at sites requiring geographic data management. This component is assumed to be a COTS product integrated via the XS module's heterogeneous data management services.

---

*This document represents the pre-requirements business context and objectives for Project 1999-dii. It is intended to guide requirements elicitation and should be read in conjunction with the I&RTS, JTA, and the Appendices referenced herein. All sections are subject to revision as stakeholder engagement and requirements elicitation activities proceed.*

ground_truth_requirements.text: Environmental Mode: This is the real-time operations that must react to varying degrees of readiness to full scale wartime operations such as crisis planning with the use of heterogeneous data types and sources, transfer capabilities, data management services. COE Compliance. XML Services shall be segmented. COE sponsors shall adhere to compliance level requirements described in the I&RTS. In the fixed (static) mode of operation (base or data processing megacenter), the data management services shall have the capability of being tuned by on-site personnel to adjust for varying workloads and sizes of associated databases. These workloads and databases are expected to change more frequently and to a greater extent than for processing associated with deployed units In a changing (dynamic) environment, such as with deployed units, the workload and database sizes may be more predetermined (given a more precise mission) and require access to fewer data management administrative capabilities than needed in a fixed environment. The XS shall have the ability to redefine or reset names of connect descriptors to database server instances. Connect descriptors are fully qualified object names and include address (protocol/host/port) and instance name. In a degraded communications environment, there is a need, for example, to be able to reset session time-out values if the data management services are being accessed by users affected by the communications degradation. At a minimum, the session time-out values shall be user definable and be able to be reset prior to initialization of a user session. The goal is to provide the option of dynamically changing session time values based on current communications performance identified by capabilities of the network management or DBMS. End User Mode: Portion of XS services shall be used by various classes of users: data consumers, data and database managers, network information infrastructure resource managers. Some of these uses of the data management services will entail unique requirements that shall be fulfilled within the capability of XS services. Maintenance Mode: This mode includes modification and/or addition of application data segments, user permission, privileges, and restructuring storage and memory areas. In addition, maintenance also shall pertain to shutdown, open not-mounted and online/off-line implementations, modifications, upgrade, or other related actions. The data management services shall support managing various types of data, database architectures and platforms that includes hardware and software at the specified sites. Training Mode. In support of training activities, the data management services shall provide for the same processing as would be encountered in a production environment. However, access to the database may be via a training application access to the DBMS rather than from the production mission application.

Produce and display submittal forms as part of the Information Resource Submittal Package from the web containing the following Information Resource artifacts: XML Tag Specification, XML Spec (i.e. DTD, DCD etc.), Sample of XML document of the tag to be submitted. The Package is to be compressed and emailed or sent ftp to an addressee. Download Information Resource Submittal Package from the web containing forms, instructions, and tools for submission to XML Registry. Submit prescribed metadata related to information resource type, information resource association, status code, data types specified and other related information specified in Appendix A within a combination of forms. These forms are part of the Information Resource Submittal Package containing the following Information Resource artifacts: XML Tag Specification, XML Spec (i.e. DTD, DCD etc.), Sample of XML document of the tag to be submitted. The Package is to be compressed and emailed or sent ftp to an addressee. Submit metadata by an on-line interactive process Submit metadata by a off-line and interactive batch process Parse submitted XML Registry specification forms Populate XML Registry database Modify of specified terms & definitions of metadata and status of Information Resources. Associate Information Resources specified in Appendix A. Annotate rejected? Assemble registered information resources to form new components. Assemble new DTDs from current Produce DTD as an instantiation (others are database schema, message definition) for modeling environment. Notify change in Information Resources or authorized producer of tag. Provide a capability to post planned changes to a registry Approve and reject submissions Forward request to different registry Consumer services. A consumer examines a registry to select a tag structure for reuse in one or more applications that will exchange data according to a pre-defined agreement. Discovery View the XML Tags with the following relationships: Ancestor/ Descendant relationship: Provide the capability to view a Tag’s origin Uses/Used by relationship: Provide the capability to view a complex Container Tag’s parent/child relationship Data type information: Provide the capability to view a tag’s data type and related information. Versioning relationship: Provide the capability to view an Information resource’s versions Reference Sets: Provide the capability to view text related to the domai values or the related reference set Amplifying information: Provide the capability to view other information resources such as ERwin models, DTDs, documents, etc., which describe or otherwise provide amplifying information. View the XML Tags via a tree/hierarchy structure or tabular format. View the XML Tags by giving the user multiple search options to find a specific Tag. View the XML tags by giving the user the search option to find all tags of a given subscriber/author. Each Information Resource will have its own web page to allow the user to view all pertinent information, according to its information resource type. Display an XML Tag Specification form to the author of a given information resource. This XML Tag Spec will be used for inputting the requesting information for a specific Information Resource. Provide capabilities to download the XML Tags or other selected Information Resources. Catalog entities and attributes within servers to enable browsing, searching, retrieving of data related to XML sources. Extract semantically-valid views from an XML document (e.g., XSL) Disassemble hierarchical view to relational representation Render

ground_truth_requirements.text: COE Software will be qualified through formal validation tests of the SRS level requirements. The Qualification Methods applied to the software shall include test, demonstration, analysis, and inspection (T, D, A, I). COE Software will be qualified through formal validation tests of the SRS level requirements. The Qualification Methods applied to the software shall include test, demonstration, analysis, and inspection (T, D, A, I). TEST. A qualification method that is carried out by operation of the item/component/I/F (or some part of the computer S/W configuration item, etc.) and that relies on the collection and subsequent examination of data. DEMONSTRATIONS A qualification method that is carried out by operation of the item/component/I/F (or some part of the computer S/W configuration item, etc.), and that relies on observable functional operation not requiring the use of elaborate instrumentation or special test equipment. ANALYSIS A qualification method that is carried out by the processing of accumulated data. An example of accumulated data is the compilation of data obtained from other qualification methods. Examples of the processing of accumulated data are interpretations or extrapolations made from the data. INSPECTION A qualification method that is carried out by visual examination, physical manipulation, or measurement to verify that the requirements have been satisfied

ground_truth_requirements.text: Automatically: Indicates processing initiated during execution of other processes, but dependent on information and/or parameters to be generated or supplied to these other processes. The information / parameters may be data dependent, or application dependent, or dependent on a manual process/human intervention. It will include controls qualifying the processing involved. Business Rule: A narrative description of policies, procedures, or principles within an organization. Business rules can be divided in to four categories: definitions, facts, constraints, and derivations. Definitions are business rules that define entities and attributes. Facts are either links (relationships) between entities or associations between an entity and attributes Constraints are conditions about the data that must always be true. They are the integrity rules that protect the data in the eventual database. Derivations are business rules that materialize a new piece of information (often attribute values) from other pieces of information. For example, a mathematical derivation might specify that you can obtain a person's age by subtracting his or her birth date from the current date. Commit/Rollback: An individual transaction is processed (commit) or discarded (rollback) by the proponent maintainer of the data items involved. Discretionary Access Controls (DAC): A means of restricting access to objects based on the identity of subjects or groups to which they belong. The controls are discretionary in the sense that a subject with a certain access permission is capable of passing that permission on to any other subject. Dynamically Generated Processing: Indicates processing initiated during execution of other processes, but dependent upon information and/or parameters to be generated or supplied to these other processes. The information/parameters may be data dependent, or application dependent, or dependent on a manual process/human intervention. It will include controls qualifying the processing involved. Location Transparency: occurs when the physical location of data is transparent to the applications and users of the database system. For example, a view that joins table data from several databases provides location transparency because the user of the view does not need to know where the data originates from. Mandatory Access Control (MAC): Imediates access to an object based on the clearance level of the subject (user) and the sensitivity label of the object. (These controls are always enforced above any discretionary control implemented by users). Mirrored Databases: Replication and maintenance of a database on a transaction basis for the purpose of rapid error or failure recovery as supported by the resident COTS RDBMS own system utilities and operating system. Object: A passive entity that contains or receives information. Access to an object potentially implies access to the information it contains. Examples of objects are records, blocks, pages, segments, files, directories, directory trees, and programs, as well as bits, bytes, words, fields, processors, video displays, keyboards, clocks, printers, and network nodes. Proponent Scheme: Describes the sites at which databases are replicated and also who owns and has update authority with respect to the data at each site. It refers to proponency at the source and record level. Redundant Array of Inexpensive Disks (RAID): A RAID system appears as one very large, reliable disk to the CPU. The main reason for using RAID storage is its reliability. RAID has the same advantages as shadowing and striping at a lower cost. There are several types/levels of RAID implementations, including: RAID 0 (known as disk striping), RAID 1 (known as disk shadowing), RAID 3 (in which data is distributed in small increments across all data disks and adds a parity value to a separate disk for recovery if any disk fails, RAID 4 (in which data is distributed in large chunks across all data disks and also has a single parity disk. RAID 4 intended to overcome performance penalties of RAID 3 for small transfers. RAID 5 (in which parity over RAID 3 or RAID 4 implementations), and RAID 6 ( in which two parity disks in addition to data disks are used in an attempt to further improve performance). In a RAID 5 implementation, the data is stored as are check sums and other information about the contents of each disk in the array. If one disk is lost, the others can use the check sums and other stored information to recreate the lost data. Storage system vendors may provide additional enhancements to RAID level implementations to improve performance and reliability. Remote Data Access (RDA): is an ISO (9579) application layer interoperability standard (protocol and formats) to support access by an application to a (remote) DBMSs over an OSI network. The goal of RDA is to allow interoperability between applications (clients) and databases (servers) of different manufacturers so that an application is able to read and update data in remote databases via well defined standards. RDA defines a set of client and server standards and a mapping of SQL commands to these services. RDA also defines an interface to ISO (transaction processing) two phase commit TP services in the case where updates to multiple remote databases need to be coordinated. RDA does not yet define interoperability between server databases (i.e. it is not yet a standard for distributed database management). Replication Scheme: Information that precisely identifies DBs, or partitions of DBs, to be copied and/or distributed, replication schedules, and master/remote sites that are to receive the copies. Spatial DBMS: Geographic information system that organizes and maintains spatial data (i.e. data with graphical attributes) in terms of type, scale, location(s), extent, topology and geometry. Supports queries of spatial data where the selection criteria are defined by spatial attributes. SRI: A Standing Request For Information (SRI) is a capability in which CASS monitors for the occurrence of conditions established by an application program, and notifies the calling or establishing application program when the conditions are satisfied. An SRI may be one of three types: timer-based, data-based, or message-based. Subject: An active entity, generally in the form of a person, process, or device that causes information to flow among objects or changes the system state. Technically, a process/domain pair. Transaction Journalling: Individual messages or database transactions are stored in a journal file, which may be a linear log file or a circular file.
ground_truth_requirements.label: FR

ground_truth_requirements.text: If the term is well recognized outside its container term, designate it as an element. Example: CTRY_CD, CTRY_NM, CTRY_ABBRD_NM, CTRY_OFF_NM, CTRY_SCP_NT_TX, and CTRY_PSTL_NM are all characteristics (ER-attributes) for the entity CTRY. In the relational world, they are columns within the table ctry, attributes of the entity ctry in er-modeling, and member attributes in object modeling. It would be expected that a submitter would identify them as attributes rather than elements. But if they were identified as attributes of the element ctry, then the additional baggage (other attributes) must be carried, or submitted as separate elements. We wish to limit the proliferation of tags, so we strongly urge folks to use XML_Attributes sparingly. If the term is well recognized outside its container term, designate it as an element. Example: CTRY_CD, CTRY_NM, CTRY_ABBRD_NM, CTRY_OFF_NM, CTRY_SCP_NT_TX, and CTRY_PSTL_NM are all characteristics (ER-attributes) for the entity CTRY. In the relational world, they are columns within the table ctry, attributes of the entity ctry in er-modeling, and member attributes in object modeling. It would be expected that a submitter would identify them as attributes rather than elements. But if they were identified as attributes of the element ctry, then the additional baggage (other attributes) must be carried, or submitted as separate elements. We wish to limit the proliferation of tags, so we strongly urge folks to use XML_Attributes sparingly.
