brd_document:
# BUSINESS REQUIREMENTS DOCUMENT

## Tactical Control System (TCS) — Common Ground Control Station for Unmanned Aerial Vehicles

**Document Reference:** TCS-BRD-001
**Program:** Tactical Control System (TCS) — Project ID 1999-TCS
**Prepared By:** Business Analysis Division, Naval Surface Warfare Center Dahlgren Division (NSWCDD)
**Document Status:** Pre-Requirements Baseline Draft
**Classification:** [Service Appropriate Classification]

---

## 1. PROBLEM STATEMENT

### 1.1 Background and Context

The United States military currently operates multiple Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) systems — including the Predator and Outrider — each with its own proprietary, service-specific ground control station. As UAV technology has proliferated across the Army, Navy, Marine Corps, and Air Force, each armed service has independently procured and fielded incompatible ground control systems. An operator trained on one service's UAV ground station cannot effectively transition to another without extensive retraining. Maintenance personnel must carry service-specific spare parts, diagnostic tools, and documentation. Logistics chains are duplicated, training pipelines are fragmented, and interoperability between services in joint operations is severely constrained.

### 1.2 Core Business Problem

The absence of a common, interoperable Tactical Control System creates the following measurable operational and programmatic pain points:

- **Operator retraining burden:** UAV operators trained on one platform require full retraining to operate a different UAV type, increasing personnel costs and reducing operational flexibility in joint or combined arms environments.
- **Logistics and sustainment inefficiency:** Each service maintains separate supply chains, spare parts inventories, and maintenance procedures for functionally equivalent ground control equipment, driving up total lifecycle costs.
- **Interoperability gaps:** Existing ground control stations do not share a common interface architecture with the DoD Command, Control, Communications, Computers, and Intelligence (C4I) ecosystem, limiting the ability to disseminate UAV-collected intelligence to joint force commanders in near-real time.
- **Scalability limitations:** Current systems are not modular or scalable, making it difficult to adapt a single ground station to varying deployment concepts — from land-based shelters to shipboard environments to airborne platforms — without significant re-engineering.
- **Imagery exploitation bottleneck:** Payload imagery collected by UAVs cannot be efficiently processed, annotated, and disseminated to downstream intelligence consumers because existing stations lack compliance with national imagery standards (e.g., NITF 2.0, CIGSS, USIS), creating delays in the intelligence cycle.
- **Recovery and continuity risk:** Current systems lack standardized, rapid recovery procedures following abnormal termination or power loss, creating unacceptable risk of loss of air vehicle control during critical flight phases.

### 1.3 Opportunity

The DoD has an opportunity to develop a single, common Tactical Control System that can serve as the standard ground control station across all armed services for current and future UAV platforms. By leveraging standard DoD hardware components, open architecture software principles, and compliance with existing military standards, the TCS can dramatically reduce lifecycle costs, improve joint interoperability, and enhance the operational effectiveness of UAV forces across all domains.

---

## 2. BUSINESS OBJECTIVES & SUCCESS CRITERIA

### 2.1 Business Objectives

| ID | Objective | Rationale |
|----|-----------|-----------|
| BO-01 | Provide a single, common ground control station capable of controlling multiple UAV types across all armed services | Eliminate duplicative procurement and training costs |
| BO-02 | Enable near-real-time dissemination of UAV-collected imagery and intelligence to joint C4I systems | Close the intelligence-to-action cycle gap |
| BO-03 | Achieve rapid system startup and recovery to minimize mission interruption | Protect air vehicle safety and mission continuity |
| BO-04 | Provide a scalable, modular hardware and software architecture that accommodates future UAV platforms and payloads | Protect the government's investment over the system lifecycle |
| BO-05 | Ensure operator safety and air vehicle safety through robust caution, warning, and fault management | Reduce risk of mishap and loss of air vehicle |
| BO-06 | Achieve full interoperability with the DoD C4I enterprise | Enable joint and combined arms operations |
| BO-07 | Reduce operator training time through a common, intuitive graphical user interface | Improve force readiness and reduce training pipeline costs |
| BO-08 | Support multi-domain deployment (land, sea, air, submarine) from a single system configuration | Maximize operational flexibility across services |

### 2.2 Success Criteria and Measurable KPIs

| KPI | Target | Source Basis |
|-----|--------|--------------|
| Normal startup time (power-on to Operations State) | ≤ 60 seconds | System startup requirement |
| Recovery startup time (abnormal termination to Operations State) | ≤ 45 seconds | Recovery mode requirement |
| Mission planning and launch readiness (from tasking) | ≤ 1 hour | Operational readiness requirement |
| Continuous operational availability | ≥ 72 hours without interruption | Endurance requirement |
| System throughput utilization ceiling (10-second average) | ≤ 50% of delivered throughput capacity (objective: ≤ 25%) | Performance headroom requirement |
| Spare memory capacity over delivered storage used | ≥ 50% (objective: ≥ 75%) | Growth and scalability requirement |
| Per-processor unused memory (10-second average, Normal Operations) | ≥ 50% unused | Processing headroom requirement |
| Per-processor unused throughput (10-second average) | ≥ 50% unused; ≥ 20% unused over any 1-second period | Processing headroom requirement |
| I/O channel reserve capacity | ≥ 50% reserve per processor | Expansion headroom requirement |
| Operator command-to-system acknowledgement latency | ≤ 1 second | HCI responsiveness requirement |
| Data latency | Not to exceed Predator or Outrider GCS latency, whichever is smaller | Benchmark-based latency requirement |
| System MTBF | ≥ MTBF specified in Predator and Outrider ORDs | Reliability requirement |
| System MTTR | ≤ MTTR specified in Predator and Outrider ORDs | Maintainability requirement |
| System operational availability (Ao) | ≥ Ao specified in Predator and Outrider ORDs | Availability requirement |
| Transport configuration/de-configuration time | ≤ 2 hours for all transport modes | Deployability requirement |
| Payload data storage capacity | ≥ 24 hours of payload data | Mission data retention requirement |
| Auditory alert volume | Adjustable to ≥ 20 dB above speech interference level at operator's ear | Human factors requirement |
| Map display simultaneous threat icons per overlay | ≥ 100 icons per overlay layer | Situational awareness requirement |

---

## 3. STAKEHOLDERS

### 3.1 Stakeholder Register

| Stakeholder | Role | Primary Need |
|-------------|------|--------------|
| **UAV Operator (Air Vehicle Operator)** | Primary end user; controls UAV flight, navigation, and emergency procedures | Intuitive, reliable interface to safely command and monitor one or more UAVs; clear cautions and warnings; minimal retraining burden when transitioning between UAV types |
| **Payload Operator** | End user; controls sensors (EO, IR, SAR) and manages imagery collection | Full payload control, real-time imagery display, exploitation tools, and ability to disseminate imagery to C4I consumers |
| **Mission Planner** | End user; develops flight route plans and payload plans prior to mission execution | Accurate map display, terrain analysis tools, threat overlay, waypoint planning, and payload swath visualization |
| **TCS System Administrator / Authorized Operator** | Privileged user; manages software upgrades, programmable parameters, and debug functions | Secure, password-protected access to system configuration, software installation, and diagnostic capabilities |
| **Maintenance Technician** | Maintainer; performs corrective and preventive maintenance on TCS hardware and software | Embedded diagnostics, BITE, accessible components, and the ability to execute maintenance software for AV, payload, data terminal, and workstation subsystems |
| **Intelligence Analyst / C4I Consumer** | Downstream consumer of TCS-disseminated imagery and tactical messages | NITF 2.0-compliant imagery with telemetry, annotated imagery, and tactical messages in standard formats (USMTF, TACFIRE, OTH-Gold, IEWCOMCAT) |
| **Individual Armed Services (Army, Navy, Marine Corps, Air Force)** | Procuring and operating organizations | Service-specific hardware configurations, desired functionality levels, and C4I connectivity tailored to their deployment concepts and area of operations |
| **Program Manager / Procuring Agency (NSWCDD)** | Government acquisition authority | System delivered on schedule, within cost, meeting all ORD and SSS requirements; quality assurance compliance |
| **Joint Force Commander / C4I System Operators** | Consumers of UAV intelligence products and tasking authorities | Timely, accurate intelligence products; ability to task UAV missions through connected C4I systems |
| **Safety Officer** | Oversight role | Assurance that no single hardware or software failure can result in an unsafe command to the air vehicle; personnel hazard protection |
| **Security Officer / Information Assurance Authority** | Oversight role | Compliance with DoD 5200.28(D) risk assessment, COMSEC requirements, and physical/logical protection of sensitive data |
| **Training Cadre / Instructors** | Developers and deliverers of TCS training programs | Embedded interactive training courseware, self-paced instruction, and measurable operator proficiency assessment |

---

## 4. CURRENT STATE vs. FUTURE STATE

### 4.1 Current State Limitations

| Domain | Current State Limitation |
|--------|--------------------------|
| **System Commonality** | Each UAV program (Predator, Outrider, and others) has a unique, proprietary ground control station. There is no common hardware or software baseline across services. |
| **Operator Training** | Operators trained on one UAV ground station cannot transition to another without full retraining, creating significant personnel readiness costs and limiting cross-service interoperability. |
| **C4I Integration** | Existing ground stations have limited or no standardized interfaces to the DoD C4I enterprise (ASAS, JSTARS, AFATDS, JMCIS, TBMCS, etc.), creating manual workarounds for intelligence dissemination. |
| **Imagery Standards Compliance** | Payload imagery is not consistently formatted to NITF 2.0, CIGSS, or USIS standards, limiting downstream exploitation by intelligence systems. |
| **Startup and Recovery** | No standardized rapid startup or recovery procedures exist; recovery from abnormal termination is ad hoc and may result in loss of mission data or air vehicle control. |
| **Situational Awareness** | Operators lack integrated map displays with threat overlays, fire support coordination zones, airspace control zones, and real-time AV position tracking in a single common operating picture. |
| **Payload Exploitation** | Limited on-station imagery exploitation capability; operators cannot annotate, enhance, freeze-frame, or disseminate imagery without external systems. |
| **Deployability** | Ground control stations are not designed for rapid multi-domain deployment; configuration for transport across ground, air, sea, and rail modes requires significant time and specialized procedures. |
| **Power Continuity** | Existing systems lack standardized uninterruptible power supply integration, creating risk of air vehicle loss during power outages at critical flight phases. |
| **Scalability** | Hardware is not modular or scalable; adding storage, processing capacity, or new UAV interfaces requires major hardware reconfiguration. |

### 4.2 Future State Capabilities Enabled by TCS

| Domain | Future State |
|--------|--------------|
| **System Commonality** | A single TCS hardware and software baseline, using standard DoD components, serves all armed services. Service-specific configurations are achieved through modular, scalable hardware selection per service procurement contracts. |
| **Operator Training** | A common, high-resolution GUI based on X-Windows and Motif enables operators trained on one UAV type to control different UAV types and payloads with minimal additional training. Embedded interactive training courseware supports self-paced proficiency development. |
| **C4I Integration** | Standardized hardware and software interfaces connect the TCS to over fifteen C4I systems (ASAS, JSTARS GSM, JMCIS, JSIPS-N, AFATDS, CARS, AFMSS, TAMPS, GCS/ACS IPF, TES, IAS, ATHS, ATWCS, Trojan Spirit II, TBMCS, MIES, ETRAC, COMPASS, TEG), enabling seamless tactical message exchange and imagery dissemination. |
| **Imagery Standards Compliance** | All imagery is processed and disseminated in compliance with NITF 2.0, CIGSS, USIS, and GCCS standards, enabling direct exploitation by downstream intelligence systems without reformatting. |
| **Startup and Recovery** | Normal startup completes within 60 seconds; recovery from abnormal termination completes within 45 seconds. Operators are guided through structured recovery options. System state data is automatically recorded to support event reconstruction. |
| **Situational Awareness** | Integrated map display with up to 100 simultaneous icons per overlay layer for threats, fire support coordination zones, airspace control zones, AV position, payload footprint, waypoints, and TCS/GDT positions. Line-of-sight terrain profile display and de-clutter capability are provided. |
| **Payload Exploitation** | On-station imagery exploitation includes image enhancement (contrast, brightness, edge enhancement, sharpness), annotation, freeze-frame capture, target location computation, range and bearing calculation, and NRT imagery display with date/time group and target coordinates. |
| **Deployability** | TCS is configurable for ground, air (CH-47/CH-53D, C-130), sea, and rail transport within 2 hours. The system can be operated from land-based platforms, ships, submarines, and tactical aircraft. |
| **Power Continuity** | Integrated UPS ensures power restoration in sufficient time to prevent loss of air vehicle control and critical mission data during outages. UPS is mandatory during critical flight phases (takeoff and landing). |
| **Scalability** | Modular hardware architecture supports storage expansion, additional processing nodes, long-range inter-TCS communications, and multiple external peripherals without major reconfiguration. |

---

## 5. BUSINESS RULES

### 5.1 Access Control and Authorization

- **BR-01:** Software upgrades may only be installed by Authorized Operators. Access to this capability shall be protected by password authentication at minimum.
- **BR-02:** Modification of TCS programmable parameters is restricted to Authorized Operators and shall require password authentication at minimum.
- **BR-03:** Software debug and diagnostic capabilities are restricted to Authorized Operators and shall require password authentication at minimum.
- **BR-04:** All TCS users, operators, maintainers, and other personnel with access to TCS shall be cleared to the highest sensitivity level of data that the TCS processes, stores, or transfers.
- **BR-05:** The TCS shall be approved for operation at the same classification level as the systems with which it interfaces.

### 5.2 Operator Acknowledgement and Warning Management

- **BR-06:** An operator must explicitly acknowledge and confirm before disabling the display of any critical warning flag associated with AV, Payload, ADT, GDT, or TCS faults. Silent dismissal of critical warnings is not permitted.
- **BR-07:** Warning messages shall be color-coded and flashed in accordance with MIL-STD-1472 guidelines based on mission criticality. The system shall not deviate from this coding scheme.
- **BR-08:** All warning messages and HCI actions shall be automatically archived for later review. Archiving cannot be disabled by the operator.
- **BR-09:** All operator inputs shall be error-checked such that no erroneous entry causes current processing to terminate. The system shall prompt for a valid input rather than failing silently.

### 5.3 Safety-Critical Command Rules

- **BR-10:** No single hardware failure shall result in an unsafe command being transmitted to the air vehicle.
- **BR-11:** No single software error shall result in an unsafe command being transmitted to the air vehicle.
- **BR-12:** Upon detection of loss of link with an air vehicle, the TCS shall automatically attempt to reestablish communications. The operator shall be presented with a Loss of Link timer and the last known AV status values with their timestamp.
- **BR-13:** The TCS shall compute and display the estimated AV position during Loss of Link based on the last known position and current flight mode (flight route plan, emergency flight route plan, or maintain current heading).
- **BR-14:** Power shall be restored in sufficient time to prevent loss of air vehicle control during power outages. An uninterruptible power supply is mandatory during takeoff and landing phases.

### 5.4 Shutdown and Data Management

- **BR-15:** Upon operator selection of a shutdown command, the TCS shall enter a Shutdown State that ensures power can be removed without damaging the system or impacting operations, and from which normal restart can be accomplished.
- **BR-16:** During shutdown, the operator shall specify whether mission data files are to be stored or deleted. The system shall execute the operator's selection before completing shutdown.
- **BR-17:** There shall be no operational modes active during the Shutdown State.

### 5.5 Recovery Procedures

- **BR-18:** Following abnormal termination, the TCS shall present the operator with exactly three recovery options: (1) Resume in the Same Modes and Data; (2) Resume in the Same Modes but Review and Modify Command Data; or (3) Perform a Command Shutdown and Startup via Normal Startup Mode. No other recovery paths are permitted without operator selection.
- **BR-19:** The TCS shall automatically record system state data, interface communications, and other information necessary to support event reconstruction following any abnormal termination.

### 5.6 Training Operations

- **BR-20:** Formal training operations shall not be conducted concurrently with the execution of an actual mission.
- **BR-21:** Actual communications processing may occur concurrently with training operations only if messages are explicitly identified as training messages.

### 5.7 Data Integrity and Message Handling

- **BR-22:** All digital messages received by the TCS shall be automatically checked for errors and corrected where possible. Messages that cannot be corrected shall be flagged to the operator; they shall not be silently discarded or processed as valid.
- **BR-23:** All incoming and outgoing formatted tactical messages shall be logged. This logging is mandatory and cannot be disabled during operations.
- **BR-24:** NITF 2.0 imagery files generated by the TCS shall contain all necessary telemetry and support data to permit subsequent imagery exploitation by C4I systems. Imagery files without complete telemetry shall not be exported.

### 5.8 Requirements Precedence

- **BR-25:** In the event of conflicting requirements, the following precedence order applies: (1) TCS Operational Requirements Document (ORD); (2) TCS System/Subsystem Specification (SSS); (3) Other TCS requirement documents; (4) Referenced documents. Conflicts shall be escalated to the procuring agency for resolution.

### 5.9 Security and Communications

- **BR-26:** All communication links between the TCS and other systems shall be secured at a level appropriate to the sensitivity of the material transmitted, in accordance with DoD Directive C-5200.5 (COMSEC).
- **BR-27:** The minimum security requirements for TCS shall be determined using the risk assessment procedures defined in DoD 5200.28(D), based on user clearance levels and data sensitivities of all interfaced systems.

---

## 6. SCOPE

### 6.1 In Scope

The following capabilities and system elements are within the scope of the TCS program:

**System Lifecycle and Modes:**
- Normal Startup Mode, Recovery Startup Mode, Operations State, and Shutdown State
- Automatic system state recording for event reconstruction

**Mission Planning:**
- Flight route planning including waypoint definition, terrain avoidance warning, and minimum reception altitude calculation for line-of-sight flights
- Payload planning (separate from flight route plans) for EO, IR, and SAR payloads
- Map display with zoom, scroll, print, and clear functions; support for DTED, DFAD, ADRG, CADRG, and vector format maps
- Threat overlay display with up to 100 simultaneous icons per overlay layer, including fire support coordination zones, airspace control zones, threat systems, and threat engagement envelopes with radar terrain masking
- Line-of-sight versus terrain profile display
- Coordinate display in Lat/Lon, UTM, and MGRS formats with multi-spheroid conversion

**Air Vehicle Control:**
- Command of flight behavior characteristics and navigation methods for all supported AV types
- Manual override capability for all automated flight and navigation functions
- Emergency recovery feature initiation
- AV status display including position, flight and avionics status, fuel parameters (remaining fuel, flow rate, bingo fuel), and Loss of Link management

**Data Link and Antenna Management:**
- ADT antenna control (pointing direction and transmission mode)
- ADT power, transmitter signal strength, and frequency control
- Automatic and manual control of data terminal transmitter/receiver functions, modes, and frequencies
- Signal strength and quality monitoring and display
- Visual depiction of minimum and maximum data link operational ranges
- Automatic and manual directional antenna pointing; 3-axis pedestal stabilization for mobile platforms
- LOS and SATCOM antenna positioning

**Payload Control and Imagery Processing:**
- Full payload control for all methods supported by installed payloads (EO, IR, SAR)
- Graphical payload control interface with override of automated and preprogrammed inputs
- Payload search footprint and search history display on map
- Payload data processing: formatting, storing, routing, recording, freeze-frame capture, retrieval, display, hard copy printing, and digital imagery export
- Storage of up to 24 hours of payload data
- Compliance with CIGSS, USIS, Video Working Group Standards Architecture, NITF 2.0, and GCCS
- Secondary HAE UAV payload imagery reception
- Limited imagery exploitation: image enhancement (contrast, brightness, edge enhancement, sharpness), annotation, target location computation, range and bearing calculation
- NRT imagery display with date/time group, target coordinates, north-seeking arrow, and AV position/heading
- Payload swath display for planning purposes

**C4I Communications:**
- Digital communications: tactical message creation and reception (USMTF, TACFIRE, OTH-Gold, IEWCOMCAT), annotated and un-annotated digital imagery exchange
- Analog communications: RS-170A imagery with and without overlay
- C4I interface monitoring and status display
- Message logging for all incoming and outgoing formatted tactical messages
- Interfaces to: ASAS, JSTARS GSM, JMCIS, JSIPS-N, AFATDS, CARS, AFMSS, TAMPS, GCS/ACS IPF, TES, IAS, ATHS, ATWCS, Trojan Spirit II, TBMCS, MIES, ETRAC, COMPASS, TEG

**Internal and Peripheral Interfaces:**
- AV Standard Interface (concurrent uplink and downlink)
- SAR Processor interface
- Intercom system (compatible with service-specific voice communication systems)
- VCR interface (RS-170A, full operator control via TCS display input devices)
- Internal hard copy printer (freeze-frame video, C4I messages, mission plans, FD/L information, map display)
- CD drive, tape drive, and RAID (as required) for data storage and retrieval
- Uninterruptible power supply interface
- Client-server interface for remotely hosted applications

**Maintenance and Diagnostics:**
- Execution of AV, payload, data terminal, and workstation/peripheral maintenance software
- Software debug capability for Authorized Operators
- Built-In Test Equipment (BITE) integration
- Software upgrade installation via CD-ROM and other media

**Human-Computer Interface:**
- High-resolution GUI based on X-Windows and Motif
- Four-window display configuration per control console (map/flight path, flight/payload data with video background, telemetry graphics, interface menus)
- Window-within-window display capability (video overlaid on map; map overlaid on video)
- Multi-level display tailoring; graphical situational awareness representations
- Ergonomic design for 5th-to-95th percentile operators; usable with cold weather clothing and MOPP gear
- Visual and auditory alert system with color coding, flashing, and adjustable volume
- Full keyboard and X/Y control device (trackball, mouse, joystick) complementary operation
- Complex joystick port capability
- DII Style Manager access for pointing device customization

**Training:**
- Embedded or add-on interactive training courseware with self-paced instruction
- Training capability covering mission planning, mission control and monitoring, imagery processing, tactical communications, AV control, and online diagnostics
- Operator and maintainer performance recording and proficiency measurement

**Physical and Environmental:**
- Ground, air (CH-47/CH-53D, C-130), sea, and rail transportability
- Operation from land-based, shipboard, submarine, and airborne platforms
- Ruggedized hardware for inter- and intra-theater movement
- EMI control and electromagnetic compatibility

**Power:**
- Support for 110/220V, 50/60 Hz military and commercial power sources
- UPS integration for critical flight phases

### 6.2 Out of Scope

The following items are explicitly outside the scope of this TCS program:

- **Air vehicle design and development:** The TCS does not include the design, manufacture, or modification of any UAV airframe, propulsion system, or avionics. AV-specific interface parameters are defined in AV-specific IDDs.
- **Payload hardware design:** The TCS does not include the design or manufacture of EO, IR, or SAR payload sensors. Payload interfaces are defined in applicable IDDs.
- **Formal training program development for Phase 1:** Formal institutional training programs are not required during Phase 1 (Program Definition and Risk Reduction). Trained OEM, government, and contractor personnel will support Phase 1 operations.
- **Service-specific procurement decisions:** The identification of specific TCS computing hardware, desired functionality levels, and C4I connectivity for each armed service is the responsibility of the individual services, not the TCS development program.
- **LRIP electromagnetic radiation specifications:** Specific RADHAZ and HERO values and LRIP electromagnetic radiation safety specifications will be determined and invoked in Phase 2, not Phase 1.
- **Weapons employment:** The TCS does not include direct weapons targeting or fire control functions beyond the provision of target location coordinates and tactical message generation.
- **Full imagery exploitation:** Deep imagery exploitation (e.g., all-source intelligence fusion, mensuration to national standards) is performed by downstream C4I and intelligence systems. The TCS provides only limited on-station exploitation.

---

## 7. CONSTRAINTS

### 7.1 Technical Constraints

| ID | Constraint |
|----|------------|
| TC-01 | TCS computing hardware shall be specified by service-specific procurement contracts; the TCS development program does not independently select hardware platforms. |
| TC-02 | All hardware shall be selected according to open architecture standards to ensure portability and future expandability. |
| TC-03 | The TCS HCI shall be implemented using X-Windows and Motif as the GUI framework. |
| TC-04 | The TCS software shall be developed in accordance with a tailored MIL-STD-498. |
| TC-05 | The TCS shall use the Defense Information Infrastructure Common Operating Environment (DII COE) to provide software flexibility and expandability. |
| TC-06 | The TCS shall use a modular software architecture to support future integration of additional UAV types and payloads without redesign of common planning and control functions. |
| TC-07 | The CD-ROM drive shall be compatible with NIMA DTED, DFAD, and embedded training media formats. |
| TC-08 | All imagery processing shall comply with NITF 2.0, CIGSS, USIS, Video Working Group Standards Architecture, and GCCS standards. |
| TC-09 | The AV Standard Interface shall be designed to accommodate future UAV types without requiring architectural changes. |
| TC-10 | The TCS shall support RS-170A analog video as the standard analog imagery format. |
| TC-11 | Power supply shall support 110/220V, 50/60 Hz sources and all DoD ground mobile, airborne, and shipboard electrical power sources. |
| TC-12 | The system shall be physically ruggedized and mounted to withstand inter- and intra-theater movement per the System/Subsystem Design Document environmental specifications. |
| TC-13 | Data latency shall not exceed the lower of the Predator or Outrider ground control station data latency benchmarks. |

### 7.2 Regulatory and Standards Constraints

| ID | Constraint |
|----|------------|
| RC-01 | Security requirements shall be determined using DoD 5200.28(D) risk assessment procedures. |
| RC-02 | All communication links shall be secured in accordance with DoD Directive C-5200.5 (COMSEC). |
| RC-03 | Personnel hazard design shall follow MIL-STD-2036, Section 5.1.3.11. |
| RC-04 | Human engineering safety design shall follow MIL-STD-1472D, Section 5.13. |
| RC-05 | Warning message color coding and flash frequencies shall comply with MIL-STD-1472. |
| RC-06 | System documentation shall follow MIL-STD-498 format. |
| RC-07 | The TCS shall comply with FAA rules for safe separation from other aircraft and safe altitude in civilian airspace. |
| RC-08 | EMI control shall be applied during Phase 1 design; specific electromagnetic environment values and RADHAZ/HERO values will be determined during Phase 1 and invoked in LRIP specifications during Phase 2. |
| RC-09 | The TCS shall be compatible with the external electromagnetic environment typical of the service-specific operational environment. |
| RC-10 | The Quality Assurance Program shall be conducted in accordance with the TCS Integration Program Quality Assurance Plan, NSWCDD/TR-96/XXX. |

### 7.3 Operational Constraints

| ID | Constraint |
|----|------------|
| OC-01 | The TCS shall be configurable for all transport modes (ground, air, sea, rail) within 2 hours and de-configurable within 2 hours. |
| OC-02 | The TCS shall be capable of planning and launching a mission within 1 hour of tasking after emplacement at the operational site. |
| OC-03 | Formal training operations shall not be conducted concurrently with actual mission execution. |
| OC-04 | All personnel with TCS access shall be cleared to the highest sensitivity level of data processed, stored, or transferred by the TCS. |
| OC-05 | The TCS shall be capable of operating continuously in functional Operations Mode for a minimum of 72 hours. |
| OC-06 | The TCS shall not support more than two simultaneous UAVs and their payloads from a single TCS station as the baseline operational requirement. |

---

## 8. ASSUMPTIONS & DEPENDENCIES

### 8.1 Assumptions

| ID | Assumption |
|----|------------|
| A-01 | Each armed service will independently identify and procure TCS computing hardware through service-specific procurement contracts. The TCS development program assumes that compliant hardware will be available and specified by the services prior to Phase 2 production. |
| A-02 | Air vehicle-specific interface parameters, uplink/downlink data formats, and AV documentation will be provided by the respective AV program offices and incorporated into AV-specific Interface Design Documents (IDDs). |
| A-03 | The Predator and Outrider ORDs will be available and will serve as the baseline benchmarks for MTBF, MTTR, and operational availability (Ao) targets. |
| A-04 | NIMA will continue to provide DTED, DFAD, and ADRG/CADRG map data in CD-ROM format compatible with the TCS CD-ROM drive specification. |
| A-05 | The C4I systems identified for integration (ASAS, JSTARS, JMCIS, AFATDS, etc.) will have stable interface specifications available for IDD development during Phase 1. |
| A-06 | Trained and proficient personnel from OEMs, government engineering teams, supporting contractors, and military personnel will be available to support Phase 1 demonstration system operations without a formal training program. |
| A-07 | The TCS HCI Specification (TCS 108) will be developed and baselined during Phase 1 to govern all HCI design and implementation decisions. |
| A-08 | Service-specific environmental specifications (temperature, humidity, shock, vibration, altitude) for land-based shelter and shipboard environments will be documented in the System/Subsystem Design Document and will be available to guide hardware ruggedization decisions. |
| A-09 | The DII COE framework will be available and supported throughout the TCS development lifecycle. |
| A-10 | External storage devices (beyond internal RAID and tape) will be available and compatible with TCS interfaces for payload data storage expansion to meet the 24-hour storage requirement. |
| A-11 | The risk assessment conducted under DoD 5200.28(D) will be completed during Phase 1 and will establish the security baseline for Phase 2 development. |

### 8.2 Dependencies

| ID | Dependency | Owner |
|----|------------|-------|
| D-01 | AV Standard IDD (TCS 229) must be developed and agreed upon with AV program offices before AV Standard Interface implementation can be completed | AV Program Offices / TCS Program |
| D-02 | All C4I Interface Design Documents (TCS 200 through TCS 240 series) must be developed in coordination with the respective C4I system program offices | C4I System Program Offices / TCS Program |
| D-03 | Service-specific hardware procurement contracts must identify computing hardware before hardware integration can be finalized | Individual Armed Services |
| D-04 | The TCS HCI Specification (TCS 108) must be baselined before HCI design and implementation begins | TCS Program / Human Factors Engineering |
| D-05 | NIMA map data (DTED, DFAD, ADRG, CADRG) must be available in compatible formats for map import and terrain analysis functions | NIMA |
| D-06 | The Predator and Outrider ORDs must be available and current to establish MTBF, MTTR, and Ao benchmarks | Predator and Outrider Program Offices |
| D-07 | The SAR Processor IDD (TCS 240) must be developed before SAR imagery dissemination functions can be implemented | TCS Program / SAR Processor Vendor |
| D-08 | The TCS Integration Program Quality Assurance Plan (NSWCDD/TR-96/XXX) must be approved and in effect before Phase 1 development activities commence | NSWCDD Quality Assurance |
| D-09 | Electromagnetic environment values and RADHAZ/HERO values must be determined during Phase 1 before EMI compliance can be verified | TCS Program / EMI Engineering |
| D-10 | The U.S. Army Intelligence and Electronic Warfare Tactical Proficiency Trainer interface specification must be available for the Outrider TCS compatibility objective | U.S. Army |

ground_truth_requirements.text: Upon the selection of a shutdown command the TCS shall enter the Shutdown State, which will cause the TCS to be placed in a condition where power can be removed without impacting operations as well as causing damage to the system, and from which restart of the system can be accomplished normally. [SSS043] Shutdown of the TCS shall include storage as well as deletion, as specified by the operator, of mission data files [SSS044], shutdown of appropriate functions [SSS045], shutdown of HCIs [SSS046], and proper termination of all active interfaces [SSS047]. There shall be no modes of operation in the shutdown state. [SSS048] Upon the selection of a shutdown command the TCS shall enter the Shutdown State, which will cause the TCS to be placed in a condition where power can be removed without impacting operations as well as causing damage to the system, and from which restart of the system can be accomplished normally. There shall be no modes of operation in the shutdown state. [SSS048]

ground_truth_requirements.text: The TCS shall display the AV status, to include but not be limited to the AV location and flight and avionics system status. ground_truth_requirements.text: The TCS shall have the functionality to process payload product data from Electro Optical (EO), Infrared (IR), and Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) payloads. [SSS182] This functionality, as a minimum, shall include: formatting, storing, internally routing, and recording the video [SSS190]; creating and storing a freeze frame of the video [SSS191]; retrieving and displaying the video [SSS192]; printing a hard copy of freeze frame video [SSS193]; and processing digital imagery for export and dissemination [SSS194]. Payload data includes the digital and analog imagery and associated telemetry sent to the TCS from each of these payloads. The TCS shall be able to store up to 24 hours of payload data. [SSS184] External storage can be utilized for this purpose. The TCS shall be in compliance with Common Imagery Ground Surface Station (CIGSS), United States Imagery Standards (USIS), Video Working Group Standards Architecture, National Imagery Transmission Format (NITF) Version 2.0, and Global Command Control Systems (GCCS) when processing payload imagery data. [SSS185] The NITF 2.0 imagery files generated by the TCS shall contain the necessary telemetry and support data to permit subsequent imagery exploitation by C4I systems. [SSS186] The TCS shall have a built-in text entry capability including the ability to annotate textual information on imagery. [SSS187] The TCS shall be capable of receiving secondary HAE UAV payload imagery. [SSS188] Payload telemetry data shall be available to support other TCS functions as required. [SSS189] The TCS shall have the functionality to process payload product data from Electro Optical (EO), Infrared (IR), and Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) payloads. Payload data includes the digital and analog imagery and associated telemetry sent to the TCS from each of these payloads. The TCS shall be able to store up to 24 hours of payload data. ground_truth_requirements.text: The TCS shall provide sufficient cues to allow the operator to safely take-off, land and navigate under Instrument Flight Rules. [SSS347] The TCS shall provide adequate capability to allow the operator to operate each UAV within its certified operational flight envelope. [SSS348] Appropriate cautions and warnings shall be provided to the operator if the air vehicle deviates into unsafe flight regime. [SSS349] For mission planning, the TCS shall provide terrain avoidance warning and minimum reception altitude calculations for line of sight flights. [SSS350] During mission execution, the TCS shall provide the operator a cautions and warnings when the UAV system has identified a malfunction. [SSS351] The TCS shall provide the required information to allow the operator to maintain safe separation from other aircraft and a safe altitude in civilian airspace per Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) rules. [SSS352] The TCS shall be designed such that no single hardware failure results in an unsafe command to be transmitted to the air vehicle. [SSS353] The TCS shall be designed such that no single software error results in an unsafe command to be transmitted to the air vehicle. [SSS556] The TCS shall be capable of restoring power in sufficient time to avoid loss of air vehicle control during power outages. [SSS354] The TCS shall monitor the uplink and downlink to each UAV under its control. [SSS355] Upon detection of loss of link, the TCS shall attempt to reestablish communications with the air vehicle. [SSS356] The TCS shall provide sufficient cues to allow the operator to safely take-off, land and navigate under Instrument Flight Rules. ground_truth_requirements.text: Using risk assessment procedures defined in DoD 5200.28(D), a risk index and the minimum security requirements for TCS shall be determined. [SSS363] The inputs to this procedure are the clearance or authorization of the TCS users and the sensitivities of the data that the TCS processes, stores or transfers. These requirements pertain to the TCS computer hardware and software. The TCS data sensitivities shall be determined by the data sensitivities of the systems with which it interfaces, to including the air vehicles, payloads, and C4I systems. ground_truth_requirements.text: Links that provide communications between the TCS and other systems shall be secured in a manner appropriate for the sensitivities of the material passed through such links, in accordance with DoD Directive C-5200.5, “Communication Security (COMSEC)” dated 21 April 1990. ground_truth_requirements.text: The TCS shall be designed to protect its communication and data links against enemy Electronic Warfare (EW) threats, physical anti-radiation weaponry and physical destruction. [SSS366] All hardware, software, documentation, and sensitive information processed by TCS shall be physically protected, minimally at the level determined by the risk index computed in Section 3.8.1, to prevent intentional as well as unintentional disclosure, destruction, and modification. [SSS367] The TCS shall be approved for operation at the same level as the systems with which it interfaces. ground_truth_requirements.text: The TCS software shall provide a high resolution, computer generated, graphical user interface that enables the UAV operator that is trained on one system to control different types of UAVs as well as UAV payloads with minimal additional training. [SSS404] Each control console shall have, at a minimum, the capability to display the following four display windows: (1) display to provide aircraft position, TCS position, flight path, and a waypoint graphics in the foreground which are positioned in relation to a map displayed in the background, (2) display to provide aircraft flight data or payload data in the foreground, and downlinked video in the background. (3)A display to provide graphic presentations of downlinked telemetry data, and (4) display to present the interface menus for workstation software. [SSS405] The TCS software shall provide a high resolution, computer generated, graphical user interface that enables the UAV operator that is trained on one system to control different types of UAVs as well as UAV payloads with minimal additional training. (3)A display to provide graphic presentations of downlinked telemetry data, and (4) display to present the interface menus for workstation software. [SSS405]
ground_truth_requirements.label: FR

ground_truth_requirements.text: The TCS data latency shall not be greater than that present in the Predator ground control station or Outrider ground control station, whichever is smaller. ground_truth_requirements.label: FR

ground_truth_requirements.text: The TCS reliability will be considered in every phase of the design and development process and shall achieve a system reliability (Mean Time Between Failures MTBF) equal to or greater than that which is specified in the Predator and Outrider ORDs. [SSS410] The TCS reliability will be considered in every phase of the design and development process and shall achieve a system reliability (Mean Time Between Failures MTBF) equal to or greater than that which is specified in the Predator and Outrider ORDs. [SSS410]
ground_truth_requirements.label: FR

ground_truth_requirements.text: The TCS maintainability will be considered in every phase of the design and development process and shall achieve a system maintainability (Mean Time To Repair MTTR) equal to or greater than that which is specified in the Predator and Outrider ORDs. [SSS411] The Design features shall be included to: [SSS412] The TCS maintainability will be considered in every phase of the design and development process and shall achieve a system maintainability (Mean Time To Repair MTTR) equal to or greater than that which is specified in the Predator and Outrider ORDs. [SSS411] The Design features shall be included to: [SSS412] minimize the number and frequency of required preventive maintenance actions based on performance requirements and lowest life cycle costs; minimize the contribution to degradation of TCS equipment reliability as a consequence of performing either preventive as well as corrective maintenance; enable the performance of all maintenance actions with safety and comparative ease by providing adequate access to all equipment components and minimizing the requirements for special tools and test equipment; minimize the requirement for specially trained maintenance personnel; improve system availability by the effective selection and incorporation of Built In Test Equipment (BITE); allow removal and replacement of replaceable units without soldering and unsoldering.
ground_truth_requirements.label: FR

ground_truth_requirements.text: The TCS equipment shall achieve an availability (Ao), as defined below, equal to or greater than that which is specified in the Predator and Outrider ORDs. [SSS413] Ao = (OT + ST)/(OT + ST + TPM + TCM + TALDT) where: The TCS equipment shall achieve an availability (Ao), as defined below, equal to or greater than that which is specified in the Predator and Outrider ORDs. [SSS413] Ao = (OT + ST)/(OT + ST + TPM + TCM + TALDT) where: OT denotes Operate Time ST denotes Standby Time TPM denotes Total Preventative Maintenance TCM denotes Total Corrective Maintenance TALDT denotes Total Administrative and Logistic Downtime
ground_truth_requirements.label: FR

ground_truth_requirements.text: The total, fully useable, addressable, physically present program instruction memory and data storage memory for each processor shall have at least 50% unused memory during the Normal Operations Mode over any 10 second period. [SSS414] The processing speed of each processor shall be such that at least 50% of the throughput of each processor remains unused over all 10 second periods and at least 20% of the throughput of each processor remains unused over one second periods regardless of the system function performed. [SSS415] The I/O channel reserve capability for each processor shall have at least a 50% reserve, addressable and useable, I/O channel capacity over any 10 second period. [SSS416] TCS software flexibility and expandability shall be provided through use of the DII COE and through use of standardized software development practices. [SSS417] The total, fully useable, addressable, physically present program instruction memory and data storage memory for each processor shall have at least 50% unused memory during the Normal Operations Mode over any 10 second period. [SSS414] The processing speed of each processor shall be such that at least 50% of the throughput of each processor remains unused over all 10 second periods and at least 20% of the throughput of each processor remains unused over one second periods regardless of the system function performed. [SSS415] The I/O channel reserve capability for each processor shall have at least a 50% reserve, addressable and useable, I/O channel capacity over any 10 second period. [SSS416] TCS software flexibility and expandability shall be provided through use of the DII COE and through use of standardized software development practices. [SSS417]
ground_truth_requirements.label: FR

ground_truth_requirements.text: Hardware and software shall be selected for use in the TCS with the goal of providing ease of future changes to the TCS elements. [SSS418] The object of portability for the TCS is to select or develop hardware which will readily host emerging software packages and software which will be as independent of host hardware as possible. The selection of processors, interface cards for communication interfaces, disk drives, video, networking equipment, and all other hardware for use in the TCS shall be made according to standards for production of an open architecture. [SSS419] The selection of operating system and programming language for use in the TCS shall be made according to standards for development of an open architecture. [SSS420] Hardware and software shall be selected for use in the TCS with the goal of providing ease of future changes to the TCS elements. The selection of processors, interface cards for communication interfaces, disk drives, video, networking equipment, and all other hardware for use in the TCS shall be made according to standards for production of an open architecture. [SSS419] The selection of operating system and programming language for use in the TCS shall be made according to standards for development of an open architecture. [SSS420]
ground_truth_requirements.label: FR

ground_truth_requirements.text: Testability shall be considered in the design and development of the TCS. [SSS421] The system shall be functionally and physically partitioned to allow for efficient fault isolation. [SSS422] Control over internal items and devices shall be provided for detecting and isolating internal faults. [SSS423] Test points and data paths shall be defined to support efficient fault isolation. [SSS424] Testability shall be considered in the design and development of the TCS. ground_truth_requirements.text: During Phase 1, control techniques to minimize electromagnetic interference, emanation, and susceptibility shall be used in the design of TCS equipment. [SSS432] This control will be inherent in the design of the TCS and the electrical and electronic equipment components and assemblies thereof. The susceptibility to coupling and the propagation of Electromagnetic Interference (EMI) will be minimized by component location, cable routing, and judicious use of shielding. There shall be neither unacceptable response nor malfunction of any TCS and associated equipment due to EMI produced by any as well as all of the TCS and equipment associated with the TCS. [SSS434] The TCS shall be compatible with the external electromagnetic environment that is typical of the service specific environment in the TCS will be operated. [SSS435] The specific electromagnetic environment values will be determined during Phase I of the TCS development. The TCS design shall ensure that personnel, fuel, and ordinance are not exposed to electromagnetic radiation as a result of operating the TCS. [SSS436] The specific radiation hazard (RADHAZ) and HERO values will be determined during Phase I of the TCS development. As TCS transitions into Phase 2, electromagnetic radiation safety and operation specifications will be invoked in the LRIP specification. [SSS438] A modular architecture shall be used by the TCS software in order to support future interoperability with multiple types of UAVs and payloads while maintaining consistent displays and user interfaces. [SSS439] Software components satisfying common planning and control functions will allow for vehicle specific components to be integrated in the future. ground_truth_requirements.text: The TCS shall have ergonomically designed operator controls and displays for the 5th percentile female to 95th percentile male operator. [SSS443] The controls shall allow the air vehicle and payload operators to perform mission control, mission monitoring, and mission updates and modifications while wearing cold weather clothing and in a Mission Oriented Protective Posture. [SSS444] The TCS shall provide the operator a caution and warning diagnostic when the TCS system has identified a malfunction. [SSS445] The TCS shall have monitor(s) that allow reading of displays under direct sunlight and low light level environments. [SSS446] The TCS HCI shall be a Graphical User Interface (GUI) based on X-windows and Motif. [SSS447] When performing a given task during mission execution, the operator shall be given appropriate warning messages from other concurrently-executing subsystem tasks. [SSS448] TCS Warning messages shall be color coded and flashed based on mission criticality. The color codes and flash frequencies will follow MIL-STD 1472 guidelines. [SSS449] The TCS operator shall be required to enter an acknowledgment prior to disabling the display of critical warning flags for any AV, Payload, ADT, GDT, and TCS faults. [SSS450] A combination of visual and auditory outputs will be provided to alert the TCS operator to situations which may require operator response. Visual alerts to the TCS operator shall be in the form of a displayed message box that has a display priority greater than other existing windows to ensure that it is viewable immediately by the operator. [SSS452] The position of the displayed message window shall be easily adjustable by the operator to ensure that important mission data is not obscured. [SSS453] In addition to displayed alert messages to the TCS operator, auditory alerts to include tones shall also be provided. [SSS454] The volume of these auditory tones shall be adjustable by the operator via keyboard and trackball input to at least 20dB above the speech interference level at the operator’s ear. [SSS455] All TCS warning messages and HCI actions shall be archived for later review. [SSS456] All TCS operator inputs shall be error checked such that any erroneous operator entry will not cause current processing to terminate. [SSS457] The HCI shall prompt the operator for a valid input. [SSS458] Display jitter and flicker shall not be perceptible by the operator. [SSS459] The operational tasks to be performed concurrently by the operator during normal operation will be determined by appropriate task analysis and function allocation. The TCS shall facilitate Human-Computer Interfaces (HCIs) that support operation of all system modes, functions, and capabilities. [SSS461] The Human Computer Interface (HCI) shall be designed and implemented in accordance with the TCS HCI Specification, TCS 108.[SSS462] The HCI shall provide redundancy in all operations, so that the loss of any one HCI input device does not prohibit operation of any TCS function. [SSS463] The TCS shall provide the functionality to display all HCI elements on any available monitor on the TCS workstation. [SSS464] The TCS shall be capable of displaying a window within a window format to include, as a minimum, displaying a video window overlaid on a map screen as well as a map screen overlaid on a video screen. [SSS465] The TCS shall provide full complementary control operations from the keyset as well as the X/Y control device (e.g., trackball, mouse, joystick). [SSS466] The TCS shall provide access to the DII Style Manager so that pointing device characteristics can be modified. [SSS467] The shall provide the functionality to have a maximum delay time of 1 second from operator command to system acknowledgement and response.[SSS 559] The TCS shall provide a capability for porting an off-the-shelf, complex control joystick with multiple toggle and multi-position switches as part of the TCS hardware suite. [SSS468] The TCS shall use graphical representations to convey information, such as system status, C4I links, and AV-GDT links. [SSS469] The TCS shall provide for multi-level information display tailoring by the operator. [SSS470] The TCS shall provide automated TCS system information, control options, and logical and simple operator guidance and support for immediate and adaptive responding to crisis situations. [SSS471] The TCS shall provide maximum automated system software support to system status monitoring and alerting of the TCS operator when a preset system parameter goes under as well as over a set threshold. [SSS472] The TCS shall provide the necessary processing, display, and control capabilities to ensure dynamic situational awareness input to the TCS operator. [SSS473] The TCS shall minimize alphanumeric data display in favor of graphic, pictorial information display [SSS474] The TCS HCI shall provide unambiguous AV and payload control and status feedback indicators to ensure safe, efficient operations of two AVs and their payloads by a single TCS station. [SSS475] The TCS shall provide for a specific icon shape on a constant contrast background, as well as other visual information coding mechanisms, to cue the TCS operator regarding which UAVs are under his or her primary control. [SSS476] The TCS HCI shall provide the capability to select and amplify an object and point on a map as well as payload screen. [SSS477] The TCS HCI shall provide coarse and fine payload control capabilities directly on the payload screen. [SSS478] The TCS HCI shall display the SAR imaging swath on the map display. [SSS479] The TCS HCI shall provide the on-screen capability to select and efficiently move as well as reorient a previously defined SAR imaging swath. [SSS480] The TCS HCI shall provide the capability to lock onto and hold a coordinate point on the payload imagery window. [SSS481] The TCS HCI shall provide the capability to display operator definable “Lock Out” zones around waypoints, Launch and Recovery Point (LRP), as well as any selected point on the AV flight path. [SSS482] The TCS HCI shall provide for a rapid means to cancel aural warnings. [SSS484] The TCS HCI shall provide for separation, grouping, and visual coding of multiple categories of alerts, to include Warnings, Cautions, and Advisories. [SSS485] The TCS HCI shall provide for visual Warnings, Cautions, and Advisories to be displayed at or near the center of the field of view, i.e., within a 30o cone, of all monitors in a TCS system. [SSS486] For AV safety as well as mission-critical Warnings, the TCS HCI shall provide a default selection as well as an override option, along with a selection of adaptive responses, and the minimum information necessary to assist the operator in responding quickly and adaptively to the emergency. [SSS487] The TCS HCI shall provide for on-screen information to include, as a minimum, overlays, headers, cursors, alphanumeric annotation, waypoints, crosshairs, designed to be visible against the complete spectrum of map and payload video backgrounds. [SSS488] The TCS HCI shall provide continuously-available, on-screen control functions for time and missioncritical operations, to include as a minimum print, freeze, declassification, mark VCR, declutter, cease RF transmission. [SSS489] The TCS HCI shall provide for the capability to automatically designate target locations from the payload screen onto the map screen. ground_truth_requirements.text: Formal training programs shall not be required for TCS Phase 1, Program definition and Risk Reduction. Trained and proficient personnel from the Original Equipment Manufacturers, Government Engineering Teams, supporting Contractors, and Military Personnel will support the operation and maintenance of the demonstration system equipment throughout Phase 1. TCS training and training support shall include the processes, procedures, techniques, training devices and equipment to train civilian, active duty and reserve military personnel to operate and support the TCS system. [SSS491] This will include: individual and crew training; new equipment training; initial, formal, and on-the-job training. TCS training will strike a balance between institutional, new equipment and unit training. The TCS system shall provide, for the operator and maintainer, an embedded or add-on interactive training courseware with self-paced instruction, duplicating UAV flight performance characteristics, capabilities, and limitations. [SSS492] The OUTRIDER TCS system shall be compatible with the U.S. Army Intelligence and Electronic Warfare Tactical Proficiency Trainer as an objective. [SSS493] The interactive courseware training capability for TCS shall be developed during Phase I and introduced to the user during scheduled demonstrations and tests. [SSS494] The training capability for performance of TCS functions shall include primary mission (flight route/payload) planning, mission control and monitoring, imagery processing, tactical communications, AV control communications and TCS system on line diagnostics. [SSS495] This capability will be qualified and improved during Phase I based upon use and feedback from qualified operators and users demonstrating TCS system equipment throughout Phase I. Instructional support materials and training courseware for classroom discussion and lecture will be developed to support institutional, new equipment training and unit training. The TCS shall provide the functional capability to train in the operation of the TCS system, performance of TCS UAV functions, and on line system troubleshooting. [SSS496] TCS system training shall include system architecture, component familiarization, and system startup, initialization, system recovery and shutdown. [SSS497] The TCS system shall not support formal Training operations concurrent with the execution of an actual mission. [SSS499] The capability for the conduct of actual communications processing concurrently with Training operations shall be provided if and only if messages are identified as training messages. [SSS500] Training shall be adequate to maintain operator and maintainer skills and proficiencies. [SSS501] TCS shall record operator and maintainer actions for self assessment and performance enhancement. [SSS502] Operator and maintainer performance shall be measurable using parameters retrievable from the TCS to determine proficiency levels. brd_document:
# Business Requirements Document (BRD)

**Project:** qheadache — Puzzle/Brain Game Application
**Project ID:** 2003-qheadache
**Document Version:** 1.0
**Status:** Draft — Pre-Requirements Elicitation

---

## 1. PROBLEM STATEMENT

There is a growing demand for lightweight, cross-platform puzzle and brain-training games that can be deployed on desktop environments without requiring internet connectivity or heavyweight runtimes. Currently, players who wish to engage in this category of game (tentatively named "Headache," likely a tile-sliding, peg-solitaire, or similar single-player logic puzzle) have no dedicated, polished desktop application available that:

- Tracks individual player performance over time in a meaningful, comparable way.
- Provides a competitive element through a persistent scoreboard, even in a single-machine context.
- Runs natively on both Windows and other major operating systems without platform-specific builds or significant porting effort.
- Gives players real-time feedback on their efficiency (time elapsed and number of moves made), enabling self-improvement.

The absence of such a tool means players cannot objectively measure their progress, compare performance against peers sharing the same machine, or be motivated by historical score data. This represents a missed opportunity to increase player engagement and retention. Anecdotally, players abandon puzzle games within the first few sessions when there is no feedback loop or competitive incentive — a problem this system is designed to solve.

---

## 2. BUSINESS OBJECTIVES & SUCCESS CRITERIA

### Business Objectives

| # | Objective | Rationale |
|---|-----------|-----------|
| BO-1 | Deliver a fully functional, self-contained desktop puzzle game that runs on all Qt-supported operating systems, with explicit support for Windows. | Maximise the addressable player base without maintaining separate codebases. |
| BO-2 | Provide players with real-time performance metrics (elapsed time and move count) during every play session. | Enable players to self-assess and improve, increasing replay value. |
| BO-3 | Maintain a persistent, named scoreboard so that multiple users sharing a machine can compete and compare results. | Drive engagement and repeat usage through social competition. |
| BO-4 | Offer a robust undo/redo capability so that players can experiment with strategies without penalty, reducing frustration and abandonment. | Lower the barrier to entry for new players; improve satisfaction scores. |
| BO-5 | Deliver a clean, menu-driven graphical interface that is intuitive without a manual. | Reduce support burden and onboarding time to under two minutes. |

### Success Criteria / KPIs

| KPI | Target |
|-----|--------|
| Cross-platform compatibility | Application launches and runs correctly on all operating systems officially supported by the Qt version selected, with zero platform-specific defects at release. |
| Windows portability | Zero critical defects specific to Windows OS at release. |
| Undo/Redo depth | System must support reversal of up to 1,000 consecutive player actions without data loss or application error. |
| Score persistence | 100% of completed play sessions where the player provides a name must result in a recorded score entry; zero data loss on normal application exit. |
| Scoreboard availability | The all-players scoreboard must be accessible from the main menu within one user interaction (one click/menu selection). |
| Session metric accuracy | Elapsed time display must be accurate to within ±1 second; move counter must reflect every discrete player action with zero missed increments. |
| Player onboarding time | A new player should be able to start a game, understand the metrics display, and access the scoreboard within 2 minutes of first launch, with no documentation. |

---

## 3. STAKEHOLDERS

| Stakeholder | Role | Primary Need |
|-------------|------|--------------|
| **End Player (Casual User)** | Primary user of the application; plays the puzzle game. | An engaging, frustration-free puzzle experience with clear feedback on performance and the ability to undo mistakes. |
| **Competitive Player** | A player motivated by rankings and personal bests. | Accurate score recording under their name and a visible leaderboard to compare against other players on the same machine. |
| **Game Developer / Development Team** | Builds and maintains the application. | Clear, unambiguous requirements; a single Qt-based codebase that compiles and runs across target platforms without significant rework. |
| **Project Sponsor / Product Owner** | Commissions and funds the project; defines scope. | On-time delivery of a working, polished game that meets stated objectives within budget. |
| **System Administrator / IT (where deployed in a lab or shared environment)** | Manages the machines on which the game is installed. | A portable, self-contained executable that does not require elevated privileges to run and stores score data in a predictable, accessible location. |

---

## 4. CURRENT STATE vs FUTURE STATE

### Current State

- No dedicated desktop application exists for this puzzle game.
- Players who wish to play must either use a web-based alternative (requiring internet access and a browser) or a non-instrumented version with no performance tracking.
- There is no mechanism to record or compare scores between sessions or between players sharing a machine.
- Players have no undo capability in existing alternatives, making the experience punishing for beginners and discouraging experimentation.
- Any existing implementations are either single-platform (Windows-only or Linux-only) or require separate builds per operating system, increasing maintenance overhead.
- Performance metrics (time, move count) are not surfaced to the player during gameplay, removing any incentive for efficiency improvement.

### Future State

- A single Qt-based application is compiled from one codebase and runs natively on Windows and all other Qt-supported desktop operating systems.
- Players see a clearly structured main window with a game board and a menu bar, providing immediate orientation.
- During every game session, a live timer and a live move counter are displayed, giving the player continuous performance feedback.
- Players can undo and redo up to 1,000 actions, enabling strategic experimentation without restarting.
- Upon completing a session, the player's name, elapsed time, and move count are saved to a persistent score record.
- Any player can open a scoreboard window at any time to view all recorded scores, fostering competition and motivation.

### Gap Summary

| Dimension | Current State | Future State |
|-----------|--------------|--------------|
| Platform support | Single-platform or browser-only | All Qt-supported OSes + explicit Windows support |
| Performance feedback | None | Live timer + live move counter |
| Undo capability | None or very limited | Up to 1,000 actions |
| Score persistence | None | Named, persistent score records |
| Scoreboard | None | Dedicated scoreboard window accessible from UI |
| UI structure | Ad hoc or absent | Standardised main window with board and menu bar |

---

## 5. BUSINESS RULES

**BR-1 — Undo/Redo Boundary:** The undo/redo history is scoped to the current play session only. History must support a minimum depth of 1,000 actions. Actions beyond the 1,000-action limit may be dropped from the oldest end of the history stack (FIFO eviction). Redo history is invalidated when the player makes a new action after undoing.

**BR-2 — Score Recording Eligibility:** A score entry is only recorded when a play session is completed (i.e., the puzzle is solved) and the player has provided a non-empty name. Incomplete or abandoned sessions must not generate a score record.

**BR-3 — Score Composition:** A score record must contain, at minimum: the player's name, the total elapsed play time (in seconds or finer granularity), and the total number of player actions taken during the session. Both metrics are mandatory; a record missing either is invalid.

**BR-4 — Move Counter Scope:** Every discrete action taken by the player on the game board (e.g., each tile move, peg jump, or equivalent puzzle interaction) must increment the action counter by exactly one. Undo and redo operations themselves may or may not count as actions — this must be decided and documented during requirements elicitation, but a consistent rule must be applied throughout.

**BR-5 — Timer Behaviour:** The play timer starts when the player begins a game session and stops when the puzzle is completed or the session is otherwise terminated. The timer must not count time when the application is paused or minimised (if a pause feature is implemented).

**BR-6 — Scoreboard Visibility:** The scoreboard must display all recorded scores across all players and all sessions stored on the machine. No score record may be hidden or filtered from the default scoreboard view without explicit player action.

**BR-7 — Player Name Uniqueness:** The system does not enforce unique player names. Multiple score entries may exist under the same name. The scoreboard must display all entries regardless of name duplication.

**BR-8 — Data Persistence:** Score data must survive application restarts. Data must be stored in a format and location accessible to the application without administrator privileges on Windows.

---

## 6. SCOPE

### In Scope

- A graphical desktop application built using the Qt framework.
- A main application window containing a game board area and a menu bar.
- Full implementation of the puzzle game logic (specific puzzle type to be confirmed during elicitation — assumed to be a single-player logic/tile puzzle).
- A real-time elapsed-time display visible during gameplay.
- A real-time player-action counter visible during gameplay.
- An undo/redo system supporting up to 1,000 actions per session.
- A score recording mechanism that captures player name, time, and action count upon session completion.
- A scoreboard window displaying all recorded player scores.
- Compilation and verified execution on Windows OS.
- Compatibility with all desktop operating systems supported by the chosen Qt version.

### Out of Scope

- Network/online multiplayer or online leaderboards.
- Mobile or tablet platforms (iOS, Android), even though Qt supports them.
- User account management, authentication, or password protection.
- Multiple distinct puzzle game types or game modes (unless elicitation reveals otherwise).
- Monetisation features (in-app purchases, advertisements).
- Automated installer or deployment packaging (unless added as a separate work item).
- Cloud synchronisation of scores.
- Accessibility features beyond what Qt provides natively (to be revisited if required by stakeholders).
- Administrative tools for managing or deleting score records (unless elicited as a need).

---

## 7. CONSTRAINTS

### Technical Constraints

| ID | Constraint |
|----|-----------|
| TC-1 | The application **must** be implemented using the Qt graphical library. No alternative UI frameworks are permitted. The specific Qt version must be agreed upon during project initiation and must support all target operating systems. |
| TC-2 | The application must be portable to and fully functional on the Windows operating system. The Windows build must be treated as a primary delivery target, not an afterthought. |
| TC-3 | The application must run on all operating systems officially supported by the selected Qt version (e.g., Linux distributions, macOS, Windows). A single codebase must serve all platforms. |
| TC-4 | Score data storage must not require a database server. A flat-file or embedded storage mechanism (e.g., XML, JSON, SQLite via Qt) is expected. |
| TC-5 | The undo/redo stack must be maintained entirely in memory during a session. Persistence of undo history across sessions is not required. |

### Operational Constraints

| ID | Constraint |
|----|-----------|
| OC-1 | The application must run without requiring administrator or root privileges on any supported operating system. |
| OC-2 | Score data files must be stored in a user-writable location (e.g., user home directory or application data folder) to ensure compatibility with multi-user operating environments. |

### Regulatory / Compliance Constraints

- No personal data beyond a player-supplied display name is collected. No GDPR, HIPAA, or other regulatory data-handling obligations are anticipated. If the application is deployed in an educational institution, local data retention policies may apply and should be confirmed with the project sponsor.

### Budget / Schedule Constraints

- To be confirmed by the project sponsor. The relatively narrow feature scope suggests this is a small-to-medium development effort; the team should plan for iterative delivery with a playable prototype available for stakeholder review before final feature completion.

---

## 8. ASSUMPTIONS & DEPENDENCIES

### Assumptions

| ID | Assumption |
|----|-----------|
| A-1 | The puzzle game type (e.g., sliding tile, peg solitaire, or similar single-player logic puzzle) has been conceptually agreed upon by the project sponsor, even if not yet formally documented. The specific game rules will be elicited and documented in the Software Requirements Specification (SRS). |
| A-2 | A single instance of the application runs per machine session. Concurrent multi-user access to the score file is not expected and need not be handled. |
| A-3 | The development team has existing familiarity with Qt development and does not require significant ramp-up time on the framework. |
| A-4 | The Qt version selected will be an actively maintained Long-Term Support (LTS) release to ensure stability and platform support longevity. |
| A-5 | "All Qt-supported operating systems" refers to desktop platforms only (Windows, macOS, Linux). Mobile platforms are explicitly excluded from this project's scope. |
| A-6 | Player names are self-reported and not validated for uniqueness or format beyond being non-empty. The system is trusted to be used in good faith. |
| A-7 | The scoreboard does not need to be sorted or ranked in any particular order at this stage; sorting preferences will be confirmed during elicitation. |
| A-8 | The action counter increments on every board-level player interaction. The treatment of undo/redo as countable actions will be confirmed with the product owner during requirements elicitation. |

### Dependencies

| ID | Dependency |
|----|-----------|
| D-1 | Availability of a licensed or open-source Qt installation (Qt is available under LGPL; licensing implications for distribution must be confirmed with the project sponsor). |
| D-2 | A Windows development and test environment must be available to the development team to validate the Windows portability requirement. |
| D-3 | The project sponsor must provide or approve the specific puzzle game rules and win condition before game board development can begin, as these directly affect the undo/redo and scoring logic. |
| D-4 | A file system location for score data storage must be agreed upon and tested on all target platforms before the score persistence feature is finalised. |

---

*This document was prepared as a pre-elicitation Business Requirements Document. It is intended to guide the requirements engineering process and will be superseded by a formal Software Requirements Specification (SRS) once stakeholder elicitation sessions are complete. All assumptions and open questions noted herein must be resolved before development commences.*
