# Business Requirements Document (BRD)

## Project: Outbreak Management (OM) System
### PHIN Project ID: 2005-PHIN
### Document Version: 1.0
### Classification: For Official Use Only

---

## 1. PROBLEM STATEMENT

Public health jurisdictions across the United States face a persistent and growing challenge in managing disease outbreak investigations effectively. When a health event occurs — whether a foodborne illness cluster, a bioterrorism incident, or a novel infectious disease outbreak — investigators must rapidly collect, organize, and share large volumes of complex, heterogeneous data involving people, animals, locations, objects, conveyances, and environmental factors. Today, this work is largely accomplished through fragmented, paper-based processes, siloed databases, and ad hoc spreadsheets that are rebuilt from scratch for each new event.

The consequences of this fragmentation are measurable and serious:

- **Redundant data entry** occurs because case data and exposure data about the same individual are captured in separate systems or forms, with no mechanism to recognize or prevent duplication. Investigators estimate that redundant demographic capture consumes a significant portion of field investigation time.
- **Inconsistent data structures** across jurisdictions mean that when two or more jurisdictions must collaborate on a multi-state outbreak, data mapping must be performed anew for every event, delaying analysis by days or weeks.
- **Broken chain of custody** for forensic and select-agent specimens creates legal and public health risk, as there is no standardized mechanism to document who collected, handled, and shipped a sample.
- **Inability to trace contacts systematically** means that exposed individuals may not be identified, monitored, or treated in time to prevent secondary spread.
- **Lack of interoperability** with laboratory systems, surveillance platforms, and early event detection (EED) systems forces manual re-entry of laboratory requests and results, introducing transcription errors and delays.
- **No standardized countermeasure tracking** means that vaccination and treatment administration cannot be reliably linked to the individuals who received them, undermining both clinical follow-up and after-action reporting.

The Public Health Information Network (PHIN) has identified Outbreak Management (OM) as a critical preparedness functional area. The absence of a standards-based, configurable, interoperable OM system represents a gap in the nation's public health preparedness infrastructure that this project is designed to close.

---

## 2. BUSINESS OBJECTIVES & SUCCESS CRITERIA

### 2.1 Business Objectives

The OM system must achieve the following business objectives:

1. **Standardize outbreak investigation data collection** across all participating jurisdictions so that common data elements are defined once and reused across events, eliminating per-event data mapping for standard fields.
2. **Enable rapid, configurable event-specific data capture** so that investigators can extend the standard data model with custom fields, entities, and relationship types within hours of a new event being declared, without requiring software development.
3. **Support end-to-end contact exposure tracing** by linking cases to contacts, contacts to multiple cases, and all entities to their epidemiological, laboratory, and treatment data.
4. **Ensure specimen and laboratory data integrity** through unique identifiers, chain-of-custody documentation, and bidirectional linkage between test requests and results.
5. **Achieve full interoperability** with partner systems — including laboratory information systems, surveillance platforms, and EED systems — through standards-based message exchange aligned with PHIN implementation guides.
6. **Protect public health data** from unauthorized access, corruption, and loss, including availability following catastrophic events.

### 2.2 Success Criteria and KPIs

| Objective | Key Performance Indicator | Target |
|---|---|---|
| Standardized data collection | Percentage of common data elements requiring re-mapping per new event | 0% (map once, reuse always) |
| Event configuration speed | Time from event declaration to availability of event-specific custom fields | < 4 hours without developer intervention |
| Contact tracing completeness | Percentage of exposed entities linked to at least one case record | ≥ 95% within 24 hours of identification |
| Laboratory interoperability | Percentage of lab test requests sent electronically via PHIN-compliant messages | 100% (key PHIN preparedness performance measure) |
| Case message exchange | Percentage of confirmed, probable, and suspect case notifications exchanged electronically with surveillance/EED systems | 100% (key PHIN preparedness performance measure) |
| Countermeasure message exchange | Percentage of countermeasure administration requests sent electronically | 100% (key PHIN preparedness performance measure) |
| Data redundancy elimination | Instances of duplicate demographic capture for the same entity across case and exposure records | 0 (system-enforced deduplication) |
| System availability | Uptime, including post-catastrophic-event recovery | ≥ 99.9% measured annually |
| Specimen identifier uniqueness | Percentage of Specimen IDs that are unique within jurisdiction | 100% |
| Event identifier uniqueness | Percentage of Event IDs that are unique within jurisdiction | 100% |

---

## 3. STAKEHOLDERS

| Stakeholder | Role | Primary Need |
|---|---|---|
| **Field Epidemiologist / Investigator** | End User — primary data collector in the field and during phone interviews | Rapid, intuitive data entry for cases, contacts, specimens, and exposures; printable questionnaires for field use |
| **Local Health Department (LHD) Staff** | End User — frontline case reporters and contact tracers | Ability to report cases, capture contact information, and link exposures to cases within their jurisdiction |
| **State Epidemiologist / Public Health Officer** | Decision Maker — oversees outbreak response | Aggregated data views (case counts, contact counts, vaccination tallies); cross-jurisdictional visibility |
| **Laboratory Personnel** | End User — specimen collectors and processors | Specimen ID assignment, chain-of-custody documentation, batch shipment tracking, and receipt of test requests |
| **Public Health Informaticist / System Administrator** | Administrator — configures the system for each event | Ability to add custom fields, entity types, relationship types, and questionnaires without code changes; management of data dictionaries and mapping interfaces |
| **PHIN / CDC Program Office** | Regulatory / Standards Body — defines interoperability standards and preparedness performance measures | Compliance with PHIN message implementation guides; measurable key performance indicators for preparedness |
| **Partner Jurisdiction Systems** | System Integrator — neighboring or co-investigating jurisdictions | Ability to send and receive investigation, case, exposure, and countermeasure data via standardized messages |
| **Surveillance / EED Systems** | System Integrator — downstream consumers of case notifications | Receipt of confirmed, probable, and suspect case messages in PHIN-compliant format |
| **Security / Compliance Officer** | Auditor — ensures data protection and regulatory compliance | Evidence of access controls, audit trails, and disaster recovery capability |
| **Veterinary / Animal Control Partners** | End User (specialized) — involved in zoonotic investigations | Ability to capture animal-specific entity data including species, owner, and tagging information |

---

## 4. CURRENT STATE vs. FUTURE STATE

### 4.1 Current State Limitations

Today's outbreak management environment is characterized by the following deficiencies:

- **Event-by-event data model reconstruction.** Each new outbreak event requires investigators to design new data collection instruments from scratch. There is no shared, reusable standard data structure, meaning that data mapping of common elements must be repeated for every event, consuming significant analyst time and introducing inconsistency.
- **Siloed entity data.** Information about persons, animals, locations, organizations, objects, conveyances, and gatherings involved in an investigation is captured in separate, unlinked systems or paper forms. There is no unified entity model, and relationships between entities (e.g., animal-to-person, person-to-place) cannot be formally recorded.
- **No systematic contact tracing support.** Linking exposed contacts to source cases is done manually, often in spreadsheets. There is no mechanism to record that a single contact is linked to multiple cases, or that a single case has multiple contacts, making network analysis impossible in real time.
- **Manual laboratory workflows.** Test requests are communicated by phone or fax. Results are manually transcribed into investigation records. There is no electronic linkage between a specimen collection record, the test request, and the result. Chain-of-custody documentation for forensic and select-agent samples is paper-based and inconsistent.
- **Redundant demographic data entry.** When the same individual appears as both a case subject and an exposure contact, their demographic information is entered multiple times in different forms, creating inconsistency and wasting investigator time.
- **No cross-jurisdictional interoperability.** When a person falls ill in one jurisdiction but resides in another, the two jurisdictions cannot electronically exchange investigation data. Case notifications, exposure contacts, and countermeasure records must be communicated informally.
- **No standardized countermeasure tracking.** Vaccination and treatment administration records are not systematically linked to the individuals who received them, making it impossible to report aggregate countermeasure data reliably.
- **Vulnerability to data loss.** Investigation data stored in local spreadsheets or desktop databases is at risk of loss during system failures or catastrophic events, with no guaranteed recovery capability.

### 4.2 Future State Capabilities

The new OM system will enable the following:

- A **unified, extensible data model** with a standard core structure that covers all common investigation elements, supplemented by event-specific custom fields, entity types, and relationship types that can be configured by administrators without software development.
- A **single entity record** that eliminates redundant demographic capture — when the same individual appears in both case and exposure contexts, their demographic data is entered once and referenced in both.
- **Comprehensive entity management** covering persons, organizations, locations, animals, objects, conveyances, gatherings, and other living things, all with structured data entry and formal relationship linkage.
- **End-to-end contact tracing** with many-to-many case-to-contact linkage, full exposure detail capture, and travel history recording.
- **Electronic laboratory workflows** including specimen ID assignment, test request creation and transmission, chain-of-custody documentation, batch shipment tracking, and automated result linkage back to specimen and subject records.
- **PHIN-compliant interoperability** enabling electronic exchange of case notifications, investigation data, exposure contacts, laboratory requests, countermeasure administration requests, and aggregated data with partner jurisdictions and downstream systems.
- **Configurable questionnaire management** with the ability to create, revise, publish, and print event-specific questionnaires and implementation guides.
- **Secure, resilient infrastructure** that protects data from unauthorized access and remains available following catastrophic events.

---

## 5. BUSINESS RULES

The following domain-specific rules govern system behavior:

### 5.1 Identification and Uniqueness
- Every health event under investigation must be assigned an Event ID that is unique within the jurisdiction. No two events within the same jurisdiction may share an Event ID.
- Every specimen or sample collected for laboratory testing must be assigned a Specimen ID that is unique within the jurisdiction.
- Every entity (person, animal, organization, etc.) involved in an investigation must be assigned a Subject ID that links them to their specimens, laboratory results, epidemiological data, and countermeasure records.
- Each new case must link its assigned Entity ID to the relevant Event ID, establishing the case's membership in the investigation.

### 5.2 Jurisdiction Capture
- For every case and associated investigation, both the **investigating jurisdiction** (the jurisdiction where the health event occurred or is being managed) and the **reporting jurisdiction** (the jurisdiction of the case's residence) must be captured and stored separately. These two jurisdictions may differ and both must be preserved.

### 5.3 Case Diagnosis Management
- The system must support updating a case's diagnosis classification — either manually by an authorized user or automatically — when the case definition for an event changes during the course of the investigation. Historical diagnosis classifications must be preserved for audit purposes.

### 5.4 Entity Deduplication
- When an entity appears in both case investigation and exposure investigation contexts, the system must recognize the entity as the same individual and prevent duplicate demographic data entry. A single demographic record must serve both contexts.

### 5.5 Contact Tracing Linkage
- The system must enforce a many-to-many relationship between contacts and cases: a single contact may be linked to multiple cases, and a single case may be linked to multiple contacts. Neither relationship is exclusive.
- All entities exposed to a case must be recorded and formally linked to that case record.

### 5.6 Specimen and Laboratory Data Rules
- Laboratory results must be linked to the specimen collection record via Specimen ID when that record exists.
- When no specimen collection record exists, laboratory results must be linked directly to the subject via Subject ID.
- When a specimen is derived from another specimen (e.g., aliquots, sub-samples), laboratory results for child specimens must be traceable to the parent specimen record.
- All levels of result granularity — from specimen-level to individual assay-level — must be supported and stored.

### 5.7 Chain of Custody
- For forensic and select-agent samples, a complete chain-of-custody record must be maintained, documenting: the identity of the collector, the collection location, every individual who handled the sample during preparation for shipment, and confirmation of acceptance by the shipper.

### 5.8 Countermeasure Linkage
- Every countermeasure (vaccination, prophylaxis, treatment) that is administered must be linked to the specific entity who received it. Countermeasure records may not exist as unlinked, free-standing records.

### 5.9 Questionnaire Governance
- Only authorized administrators may create, revise, or publish investigation-specific questionnaires. Version control must be maintained so that the questionnaire version in use at any point during an investigation can be identified.

### 5.10 Data Exchange and Mapping
- All data exchanges with partner systems must include clearly defined mapping interfaces and data dictionaries that describe both standard fields and any event-specific custom fields. Custom fields must be explicitly identified as such in all outbound messages.

### 5.11 Animal Identification
- Because standard demographic identifiers are insufficient for unambiguous animal identification, animals involved in investigations may require physical tagging. The system must support recording a tag-based Subject ID for animals.

---

## 6. SCOPE

### 6.1 In Scope

The following functional areas are within scope for the OM system:

- **Event Management:** Creation and management of health event records with unique Event IDs; assignment of cases to events; support for multi-jurisdictional events.
- **Entity Data Capture:** Structured data collection for all entity types involved in an investigation, including persons, organizations, locations, animals, objects, conveyances, public/private gatherings, and other living things.
- **Case Investigation:** Capture of epidemiological data (symptom onset, symptom type, risk factors, medical history, laboratory data, procedure data, questionnaire responses); dynamic event-specific case data; case diagnosis management.
- **Exposure Investigation:** Capture of exposure-level detail (exposure type, place, duration, frequency, proximity); contact identification and linkage; travel history; dynamic event-specific exposure data.
- **Contact Exposure Tracing:** Many-to-many case-to-contact linkage; entity relationship mapping (person-to-person, person-to-place, animal-to-person, object-to-place, etc.).
- **Specimen and Laboratory Management:** Specimen ID assignment; test request creation; chain-of-custody documentation; batch shipment tracking; result receipt and linkage; parent-child specimen relationships; multi-granularity result storage.
- **Countermeasure Tracking:** Recording and linkage of administered vaccinations, prophylaxis, and treatments to recipient entities.
- **Questionnaire Management:** Configuration, versioning, publication, and printing of event-specific questionnaires and implementation guides.
- **Data Aggregation:** Aggregation of case counts, contact counts, vaccination/treatment tallies, and other summary statistics.
- **System Integration and Data Exchange:** PHIN-compliant electronic message exchange for laboratory test requests, case notifications (confirmed/probable/suspect), investigation data, exposure contacts, countermeasure administration requests, and aggregated data with partner OM systems, surveillance systems, and EED systems.
- **System Configuration:** Administrator capability to add custom data fields, entity types, entity relationship types, and event-specific data structures without software development.
- **Security and Availability:** Access controls, data protection from sabotage and unauthorized access, and post-catastrophic-event availability.

### 6.2 Out of Scope

- **Surveillance system functionality** (the OM system exchanges data with surveillance systems but does not replace them).
- **Early Event Detection (EED) system functionality** (the OM system exchanges data with EED systems but does not perform EED functions).
- **Laboratory Information Management System (LIMS) functionality** (the OM system creates and sends test requests and receives results but does not perform laboratory analysis or internal LIMS workflows).
- **Countermeasure inventory management** (the OM system records administration of countermeasures but does not manage supply chain or inventory).
- **Clinical treatment management** (the OM system captures treatment data for epidemiological purposes but is not a clinical decision support or electronic health record system).
- **Public-facing reporting portals** (the OM system is designed for public health professionals, not for direct public access).

---

## 7. CONSTRAINTS

### 7.1 Regulatory and Standards Compliance
- The system must comply with all applicable **PHIN standards**, including PHIN message implementation guides for case notifications, laboratory test requests, investigation data, exposure contacts, and countermeasure administration. Compliance with PHIN Preparedness Key Performance Measures (available at www.cdc.gov/phin) is mandatory.
- The system must adhere to **PHIN Preparedness Cross Functional Components Requirements** for security and availability, as published by CDC.
- All data handling must comply with applicable federal and state **health data privacy regulations**, including HIPAA Privacy and Security Rules, given that the system will process individually identifiable health information.
- Handling of **select agent** specimens must comply with federal select agent program regulations (42 CFR Part 73), which impose strict chain-of-custody and access control requirements.

### 7.2 Interoperability Constraints
- All inter-system message exchange must use **PHIN-compliant message formats** as defined in the applicable PHIN message implementation guides. Proprietary message formats are not acceptable for cross-jurisdictional or cross-system exchange.
- The system must be capable of accepting data from other partner OM systems, meaning it must support inbound as well as outbound message exchange.
- Data dictionaries and mapping interfaces must be included in all data exchanges to support both standard and custom field interpretation by receiving systems.

### 7.3 Configuration Constraints
- The system must support configuration of new data fields, entity types, and relationship types **without requiring software code changes or vendor involvement**. This is a hard constraint driven by the operational reality that new outbreak events may require unique data structures within hours of event declaration.

### 7.4 Identifier Constraints
- Event IDs and Specimen IDs must be **unique within the jurisdiction** that assigns them. The system must enforce this uniqueness at the point of record creation and prevent duplicates.

### 7.5 Availability Constraints
- The system must remain available — or be recoverable — **subsequent to a catastrophic event** (e.g., natural disaster, cyberattack). This implies requirements for redundant infrastructure, geographic failover, and tested disaster recovery procedures, consistent with PHIN cross-functional requirements.

### 7.6 Operational Constraints
- The system must support **offline or printable questionnaire use** for field investigations where electronic access may be unavailable (e.g., remote locations, disaster zones). Printed questionnaires must be suitable for phone interviews as well as in-person field use.

---

## 8. ASSUMPTIONS & DEPENDENCIES

### 8.1 Assumptions

- **PHIN standards are stable enough** for implementation. It is assumed that the PHIN message implementation guides referenced throughout this document are published, accessible at www.cdc.gov/phin, and will not undergo breaking changes during the development lifecycle without advance notice.
- **Jurisdictions have network connectivity** sufficient to support electronic data exchange under normal operating conditions. Offline capability is required only for questionnaire printing and field data collection, not for full system operation.
- **Each jurisdiction operates its own instance** or a shared instance of the OM system, and jurisdiction-level uniqueness of Event IDs and Specimen IDs is enforced within that instance. Cross-jurisdictional ID uniqueness is not assumed.
- **Partner systems** (laboratory systems, surveillance systems, EED systems) are capable of receiving and processing PHIN-compliant messages. The OM system is responsible for generating compliant outbound messages; it is not responsible for remediating non-compliant partner systems.
- **Authorized administrators** within each jurisdiction will be trained and available to perform event-specific system configuration (custom fields, questionnaires, entity types) at the time a new health event is declared.
- **Animals involved in investigations can be physically tagged** when necessary to support unambiguous identification. The operational process for tagging is outside the scope of the software system but is assumed to be feasible in the field.
- **Legal authority exists** for the collection of all data elements described in this document, including personally identifiable information about cases and contacts, animal owner information, and chain-of-custody records for forensic samples.

### 8.2 Dependencies

- **PHIN Message Implementation Guides:** The design of all inter-system message formats depends on the published PHIN implementation guides. Any changes to these guides may require corresponding changes to the OM system's integration layer.
- **PHIN Preparedness Cross Functional Components Requirements:** Security and availability requirements for this system are defined in a separate PHIN document. That document must be reviewed and incorporated into the technical design; this BRD does not restate those requirements in full.
- **Partner System Readiness:** The realization of interoperability benefits depends on partner jurisdictions and partner systems (laboratory, surveillance, EED) having implemented compatible PHIN-compliant interfaces. The OM system cannot exchange data electronically with systems that have not implemented the required message standards.
- **Jurisdiction Governance Agreements:** Multi-jurisdictional data exchange (e.g., sharing investigation data when a case resides in one jurisdiction but is investigated in another) depends on the existence of data sharing agreements between jurisdictions. These agreements are a pre-condition for cross-jurisdictional interoperability and are outside the scope of this system.
- **Infrastructure Provisioning:** The availability and disaster recovery requirements of this system depend on the provisioning of appropriate hosting infrastructure (redundant servers, geographic failover, backup systems). This infrastructure must be planned and funded as part of the overall program, not solely as a software deliverable.
- **Vocabulary and Code Set Standards:** Structured data entry for common fields (e.g., symptom types, exposure types, conveyance types) depends on the adoption of standard public health vocabularies and code sets (e.g., SNOMED CT, LOINC, HL7 value sets). The selection and governance of these vocabularies must be resolved prior to detailed system design.

---

*This document represents the business requirements baseline for the PHIN Outbreak Management (OM) system as of the date of issuance. All functional requirements derived from this document should be traceable to the objectives, business rules, and scope defined herein. Changes to this document require review and approval by the PHIN Program Office and affected jurisdiction stakeholders.*

# Business Requirements Document (BRD)

## Project: EIRENE System Requirements Specification — Railway Radio System (GSM-R)
**Project ID:** 2006 – EIRENE SYS 15
**Document Status:** Pre-Requirements Elicitation Draft
**Prepared by:** Business Analysis Function
**Audience:** Requirements Engineers, System Architects, National Railway Operators, Infrastructure Managers

---

## 1. PROBLEM STATEMENT

European railway operations are conducted across a patchwork of national networks, each historically equipped with incompatible radio communication systems. Train drivers, controllers, shunting teams, and maintenance personnel crossing national borders — or operating on infrastructure shared between multiple national operators — face a fundamental breakdown in voice and data communications at precisely the moments when reliable, prioritised communication is most critical to safety.

The specific pain points driving this initiative are:

**Interoperability failure at national borders.** A train crew operating a cross-border service cannot maintain continuous radio contact with ground controllers when transitioning between national railway networks. There is no common protocol, numbering plan, or frequency allocation that allows seamless handover. This creates communication blackouts that are operationally unacceptable and potentially life-threatening.

**Absence of a standardised emergency call mechanism.** There is no pan-European standard for initiating, routing, or confirming railway emergency calls. Emergency communications today depend on national implementations that are mutually incompatible. A driver initiating an emergency call cannot be guaranteed that all relevant parties — other train drivers in the area, ground controllers, and network infrastructure — will receive and acknowledge the alert in a consistent, timely manner.

**Inconsistent priority handling.** Different national systems apply different rules for call priority, meaning that safety-critical communications (e.g., emergency calls, ETCS-related data) may be pre-empted by lower-priority traffic in some networks. There is no enforced hierarchy of call priority that applies uniformly across all participating networks.

**No standardised functional addressing.** Train drivers, shunting leaders, maintenance teams, and controllers are identified differently in each national system. There is no common scheme for addressing a "driver of train 12345" or "controller at location X" that works across network boundaries, making location-dependent call routing and functional number registration impossible at a European level.

**Coverage gaps on high-speed lines.** Existing radio infrastructure does not guarantee the signal coverage levels required to support ETCS Level 2 and Level 3 train control, particularly on lines operating at speeds up to 220 km/h. The absence of defined, measurable coverage probability targets means that infrastructure investment decisions are made without a common baseline.

**Quantified pain points:**
- Cross-border communication failures affect an estimated hundreds of international train services daily across the European rail network.
- Emergency call setup times are unmeasured and unguaranteed under current national systems.
- Coverage probability on safety-critical lines is unverified against any common standard.
- Functional number registration and deregistration are manual, error-prone, and non-interoperable.

---

## 2. BUSINESS OBJECTIVES & SUCCESS CRITERIA

The EIRENE system shall achieve the following business objectives, each with measurable success criteria that requirements engineers shall use to validate the implemented system.

### Objective 1: Guarantee Radio Coverage for Safe Train Operation
The system shall provide a radio network that meets defined minimum coverage levels, ensuring that mobile equipment can communicate reliably throughout the railway operating environment.

**Success Criteria:**
- Coverage probability of at least 95% (measured per 100-metre location interval) at a signal level of 38.5 dBmV/m (–98 dBm) for voice and non-safety-critical data across all covered lines.
- Coverage probability of at least 95% at 41.5 dBmV/m (–95 dBm) on all lines equipped with ETCS Level 2 or Level 3 for speeds up to 220 km/h.
- Maximum antenna-to-receiver loss of 3 dB, with an additional 3 dB margin for ageing and other factors built into the coverage design.

### Objective 2: Achieve Defined Call Setup Performance
The system shall establish calls within the time limits defined in the EIRENE Functional Requirements Specification (FRS), even when authentication and ciphering are active.

**Success Criteria:**
- All call setup times as defined in the EIRENE FRS shall be met with authentication and ciphering enabled (authentication may be deferred where necessary to meet timing targets).
- Transit time through any network external to GSM (e.g., MSC to controller via ISDN) shall not exceed 250 milliseconds.
- If a Railway emergency call is not established within 2 seconds, the user shall receive an audible and visual status indication.
- Automatic retry of a failed Railway emergency call shall continue for up to 30 seconds or until the user abandons the attempt.

### Objective 3: Enable Pan-European Interoperability
The system shall allow railway radio equipment from any participating national network to operate on any other participating national network without modification to the mobile equipment.

**Success Criteria:**
- All mobile equipment shall operate in the R-GSM band (876–915 / 921–960 MHz) without reconfiguration.
- A standardised EIRENE numbering plan shall be implemented as a private numbering plan within every GSM-R network, with internationally agreed short codes and function codes.
- Re-registration of on-train functional numbers shall occur automatically every time a train crosses from one EIRENE network to another.
- International call setup between EIRENE networks shall use the standardised Breakout Code and International Code combination.

### Objective 4: Provide Reliable Emergency and Priority Communications
The system shall ensure that safety-critical communications are never pre-empted by lower-priority traffic and that emergency calls are confirmed to a designated confirmation centre.

**Success Criteria:**
- All Railway emergency calls shall use GSM VGCS with eMLPP priority levels enforced across all network elements.
- 100% of Railway emergency calls shall trigger an automatic confirmation process after call cleardown, with confirmation messages sent to a designated confirmation centre using a standardised short code.
- The Railway emergency call retry mechanism shall operate without any manual intervention for up to 30 seconds.

### Objective 5: Support Operational Railway Working Environments
The system shall support shunting, maintenance, and multi-driver operations with dedicated group call management and link assurance mechanisms.

**Success Criteria:**
- Shunting mode shall support both common and dedicated group calls, with automatic group ID management via SIM.
- Link assurance signals shall be generated and deactivated in accordance with the MORANE SM FFFIS specification.
- Multi-party calls between active cabs on the same train shall be established and maintained using the GSM Multi-Party service.

### Objective 6: Ensure Equipment Durability and Quality
All mobile equipment shall meet defined environmental, EMC, and quality standards appropriate to the railway operating environment.

**Success Criteria:**
- All equipment shall operate across the temperature range –20°C to +55°C (Cab radio: –20°C to +70°C) and survive storage at –40°C.
- All equipment shall pass the environmental test suite defined in the IEC 68 series (Cold, Dry heat, Damp heat, Impact, Vibration, Acceleration, Corrosive atmospheres, Air pressure, Temperature change, Fire hazard).
- All design, manufacturing, testing, and installation shall comply with ISO 9001 quality procedures.

---

## 3. STAKEHOLDERS

### 3.1 National Railway Infrastructure Managers
**Role:** Network owners and operators responsible for deploying and maintaining the GSM-R network infrastructure within their national territory.
**Primary Need:** A clear specification of network design criteria (coverage, frequency, call setup performance) that they must meet to achieve interoperability with other EIRENE networks. They need to understand the infrastructure investment required and the bilateral agreements needed at network boundaries.

### 3.2 Train Operating Companies (TOCs) and Cross-Border Operators
**Role:** Operators of train services, including international services crossing multiple national networks.
**Primary Need:** Assurance that their rolling stock, equipped with EIRENE-compliant Cab radios, will function correctly on any participating national network without modification. They need the functional addressing and re-registration mechanisms to work transparently as trains cross borders.

### 3.3 Train Drivers
**Role:** Primary end users of Cab radios in operational service.
**Primary Need:** Simple, reliable access to controllers, emergency services, and other drivers. The MMI must minimise the cognitive burden on the driver, particularly for emergency and priority calls. Drivers need confidence that the system will work in all operating conditions, including tunnels, high-speed lines, and cross-border transitions.

### 3.4 Ground Controllers and Dispatchers
**Role:** Railway operations controllers who manage train movements and communicate with drivers via fixed controller terminals connected to the GSM-R network.
**Primary Need:** The ability to initiate, manage, and terminate group calls (VGCS/VBS) to trains in their area, including the ability to grant and revoke talking rights using standardised DTMF sequences. Controllers need reliable call setup times and priority enforcement.

### 3.5 Shunting Team Leaders and Members
**Role:** Operational staff conducting shunting operations in yards and stations.
**Primary Need:** Reliable group communications within a dedicated shunting group, with link assurance to confirm that the radio connection is maintained. Shunting leaders need the ability to leave and rejoin group calls without disrupting other team members.

### 3.6 Maintenance Teams
**Role:** Railway maintenance personnel working on or near the track.
**Primary Need:** Access to the GSM-R network using operational radios with functional number registration, and the ability to communicate with controllers and other maintenance team members. Equipment must withstand the harsh physical environment of trackside maintenance work.

### 3.7 Mobile Equipment Manufacturers
**Role:** Suppliers of Cab radios, operational radios, general purpose radios, and associated equipment.
**Primary Need:** A precise, unambiguous specification of hardware, software, environmental, EMC, and interface requirements that their equipment must meet to be certified as EIRENE-compliant. Manufacturers need clarity on which requirements are mandatory versus optional.

### 3.8 GSM-R Network Operators
**Role:** Entities responsible for operating the GSM-R network, including MSCs, HLRs, routing databases, and confirmation centres.
**Primary Need:** Specifications for network-side behaviour, including VGCS/VBS call management, DTMF handling, functional number routing databases, USSD message handling, and inter-network signalling using SS7/MAP.

### 3.9 ETCS/ERTMS System Integrators
**Role:** Integrators responsible for connecting the EIRENE radio system to the European Train Control System.
**Primary Need:** A defined interface (EURORADIO FFFIS) between the Cab radio and the ETCS on-board unit, and assurance that the radio network meets the coverage and performance requirements for ETCS Level 2 and Level 3 operation.

### 3.10 Regulatory and Standards Bodies (UIC, ETSI, ITU-T)
**Role:** Bodies responsible for allocating frequencies, defining GSM standards, and approving the EIRENE specification for use as a basis for interoperability.
**Primary Need:** Assurance that the EIRENE system is built on recognised international standards (GSM, EN 301 515, ITU-T E.164) and that the numbering plan and frequency allocations are coordinated with existing international frameworks.

### 3.11 Maintenance and Test Facilities
**Role:** Accredited test laboratories and maintenance organisations responsible for certifying equipment compliance and maintaining equipment in service.
**Primary Need:** Clear test procedures (IEC 68, EN 61000-4, EN 50140, EN 45001) and quality standards (ISO 9001) against which equipment can be evaluated and certified.

---

## 4. CURRENT STATE vs FUTURE STATE

### 4.1 Current State — Limitations

**Fragmented national radio systems.** Each European national railway operates its own radio system, typically based on analogue VHF/UHF technology or early digital systems. These systems are mutually incompatible in terms of frequency, protocol, and addressing. A train crossing from one country to another must either carry multiple radios or accept a complete loss of radio communication at the border.

**No standardised emergency call protocol.** Emergency calls are handled differently in each national system. There is no guaranteed mechanism for broadcasting an emergency to all trains in an area, no standardised confirmation process, and no defined retry behaviour if the initial call fails. The absence of a common priority scheme means that emergency calls may be delayed by lower-priority traffic.

**Manual, error-prone functional addressing.** There is no standardised way to address a train by its train number, a driver by their functional role, or a controller by their location. Calls are made using MSISDN numbers that must be looked up manually, creating delays and errors in time-critical situations.

**Unverified coverage on high-speed and ETCS lines.** There are no common coverage probability targets for railway radio networks. Infrastructure managers invest in coverage without a common baseline, resulting in coverage gaps that are particularly dangerous on high-speed lines where ETCS Level 2 and Level 3 train control depends on continuous radio connectivity.

**No standardised shunting communications.** Shunting operations rely on local radio systems that are not integrated with the main railway radio network. There is no standardised group call mechanism for shunting teams, no link assurance protocol, and no integration with the functional addressing scheme.

**Equipment diversity and incompatibility.** Mobile radio equipment is procured to national specifications, resulting in a wide variety of equipment types that cannot be used interchangeably across national networks. There are no common environmental, EMC, or quality standards applied consistently across all equipment.

**No direct mode fallback standard.** In areas without network coverage, there is no standardised fallback to direct (simplex) radio communication. Where direct mode is implemented, it uses different frequencies and protocols in different countries.

### 4.2 Future State — What the EIRENE System Will Enable

**Seamless cross-border operation.** A single EIRENE-compliant Cab radio, equipped with a MORANE-compliant SIM card, will operate on any participating national GSM-R network. Functional number re-registration will occur automatically at network boundaries, and international calls will be routed using a standardised numbering plan without any driver intervention.

**Guaranteed emergency call performance.** Railway emergency calls will be initiated using a single dedicated control (e.g., a red button), broadcast to all trains in the affected area using GSM VGCS, automatically retried for up to 30 seconds if unsuccessful, and confirmed to a designated confirmation centre after cleardown. All of this will occur with defined, measurable performance targets.

**Standardised functional addressing.** Every train, engine, coach, driver, controller, shunting team, and maintenance team will be addressable using a standardised EIRENE numbering plan. Location-dependent addressing will allow calls to be routed to the correct controller based on the train's current position, using cell-based routing as a minimum.

**Verified coverage on safety-critical lines.** Infrastructure managers will design and verify their networks against common coverage probability targets, ensuring that ETCS Level 2 and Level 3 operations are supported with the required signal levels.

**Integrated shunting and maintenance communications.** Shunting teams will operate within a standardised group call framework, with dedicated group IDs, link assurance signals, and integration with the functional addressing scheme. The shunting leader will have defined procedures for entering and leaving shunting mode.

**Common equipment standards.** All EIRENE-compliant equipment will meet common environmental, EMC, vibration, shock, and quality standards, enabling equipment from any certified manufacturer to be used on any participating national network.

**Standardised direct mode fallback.** Where direct mode is implemented, it will use standardised FM equipment on defined channels, with CTCSS squelch, a common access channel, and integration with the shunting link assurance mechanism.

---

## 5. BUSINESS RULES

The following domain-specific rules govern the behaviour of the EIRENE system. These rules are non-negotiable and must be reflected in all system design decisions.

### 5.1 Call Priority and Arbitration

**BR-01:** The system shall enforce a strict eMLPP priority hierarchy. Railway emergency calls have the highest priority; Railway operation calls have the next priority; Railway information calls (eMLPP priority 4) are the default for dialled and stored-number calls. No lower-priority call shall pre-empt a higher-priority call.

**BR-02:** All calls of Railway emergency priority shall require confirmation. The system shall automatically initiate a confirmation call after cleardown of any Railway emergency call, with a random delay to avoid network congestion. Confirmation messages shall be of eMLPP priority 4.

**BR-03:** When a Cab radio is in an ongoing call and a new call is received or initiated, the arbitration rules defined in the EIRENE call arbitration tables shall be applied. The driver shall not be required to manually resolve priority conflicts.

**BR-04:** Replacing the handset during a call shall produce defined outcomes as specified in the EIRENE call management tables. The system shall not leave calls in an undefined state as a result of handset replacement.

### 5.2 Functional Number Registration and Deregistration

**BR-05:** Functional number registration shall be performed using USSD messages over the GSM air interface, following the GSM Follow-me service protocol. Registration shall transmit the functional number together with the subscriber's IMSI.

**BR-06:** Deregistration of a functional number shall only be performed by the subscription (MSISDN) that is currently associated with that functional number. No other subscription may deregister a functional number without performing a forced de-registration sequence.

**BR-07:** Forced de-registration (where a functional number is already registered to another mobile) shall require a defined seven-step sequence: interrogation, receipt of MSISDN, forced de-registration, acknowledgement, registration, acknowledgement, and user notification. National rules may require additional steps (e.g., contacting a dispatcher) before or instead of this sequence.

**BR-08:** Deregistration of a functional number shall not be initiated until registration of the new functional number has been confirmed as successful. The system shall not leave a functional number in an unregistered state as a result of a failed registration attempt.

**BR-09:** The Engine Number to MSISDN relationship shall require manual confirmation before it can be removed. This rule exists to prevent accidental deregistration of safety-critical identifiers.

**BR-10:** On-train functional numbers based on the train number shall be automatically re-registered every time the train crosses from one EIRENE network to another.

**BR-11:** Access to functional number registration and deregistration facilities shall be controllable on a per-functional-number-type basis. National operators may restrict which users can register which types of functional numbers.

### 5.3 Network Selection and Roaming

**BR-12:** Mobile stations shall give preference to the GSM frequency band allocated for railway use (R-GSM) during network selection, in order to minimise the duration of the network selection procedure.

**BR-13:** Manual network selection shall not be available to the driver while a voice call is ongoing. If directed network selection is triggered by an external device during an ongoing call, the network change shall be deferred until the call is terminated or coverage of the current network is lost, and the driver shall be notified.

**BR-14:** The user shall be prevented from entering direct mode if GSM-R service is available. Direct mode is a fallback for use only when the GSM-R network is unavailable.

### 5.4 VGCS/VBS and Group Call Management

**BR-15:** Termination of a VGCS/VBS call by DTMF signalling shall use the three-digit sequence "***". No other sequence shall be used for this purpose.

**BR-16:** A controller wishing to start talking in a group call shall signal this by transmitting the DTMF sequence "###". A controller wishing to stop talking shall transmit "#**". These sequences shall trigger the network to send SET-PARAMETER messages to the talking mobile.

**BR-17:** If multiple controllers are talking simultaneously, the receipt of a "###" sequence from an additional controller shall not cause the network to send a duplicate SET-PARAMETER message with D-ATT=T. The SET-PARAMETER message with D-ATT=F shall only be sent when all talking controllers have transmitted "#**".

**BR-18:** DTMF tones added to the voice channel shall have a duration of 70ms ± 5ms with a minimum gap of 65ms between tones. This applies to both fixed-line dispatcher tones and controller terminal tones.

**BR-19:** Muting and unmuting for VGCS shall comply with EN 301 515, Index [4].

### 5.5 Shunting Mode

**BR-20:** On entering shunting mode, the terminal shall first check whether a shunting group ID is already activated on the SIM. If so, it shall re-establish the group call rather than initiating a new one.

**BR-21:** On entering shunting mode, the terminal shall initially select the common shunting group.

**BR-22:** Members of a dedicated shunting team shall be prevented by the terminal application from initiating new calls, unless they are the registered shunting team leader who has left shunting mode using the "maintain group call" function.

**BR-23:** Users shall not be permitted to join a dedicated shunting group unless a valid functional number for the group has been successfully registered to the GSM-R network by their terminal.

**BR-24:** The shunting leader shall have two defined options when leaving shunting mode: "Release group call" (all members deregistered) or "Maintain group call" (leader leaves temporarily, other members remain active).

### 5.6 SIM Card and Identity Management

**BR-25:** All SIM cards used in EIRENE mobiles shall comply with the MORANE FFFIS for GSM-R SIM Cards. SIM cards in Cab radios shall be physically integrated and removable only by maintenance staff. SIM cards in Operational radios shall be fixed and removable only with a tool.

**BR-26:** Each mobile shall store a list of emergency Group IDs in the SIM appropriate to its function. All mandatory emergency Group IDs shall maintain active status at all times while the mobile is powered up.

**BR-27:** The calling party's functional number shall be passed to the receiving mobile using UUS1 during call setup. If the functional number is not available, the CLI shall be displayed instead.

### 5.7 Numbering Plan

**BR-28:** All numbers in the EIRENE numbering plan (excluding access prefixes) shall not exceed 15 digits in length and shall consist entirely of the digits 0–9.

**BR-29:** Each Train Number shall be unique within each GSM-R network for the duration of the journey.

**BR-30:** Short dialling codes shall consist of exactly four digits, beginning with the digit 1 (Call Type = 1). Internationally mandated short codes shall be implemented identically in all networks.

**BR-31:** Access to public networks from the GSM-R network shall use a defined Access Code followed by the international or national number of the called subscriber, subject to call barring restrictions.

**BR-32:** Every railway network shall treat a number as a National EIRENE Number unless it is preceded by an International Code identifying another GSM-R network.

### 5.8 Quality and Compliance

**BR-33:** All design, manufacturing, testing, and installation of EIRENE mobile radio equipment shall comply with ISO 9001 quality procedures.

**BR-34:** Stricter national environmental or physical standards shall not prevent the use of EIRENE-compliant mobiles from other countries on that national network.

---

## 6. SCOPE

### 6.1 In Scope

**Network Infrastructure Requirements:**
- Definition of minimum coverage probability targets for voice, non-safety-critical data, and ETCS Level 2/3 lines.
- Frequency band allocation and ARFCN conventions for the R-GSM band and UIC frequency band.
- Call setup performance targets, including external network transit time limits.
- VGCS/VBS network-side behaviour, including DTMF handling, SET-PARAMETER messaging, and muting/unmuting.
- Network-side functional number routing database management.
- Inter-network signalling using SS7/MAP and SCCP with ITU-T E.164 Global Titles.
- Confirmation centre requirements for Railway emergency call confirmation.
- Cell broadcast message identifier support for SMS-CB.

**Mobile Equipment — Cab Radio:**
- Full specification of Cab radio hardware, including environmental, EMC, vibration, shock, power supply, and insulation requirements.
- Cab radio MMI requirements, including call initiation methods (emergency, priority, stored number, dial), call arbitration, and call termination.
- Registration, deregistration, and re-registration of functional numbers via USSD.
- Multi-party call support for multi-driver operations.
- Shunting mode support, including group call management and link assurance.
- ETCS/ERTMS interface via EURORADIO FFFIS.
- Standard data interface for computer connection.
- SMS reception, display, and storage.
- Network selection (manual, directed, and automatic).
- SIM card physical integration and security.

**Mobile Equipment — General Purpose Radio:**
- GSM terminal functionality as defined in the mobile equipment baseline specification.
- Rechargeable battery requirements (minimum 8 hours, +18°C to +25°C, defined usage cycle).
- Functional number registration via USSD.
- Car adapter kit compatibility.

**Mobile Equipment — Operational Radio:**
- GSM terminal functionality with enhanced environmental specifications for trackside use.
- IP 54 ingress protection as a minimum.
- Enhanced shock resistance (5g semi-sinusoidal, 10g exceptional, 1.0m free fall).
- Rechargeable battery requirements (minimum 8 hours, –10°C to +55°C, defined usage cycle with 60% group calls).
- Shunting mode support.
- Car adapter kit compatibility.

**Numbering Plan:**
- National EIRENE Number structure (Call Type + User Number).
- International EIRENE Number structure (International Code + National EIRENE Number).
- Train Function Numbers, Engine Function Numbers, Coach Function Numbers.
- Shunting Team, Maintenance Team, and Train Controller functional numbers.
- MSISDN allocation and structure.
- Short dialling codes (four-digit, CT=1) including internationally mandated codes.
- Service Area definition and group/broadcast call numbering.
- Breakout Codes and Access Codes for external network access.

**Supplementary Services and Data:**
- USSD message handling for functional number management.
- UUS1 for functional number transfer during call setup.
- SMS (point-to-point and cell broadcast).
- eMLPP priority enforcement.
- Closed User Group (CUG) implementation with interoperability considerations.
- Encryption using standardised ciphering algorithms.
- Location-dependent addressing (cell-based as minimum; enhanced LDA if implemented).

**Controller Equipment:**
- DTMF signalling requirements for group call management.
- Controller terminal interface to GSM-R network.

**Direct Mode (Optional but Standardised Where Implemented):**
- FM equipment conforming to ETS 300 086.
- Maximum transmit power (1 Watt), minimum sensitivity (–107 dBm).
- Defined channel plan including common access channel (876.0125 MHz).
- Simplex operation with PTT.
- CTCSS at 203.5 Hz.
- GSM-R network presence indication.
- Shunting link assurance signal.

### 6.2 Out of Scope

- The internal design of national GSM-R network infrastructure beyond the interface and performance requirements defined in this specification.
- ETCS/ERTMS on-board unit design and functionality beyond the EURORADIO FFFIS interface.
- Public GSM network design or operation.
- Train-borne recorder design beyond the interface requirement for Railway emergency call confirmation data.
- Enhanced Location Dependent Addressing (eLDA) system design beyond the compliance requirement with the eLDA FRS and IRS.
- National-specific numbering plan details beyond the internationally mandated elements.
- Billing, charging, and revenue management systems.
- Passenger information or entertainment systems.
- Trackside signalling systems other than ETCS Level 2/3 radio interface.
- Validation of ETCS Level 2/3 coverage and speed limitation values (pending first operational implementation).

---

## 7. CONSTRAINTS

### 7.1 Technical Constraints

**TC-01: GSM Standard Compliance.** The entire EIRENE system is built on the GSM standard as defined in EN 301 515 and associated GSM Technical Specifications. No proprietary air interface or protocol may be used for any mandatory function. All mandatory functions must be implementable within the GSM framework without modification to core GSM layer 3 procedures.

**TC-02: Frequency Band.** The network shall operate exclusively within the R-GSM band (876–915 / 921–960 MHz) or a defined sub-band thereof. Mobile equipment shall support the full R-GSM band. The UIC frequency band ARFCN convention (n = 955 to 973) shall be used for carriers in that sub-band.

**TC-03: External Network Transit Time.** The transit time for calls passing through networks external to GSM (e.g., ISDN connections between MSC and controller) shall not exceed 250 milliseconds. This is a hard constraint on network architecture design.

**TC-04: DTMF Tone Timing.** DTMF tones added to the voice channel shall be exactly 70ms ± 5ms in duration with a minimum inter-tone gap of 65ms. Equipment that cannot meet these timing constraints shall not be used for VGCS/VBS group call management.

**TC-05: SIM Card Standard.** All SIM cards shall comply with the MORANE FFFIS for GSM-R SIM Cards. No other SIM card format is acceptable in EIRENE mobile equipment.

**TC-06: Signalling Protocol.** Inter-network call routing shall use SS7 with MAP. No alternative signalling protocol is permitted for inter-EIRENE-network communication.

**TC-07: Direct Mode Power Limit.** Direct mode equipment shall not exceed 1 Watt transmit power. This is a regulatory constraint related to spectrum management and interference avoidance.

**TC-08: CTCSS Frequency.** Direct mode equipment shall use CTCSS at exactly 203.5 Hz. No other CTCSS frequency is permitted, as this is required for interoperability between direct mode equipment from different manufacturers.

### 7.2 Regulatory and Standards Constraints

**RC-01: EN 301 515.** The primary technical standard governing the GSM-R air interface and network behaviour. All EIRENE requirements are subordinate to or consistent with EN 301 515.

**RC-02: ITU-T E.164.** The EIRENE numbering plan shall be consistent with ITU-T E.164. International Codes shall be based on ITU-T country codes allocated by the UIC on a network-by-network basis. Numbers shall not exceed 15 digits.

**RC-03: EN 60950.** All EIRENE mobile equipment shall conform to EN 60950 (Safety of Information Technology Equipment) including amendments A1 and A2.

**RC-04: EN 50153.** Electrical safety of railway rolling stock equipment. All Cab radio and Operational radio equipment shall protect users against electrical hazards as defined in EN 50153.

**RC-05: ENV 50121.** EMC emission and immunity standards for the railway environment. All equipment shall comply with the applicable parts of ENV 50121. Where GSM transmission band emissions exceed ENV 50121 limits, EN 301 515 takes precedence.

**RC-06: prEN 50125 / prEN 50155.** Shock and vibration standards for railway equipment. All equipment shall be tested against these standards.

**RC-07: ISO 9001.** All design, manufacturing, testing, and installation shall comply with ISO 9001 quality management procedures.

**RC-08: EN 45001.** Environmental and physical tolerance testing shall be conducted at facilities accredited in accordance with EN 45001.

**RC-09: IEC 68.** Environmental test procedures shall follow IEC 68 Part 1 guidelines, with the specific test suite (Cold, Dry heat, Damp heat, Impact, Vibration, Acceleration, Corrosive atmospheres, Air pressure, Temperature change, Fire hazard) applied to all EIRENE mobile equipment.

**RC-10: prEN 50124 Part 1.** Cab radio equipment shall comply with insulation co-ordination requirements for clearances and creepages.

**RC-11: IEC 571.** Cab radio equipment shall pass electrical tests as defined in IEC 571 Parts 1, 2, and 3, and shall withstand power supply transients as defined in prEN 50155.

**RC-12: ETS 300 086.** Direct mode equipment shall use frequency-modulated equipment conforming to ETS 300 086.

**RC-13: IEC 529 / EN 60529.** Operational radios shall conform to IP 54 as a minimum ingress protection rating.

**RC-14: IEC 721 Part 1.** Operational radios shall be capable of withstanding extreme environmental conditions as defined in IEC 721 Part 1.

**RC-15: MORANE EURO FFFIS.** Where ERTMS/ETCS communications are required, the EURORADIO interface shall be implemented as defined in the MORANE EURO FFFIS.

**RC-16: eLDA FRS and IRS.** Where enhanced location-dependent addressing is implemented using train-based position information, the implementation shall comply with the eLDA FRS and IRS.

### 7.3 Operational Constraints

**OC-01: Backward Compatibility with GSM.** The EIRENE system shall not require modifications to core GSM layer 3 procedures. All EIRENE-specific functionality shall be implemented within the existing GSM framework.

**OC-02: National Sovereignty.** National operators retain the right to apply stricter environmental and physical standards than those defined in this specification, provided that such stricter standards do not prevent the use of EIRENE-compliant equipment from other countries on their network.

**OC-03: Bilateral Agreements at Network Boundaries.** Service Area allocation in network boundary areas shall be determined by bilateral agreement between the adjacent national operators. This constraint means that boundary area numbering cannot be defined unilaterally.

**OC-04: ETCS Coverage Validation.** The coverage and speed limitation values for ETCS Level 2/3 are subject to validation and potential revision after the first operational implementation of ETCS. Equipment and network designs shall be capable of accommodating revised values without fundamental redesign.

**OC-05: DTX/DRX Prohibition for Battery Testing.** Battery life requirements shall be measured without the use of discontinuous reception or transmission (DTX/DRX). Battery performance claims based on DTX/DRX are not acceptable.

---

## 8. ASSUMPTIONS & DEPENDENCIES

### 8.1 Assumptions

**A-01: GSM Infrastructure Availability.** It is assumed that participating national railways will deploy GSM-R network infrastructure (BTS, BSC, MSC, HLR, GMSC) meeting the design criteria defined in this specification. The EIRENE mobile equipment specification assumes a compliant network; mobile equipment performance cannot be guaranteed on non-compliant networks.

**A-02: UIC Frequency Allocation.** It is assumed that the UIC has allocated or will allocate the R-GSM frequency band for railway use in all participating countries, and that national spectrum regulators have licensed this band for railway radio use.

**A-03: International Code Allocation.** It is assumed that the UIC will allocate International Codes (based on ITU-T country codes) to each participating GSM-R network on a network-by-network basis, and that these allocations will be maintained in a central registry accessible to all network operators.

**A-04: MORANE Standards Availability.** It is assumed that the MORANE FFFIS for GSM-R SIM Cards, the MORANE EURO FFFIS (EURORADIO), and the MORANE SM FFFIS (shunting mode) are published, stable, and available to equipment manufacturers and network operators.

**A-05: EN 301 515 Stability.** It is assumed that EN 301 515 is the definitive technical standard for GSM-R and that it will remain stable (or that EIRENE requirements will be updated to track any revisions) throughout the implementation period.

**A-06: Bilateral Network Boundary Agreements.** It is assumed that adjacent national railway operators will negotiate and agree on Service Area allocations and group call configurations in network boundary areas prior to the commencement of cross-border operations.

**A-07: Confirmation Centre Deployment.** It is assumed that each GSM-R network will deploy and operate a confirmation centre capable of receiving and decoding Railway emergency call confirmation messages in both defined formats (A and B).

**A-08: Train-Borne Recorder Interface.** Where a train-borne recorder is connected to the Cab radio, it is assumed that the recorder provides a compatible interface for receiving Railway emergency call confirmation data. The design of the recorder itself is outside the scope of this specification.

**A-09: ETCS On-Board Unit Availability.** Where ERTMS/ETCS communications are required, it is assumed that the ETCS on-board unit is present and provides the EURORADIO interface as defined in the MORANE EURO FFFIS.

**A-10: Driver Training.** It is assumed that train drivers will receive appropriate training in the use of the Cab radio MMI, including emergency call procedures, registration/deregistration, and shunting mode. The specification defines the MMI behaviour but does not define the training programme.

**A-11: National Numbering Plan Coordination.** It is assumed that national railway operators will coordinate their national EIRENE numbering plans to avoid conflicts, particularly in the allocation of Location Numbers for controllers, shunting teams, and maintenance teams.

### 8.2 Dependencies

**D-01: EN 301 515.** The EIRENE system requirements are directly dependent on EN 301 515 for the definition of GSM-R air interface parameters, supplementary service specifications (VGCS, VBS, eMLPP, UUS, USSD), and equipment performance requirements. Any revision to EN 301 515 may require corresponding updates to EIRENE requirements.

**D-02: EIRENE Functional Requirements Specification (FRS).** The EIRENE SRS is dependent on the EIRENE FRS for the definition of call setup time targets, link assurance tone specifications, and shunting mode functional requirements. The FRS must be completed and approved before the SRS can be finalised.

**D-03: MORANE FFFIS Documents.** Equipment manufacturers are dependent on the MORANE FFFIS for GSM-R SIM Cards, MORANE EURO FFFIS, and MORANE SM FFFIS for detailed interface specifications. These documents must be available and stable before equipment development can commence.

**D-04: eLDA FRS and IRS.** Where enhanced location-dependent addressing is implemented, the system is dependent on the eLDA FRS and IRS for the specification of the interface between train-based position systems and the GSM-R network.

**D-05: ITU-T E.164 and Country Code Allocations.** The EIRENE international numbering plan is dependent on ITU-T E.164 country code allocations and the UIC's assignment of International Codes to individual GSM-R networks.

**D-06: National Spectrum Licensing.** Network deployment is dependent on national spectrum regulators licensing the R-GSM band for railway use in each participating country. Delays in spectrum licensing will delay network deployment.

**D-07: GSM-R Network Deployment.** Mobile equipment performance is dependent on the deployment of a compliant GSM-R network. Mobile equipment cannot be fully validated without a compliant network against which to test.

**D-08: ETCS Operational Implementation.** The validation of coverage and speed limitation values for ETCS Level 2/3 is dependent on the first operational implementation of ETCS. Until this validation is complete, the coverage values specified in this document are provisional and subject to revision.

**D-09: ISO 9001 Certification of Manufacturers.** Equipment manufacturers are required to hold ISO 9001 certification. Procurement processes must verify this certification before awarding contracts for EIRENE mobile equipment.

**D-10: EN 45001 Accredited Test Facilities.** Environmental and physical testing is dependent on the availability of test facilities accredited in accordance with EN 45001. National operators must identify and engage accredited facilities before equipment certification can commence.

---

*End of Business Requirements Document*

*This document is intended to provide sufficient business context for requirements engineers to elicit, validate, and structure the detailed system requirements for the EIRENE GSM-R railway radio system. It should be read in conjunction with the EIRENE Functional Requirements Specification and the referenced standards documents.*

ground_truth_requirements.text: 1) Call Type (CT) This prefix defines how to interpret the User Number that follows. It shall consist of one or two digits as defined in table 9-1. 2) User Number (UN) The UN is variable in length and depends on the information on which it is based. The following formats are defined: Train Function Numbers (TFN) shall take one of the forms as defined in table 9-2. Leading zeros shall be used in those situations where the train number is less than five digits. Engine Function Numbers (EFN) shall take the format as defined in table 9-3. Coach Function Numbers (CFN) shall take the format as defined in table 9-4. Maintenance and Shunting Team Members. The UIN field format for calls to shunting teams and maintenance teams shall consist of a Location Number (LN), which identifies the location where the called party is registered, followed by a Function Code (FC). The Location Number shall consist of 5 digits and shall be assigned on a national basis. The Function Code shall consist of 4 digits. The first digit of the FC is related to the Team Type (TT) and is specified by table 9-5. The composition of the Function Code field for calls to shunting teams and maintenance teams shall be as defined in table 9-6. Train Controllers. The UIN field for calls to train controllers shall be a Location Number (LN) which identifies the location where the called party is registered. The Location Number shall consist of 5 digits and shall be assigned on a national basis. The FC field for calls to train controllers shall consist of two digits and be as defined in table 9-7. Mobile Subscriber Number (MSN). The UN of the MSN shall consist of the Subscriber Number. Group and broadcast calls. The UIN field format for group calls shall be a Service Area (SA) indicator, which identifies the area in which the group call is to be active. Each Service Area shall be allocated on a national basis. In network boundary areas, the Service Area shall be allocated on a bilateral basis. (See also subsection 9.9.) The FC field format for group calls shall consist of three digits and be as defined in table 9-8.
ground_truth_requirements.label: FR

ground_truth_requirements.text: GSM-R networks shall recognise International EIRENE Numbers starting with the IC of the GSM-R network in which the calling party is currently operating as National EIRENE Numbers. (M) GSM-R networks shall recognise International EIRENE Numbers starting with the IC of the GSM-R network in which the calling party is currently operating as National EIRENE Numbers. (M)
ground_truth_requirements.label: FR

ground_truth_requirements.text: The International EIRENE Number shall consist of three distinct parts, as shown in figure 9-2: (M) The International EIRENE Number shall consist of three distinct parts, as shown in figure 9-2: (M)
ground_truth_requirements.label: FR
