# Business Requirements Document (BRD)

## EIRENE Functional Requirements Specification — Railway Radio Communication System
### Project ID: 2007-eirene_fun_7-2
### Document Status: Pre-Requirements Elicitation Draft
### Prepared by: Business Analysis Team — UIC EIRENE Programme

---

## 1. PROBLEM STATEMENT

European railways operate across national borders with fundamentally incompatible radio communication systems. Train drivers, controllers, shunting teams, and trackside maintenance personnel currently rely on a patchwork of national analogue and early digital radio technologies that cannot interoperate when trains cross international boundaries. This creates a series of measurable operational and safety problems:

**Safety-Critical Communication Failures.** When a train crosses from one national network to another, the driver may lose contact with the controlling authority for a period that can extend to several minutes. There is no standardised mechanism for a driver to initiate a railway emergency call that is guaranteed to reach all relevant drivers and controllers in the affected area across network boundaries. The absence of a defined call priority hierarchy means that emergency communications can be blocked by lower-priority calls already occupying the channel.

**Operational Inefficiency.** Controllers cannot reliably address a train by its operational train number; they must know the physical telephone number of the cab radio installed in a specific locomotive. Because rolling stock is reassigned between services, this creates a significant administrative burden and introduces the risk of misdirected calls. There is no standardised functional addressing scheme that maps a train number to the correct cab radio regardless of which locomotive is hauling the service.

**Shunting Safety Risk.** During pushing manoeuvres, the driver cannot see the way ahead and depends entirely on radio contact with a shunting team member. Existing systems provide no standardised link assurance mechanism to confirm that the radio channel remains intact during silent periods, nor any guarantee that the shunting team member is not incapacitated. The absence of a regulated shunting group membership scheme means unauthorised parties can join shunting communications.

**Absence of Train Control Data Bearer.** The emerging European Train Management System (ERTMS/ETCS) at Levels 2 and 3 requires a reliable, standardised data communications bearer between the on-board ETCS unit and the Radio Block Centre (RBC) on the ground. No such standardised bearer exists across European networks, blocking the deployment of ERTMS and the associated improvements in line capacity and safety.

**Inconsistent User Equipment.** There is no common specification for cab radio man-machine interfaces, meaning that a driver trained on one national network faces an unfamiliar interface when operating internationally. Emergency call buttons, loudspeaker controls, and display formats differ between manufacturers and countries, increasing the risk of operator error in safety-critical situations.

**Quantified Pain Points:**
- International train services may experience communication blackouts of several minutes at border crossings.
- Emergency call set-up times on existing systems are not guaranteed; there is no contractual performance standard.
- Functional addressing failures require controllers to maintain manual cross-reference tables between train numbers and radio telephone numbers, a process prone to error and delay.
- Shunting incidents attributable to loss of radio contact have been recorded across multiple European networks.

---

## 2. BUSINESS OBJECTIVES & SUCCESS CRITERIA

The EIRENE system must achieve the following business objectives, each accompanied by measurable success criteria derived from operational requirements.

### Objective 1: Guarantee Safety-Critical Communication Performance
The system must ensure that railway emergency communications are established within a time frame that allows trains to stop before reaching a hazard.

**Success Criteria:**
- Railway emergency calls shall be connected (audible indication commenced) within 2 seconds in 95% of cases, and within 3 seconds (1.5× the target) in 99% of cases.
- Group calls between drivers in the same area shall be set up within 5 seconds in 95% of cases.
- All operational mobile-to-fixed calls shall be set up within 5 seconds (95th percentile).
- All operational fixed-to-mobile calls shall be set up within 7 seconds (95th percentile).
- All operational mobile-to-mobile calls shall be set up within 10 seconds (95th percentile).
- Emergency call confirmation messages shall be received at the central ground location with 99% probability of success within 5 minutes of call termination.
- The system shall automatically retry a failed emergency call connection for 30 seconds before reporting failure.

### Objective 2: Enable Seamless International Interoperability
Drivers and other railway personnel shall be able to operate across national network boundaries without manual reconfiguration and without loss of essential services.

**Success Criteria:**
- Functional number re-registration after network change shall be automatic, requiring zero manual driver intervention.
- Up to ten functional numbers shall be re-registered within 30 seconds of network selection.
- The system shall support all EIRENE-compliant mobile equipment regardless of the national network being used.
- Radio coverage shall be maintained at a minimum of 95% of the time over 95% of the designated coverage area for vehicle-mounted equipment with an external antenna.

### Objective 3: Provide a Reliable Data Bearer for ERTMS/ETCS
The system shall support the data communications requirements of ERTMS/ETCS Levels 2 and 3, enabling deployment of the European Train Management System.

**Success Criteria:**
- The network shall support a minimum data rate of 2.4 kbit/s for all data applications.
- The system shall be capable of supporting simultaneous data communications for a high density of trains in a given area.
- The on-board radio shall provide a standardised interface to ERTMS/ETCS equipment.

### Objective 4: Standardise the Driver Man-Machine Interface
A common MMI specification shall reduce driver error and training costs across European networks.

**Success Criteria:**
- All cab radios shall implement the standard audible and visual indications specified in the CENELEC Standard for the Driver-Machine Interface for ERTMS.
- The emergency call button shall be red and protected against accidental activation on all radio types that support railway emergency calls.
- Displayed characters shall have a minimum height of 5 mm and be readable from a normal driving position.
- The radio shall support a minimum of ten display languages.

### Objective 5: Ensure Shunting Safety Through Link Assurance
The system shall provide a standardised mechanism to confirm radio link integrity during shunting operations.

**Success Criteria:**
- The link assurance signal (800–850 Hz, 1 second on / 2 seconds off) shall be audible to all shunting group members.
- The shunting emergency call shall interrupt the link assurance signal and be receivable by all shunting group members at any time.
- Shunting group membership shall be controlled to prevent unauthorised access.

### Objective 6: Support Post-Incident Analysis
The system shall provide an auditable record of railway emergency call events.

**Success Criteria:**
- Automatic confirmation messages for both originating and receiving mobiles shall be transmitted to a central ground location within 5 minutes of call termination with 99% reliability.
- If network contact is lost, the confirmation mechanism shall resume as soon as contact is regained, for up to a maximum of 5 minutes of attempted contact.
- Train-borne recorders shall receive details of emergency call activation, termination, and failures in real time.

---

## 3. STAKEHOLDERS

### 3.1 Train Drivers (Primary End Users — Cab Radio)
Train drivers are the primary safety-critical users of the system. Their core need is to be able to communicate with controllers and other drivers quickly and reliably, to initiate and receive railway emergency calls, and to operate the radio with minimal distraction from the primary task of driving. Drivers operating internationally require the system to handle network transitions automatically and to present information in their preferred language. Drivers also need to register their train number at the start of a journey and deregister at the end, and to communicate with on-board staff and other drivers on the same train.

### 3.2 Train Controllers (Primary Controllers, Secondary Controllers, Power Supply Controllers)
Controllers are ground-based operational staff responsible for managing train movements within a defined geographical area. Their primary need is to be able to initiate and receive calls to and from any train within their control area using the train's functional number, to manage railway emergency calls, and to monitor and queue incoming calls by priority. Controllers require the ability to establish group and broadcast calls to all drivers in an area, and to record operational speech and data calls for post-incident analysis. Different controller types (primary, secondary, power supply) have overlapping but distinct addressing and communication needs.

### 3.3 Shunting Team Members and Shunting Leaders
Shunting personnel operate in marshalling yards and stations, often in situations where the driver cannot see the way ahead. Their primary need is a regulated group communication channel with link assurance, protection against unauthorised access, and the ability to initiate a shunting emergency call at any time. The shunting leader has additional responsibilities for managing group membership and communicating with parties external to the shunting group.

### 3.4 Trackside Maintenance Personnel
Maintenance teams working on or near the track require portable radio equipment that is rugged, operable with gloves, and capable of receiving railway emergency calls. Their primary need is reliable communication with controllers and the ability to participate in group calls relevant to their work area.

### 3.5 General Railway Personnel (General Purpose Radio Users)
This group includes conductors, catering staff, station staff, and other railway employees who require general voice and data communication services. Their needs are less safety-critical than those of drivers and shunting teams, but they require access to group calls, text messaging, and the ability to receive railway emergency calls where authorised.

### 3.6 National Railway Operators (Infrastructure Managers and Train Operating Companies)
Railway operators are responsible for deploying and managing EIRENE networks within their national territories. Their primary need is a system that meets the UIC minimum service requirements, supports interoperability with other national networks, and can be configured to meet national operational requirements (e.g. call restrictions, group area definitions, language settings). They are also responsible for ensuring that EIRENE equipment does not interfere with other on-train or ground-based systems.

### 3.7 Network Managers and System Administrators
These stakeholders are responsible for configuring and maintaining the EIRENE network, including managing functional number registrations, defining group call areas, setting call priorities, configuring call barring and forwarding rules, and managing the closed user group. Their primary need is a flexible, remotely configurable network management capability.

### 3.8 ERTMS/ETCS System Integrators
These are technical teams responsible for integrating the EIRENE radio system with ERTMS/ETCS on-board and ground-based equipment. Their primary need is a standardised, well-defined interface between the cab radio and the ETCS unit, and a reliable data bearer for RBC communications.

### 3.9 Rolling Stock Manufacturers and Radio Equipment Manufacturers
These stakeholders design and build the physical radio equipment and its integration into rolling stock. Their primary need is a clear, unambiguous specification of environmental, physical, electrical, and EMC requirements, as well as the MMI standards that must be met.

### 3.10 Safety and Regulatory Authorities
National and European safety authorities require evidence that the system meets the safety requirements for railway communications, including emergency call performance, link assurance, and post-incident recording. Their primary need is auditability, traceability of requirements, and compliance with relevant European Norms and CENELEC standards.

### 3.11 Train-Borne Recorder Systems (Automated System Stakeholder)
The train-borne recorder is an automated system that receives event data from the cab radio for post-incident analysis. Its interface requirements must be standardised to ensure that emergency call events, driver safety device alarms, and radio faults are captured reliably.

---

## 4. CURRENT STATE vs FUTURE STATE

### 4.1 Voice Communications

**Current State:** National railway radio systems (predominantly analogue or early digital) support basic point-to-point voice calls within a single national network. There is no standardised mechanism for group calls, broadcast calls, or multi-party calls that works across national boundaries. Emergency calls are handled differently in each country, with no guaranteed set-up time, no standardised audible/visual indication, and no automatic confirmation mechanism. Drivers crossing international borders must manually switch to a different radio system or lose communication entirely.

**Future State:** The EIRENE system will provide a standardised suite of voice services — point-to-point, broadcast, group, multi-party, public emergency, and railway emergency calls — that operate consistently across all UIC standard networks. Railway emergency calls will be set up within 2 seconds, will automatically reach all relevant drivers and controllers in a pre-defined area, and will generate automatic confirmation messages for post-incident analysis. Drivers will experience seamless network transitions at international borders with automatic functional number re-registration.

### 4.2 Functional Addressing

**Current State:** Controllers must maintain manual cross-reference tables between operational train numbers and the physical telephone numbers of cab radios installed in specific locomotives. When rolling stock is reassigned, these tables become incorrect, leading to misdirected calls and communication delays. There is no mechanism for a controller to call a train by its train number and have the call automatically routed to the correct cab radio regardless of which locomotive is in use.

**Future State:** The EIRENE functional addressing scheme will allow controllers to call any train by its operational train number. The network will automatically translate the functional number to the current telephone number of the cab radio registered to that train. Up to three functional numbers can be assigned to a single user simultaneously. Functional numbers will remain valid as users roam between networks. Duplicate train number registrations will be detected and flagged, with a controlled override mechanism.

### 4.3 Data Services and ERTMS Support

**Current State:** There is no standardised data bearer for ERTMS/ETCS Level 2 and 3 communications across European networks. Each national deployment uses proprietary or interim solutions, preventing the pan-European deployment of ERTMS and the associated improvements in line capacity and safety.

**Future State:** The EIRENE network will provide a standardised data bearer supporting a minimum of 2.4 kbit/s, capable of supporting ERTMS/ETCS data communications for a high density of simultaneous train connections. The cab radio will provide a standardised interface to the ETCS on-board unit. Text messaging, general data applications, and optional fax services will also be supported.

### 4.4 Shunting Communications

**Current State:** Shunting communications rely on national systems with no standardised link assurance mechanism. There is no regulated group membership scheme, meaning that unauthorised parties can join shunting communications. During pushing manoeuvres, the driver has no reliable means of confirming that the radio link to the shunting team member remains intact during silent periods.

**Future State:** The EIRENE shunting mode will provide a regulated group call with a standardised link assurance signal (800–850 Hz tone, 1 second on / 2 seconds off) that confirms radio link integrity during silent periods. Group membership will be controlled by the shunting leader. A shunting emergency call will be available to all group members at any time and will automatically interrupt the link assurance signal. The system will support two configurable link assurance operating modes to meet the requirements of different national railways.

### 4.5 Man-Machine Interface

**Current State:** Cab radio MMIs differ significantly between manufacturers and national networks. Emergency call buttons are not standardised in colour, position, or protection against accidental activation. Display formats, language support, and audible/visual indication schemes are inconsistent, increasing the risk of driver error when operating internationally.

**Future State:** All EIRENE cab radios will implement a standardised MMI complying with the CENELEC Standard for the Driver-Machine Interface for ERTMS. The emergency call button will be red and protected against accidental use on all radio types. Displays will support a minimum of ten languages, with a minimum character height of 5 mm. Brightness and contrast will be adjustable. The MMI will be designed to comply with UIC fiche 651 for cab equipment layout.

### 4.6 Network Selection and Roaming

**Current State:** When a train crosses an international border, the driver must manually intervene to select the correct national network, often while managing other operational tasks. There is no standardised procedure for this transition, and the risk of the radio attaching to an incorrect (e.g. public) network is significant.

**Future State:** The EIRENE system will support three network selection modes: manual (suitable for international border crossings), directed (triggered by an external device such as a balise, also suitable for border crossings), and automatic (suitable for domestic use where unpredictable foreign network attachment is not a risk). Automatic network selection will be explicitly prohibited for international border crossings. The driver will always be informed by audible and visual indication when a network change occurs without user intervention.

---

## 5. BUSINESS RULES

### 5.1 Call Priority and Pre-emption

The system shall enforce a strict five-level call priority hierarchy. In descending order of priority: Railway Emergency, Control-Command (Safety), Public Emergency and Group Calls Between Drivers in the Same Area, Railway Operation, and Railway Information. A higher-priority call shall always be able to pre-empt a lower-priority call. When pre-emption occurs, the lowest-priority ongoing call shall be terminated first. Railway emergency calls and control-command calls shall be answered automatically on voice channels. Railway operation calls shall be answered automatically on cab radios (mandatory) and optionally on other equipment. Railway information calls shall not be answered automatically.

### 5.2 Closed User Group

Access to the EIRENE network shall be restricted to authorised users only. Any user not on the authorised EIRENE user list shall be denied access to all network functions and services. The access matrix (defining which subscriber types may call which other subscriber types) shall be enforced by the network. Railways may apply additional restrictions to the access matrix but may not relax the mandatory permissions defined in the EIRENE specification.

### 5.3 Functional Number Uniqueness and Registration

Only one EIRENE user may be assigned to a given functional number at any one time. Duplicate registrations shall be detected and the registering user warned. A controlled override mechanism shall be available to the newly registering user, with notification to the previously registered user. Functional numbers shall consist of numeric characters only. Up to three functional numbers may be assigned to a single user simultaneously. Functional numbers shall remain valid as the user roams between networks.

### 5.4 Railway Emergency Call Initiation and Termination

A railway emergency call may only be initiated by a single MMI action (e.g. pressing the red emergency button). The type of emergency call (train emergency or shunting emergency) shall be determined automatically based on the current operating mode of the radio. A railway emergency call may only be terminated by the originator, a participating controller, or the network after a nationally determined period of no speech. Drivers shall not be able to terminate a railway emergency call that was not initiated by them. The emergency call button shall be red and protected against accidental activation on all radio types.

### 5.5 Shunting Group Membership and Link Assurance

Only the shunting leader may communicate with parties external to the shunting group. Non-leader shunting group members who attempt to make calls outside the group shall have those attempts ignored. Only one member of the shunting group may transmit the link assurance signal at any time. The driver shall not be able to transmit the link assurance signal. The link assurance signal shall be automatically deactivated upon receipt of a shunting emergency call. Shunting group membership shall be protected against unintentional and unauthorised access.

### 5.6 Network Selection Rules

Automatic network selection shall not be used for international border crossings. The priority order for network selection shall always be: home EIRENE network first, then foreign EIRENE networks, then public networks. Manual network selection and directed network selection are the only permitted methods for crossing international borders. If directed network selection is triggered while a voice call is in progress, the network change shall be deferred until the call is terminated or coverage of the current network is lost.

### 5.7 Emergency Group Subscription

All mobiles with a railway emergency group call subscription shall be prevented from deactivating the emergency group while operational. Cab radios shall be members of the following standard groups as a minimum: Railway Emergency Call, All Train Drivers, and Shunting Team.

### 5.8 Call Barring and Restrictions

Call barring rules shall be configurable by network management for individual users, covering both outgoing calls (by destination network type, number type, or specific number) and incoming calls (by originating network type, number type, or specific number). Any implementation of call restrictions shall not affect international interoperability.

### 5.9 Train Number Registration

The driver's functional number shall be automatically registered and deregistered when the train number is registered and deregistered. Where ERTMS/ETCS and radio systems are both available, the train number shall be entered only once (on either system) and transmitted to the other. A fixed relationship shall be maintained between the telephone numbers of radios installed in a locomotive or coach and the stock number, even if the radio unit is replaced.

### 5.10 Text Message Non-Interference

The text message facility shall not interfere with the ability of users to make or receive high-priority voice or data calls. Where fax functionality is provided, it shall be possible to interrupt the fax to make or receive a high-priority voice or data call.

### 5.11 Emergency Confirmation Reporting

Automatic confirmation of railway emergency call receipt shall be generated without any user input. The confirmation shall commence at the end of the call or when the radio moves out of the call area. If network contact is lost, the confirmation shall be sent as soon as contact is regained, for up to a maximum of 5 minutes. The confirmation message shall be received at the central ground location with 99% probability within 5 minutes of call termination.

### 5.12 Quality and Environmental Compliance

All design, testing, and installation of EIRENE mobile radio equipment shall comply with ISO 9001 quality procedures. National environmental standards may supersede EIRENE environmental requirements only where they provide a higher level of protection, and stricter national standards shall not prevent the use of other EIRENE-compliant mobiles in that country.

---

## 6. SCOPE

### 6.1 In Scope

**Network Services:**
- Point-to-point voice calls between any combination of fixed and mobile users, with full duplex capability.
- Public emergency voice calls (including 112 calls).
- Broadcast voice calls (one-way, from a single user to a pre-defined group in a local area).
- Group voice calls (multi-user, within a pre-defined local area, with PTT arbitration).
- Multi-party voice calls supporting up to six simultaneous parties.
- Point-to-point data communications at a minimum of 2.4 kbit/s.
- Text messaging (point-to-point and point-to-multipoint, ground-to-mobile and mobile-to-ground).
- General data applications (timetable information, maintenance diagnostics, e-mail, remote database access).
- Optional automatic fax between ground and mobile users.
- Data bearer for ERTMS/ETCS Level 2 and 3 train control applications.

**Call-Related Services:**
- Display of calling and called party identity (telephone number and functional description).
- Optional restriction of identity display.
- Call priority and pre-emption (five-level hierarchy).
- Closed user group enforcement.
- Call forwarding (unconditional mandatory; busy mandatory; no reply and not reachable optional).
- Call hold and re-join.
- Call waiting notification.
- Optional charging information display.
- Call barring (configurable by network management).

**Railway-Specific Services:**
- Functional addressing with registration, deregistration, and automatic re-registration on network change.
- Location-dependent addressing for routing calls to the appropriate controller based on mobile location.
- Shunting mode with link assurance signal and regulated group membership.
- Railway emergency calls (train emergency and shunting emergency), including automatic confirmation.
- Optional direct mode (off-network, short-range communications).
- Driver safety device (DSD) interface and alarm transmission.
- Train-borne recorder interface.
- ERTMS/ETCS standardised interface.

**Mobile Equipment Types:**
- Cab radio (train-mounted, for drivers and ERTMS/ETCS integration).
- General purpose radio (handheld/portable, for general railway personnel).
- Operational radio (handheld/portable, for shunting and trackside maintenance personnel).

**Controller Equipment:**
- Primary controller MMI (call queuing, priority display, group call management, text messaging).
- Secondary and power supply controller MMIs.

**Network Management:**
- Functional number management (registration, deregistration, override).
- Group call area configuration.
- Call barring and restriction configuration.
- Subscription profile management.
- Access matrix configuration.

**Numbering and Addressing:**
- Standardised international numbering plan for key functions (emergency calls, controller routing, ERTMS/ETCS RBC routing, public emergency).
- Functional addressing scheme (train numbers, engine/coach numbers, shunting team numbers, maintenance team numbers, controller numbers).
- Group call service area configuration.

### 6.2 Out of Scope

- Detailed specification of the interface between controller equipment and the EIRENE network (this is at the discretion of the railway operator).
- The communications architecture between controller equipment and the fixed railway network.
- Public emergency call handling procedures (112 calls are supported as a bearer but the handling procedures are outside EIRENE scope).
- Nationally specific pre-defined messaging applications (these are to be specified as part of individual national procurements).
- Alphanumeric train number support for international interoperability (national railways are responsible for translation to numeric functional numbers).
- Detailed specification of ERTMS/ETCS communications requirements (covered in the separate Summary of ERTMS Communications Requirements document).
- The content and format of safety-related messages (safety mechanisms are at the application level, not the EIRENE bearer level).
- Specification of the communications architecture between the EIRENE network and external public networks (subject to bilateral agreements between operators).
- National-specific variants of the MMI beyond the mandatory CENELEC standard requirements.

---

## 7. CONSTRAINTS

### 7.1 Technical Constraints

**Frequency Bands:** All EIRENE mobile equipment shall operate in the frequency bands around 900 MHz allocated for railway use and in public GSM networks. Equipment shall not be designed for other frequency bands.

**Speed of Operation:** The land-based network shall provide communications for mobiles when stationary and when travelling at the maximum allowable line speed. Mobile equipment shall function correctly across the full operational speed range of the railway.

**Data Rate Floor:** The network shall support a minimum data rate of 2.4 kbit/s. Higher data rates are desirable for some applications but the minimum floor is non-negotiable.

**Coverage Standard:** Network coverage shall achieve at least 95% of the time over 95% of the designated coverage area for vehicle-mounted equipment with an external antenna. This is a planning target, not a guaranteed minimum at every point.

**Pressure Pulse Resilience:** Equipment installed in rolling stock shall not be affected by pressure pulses caused by vehicles passing through, entering, or leaving tunnels.

**EMC:** EIRENE equipment shall not interfere with signalling relays, track circuits, axle counters, train describers, radar speed measurement equipment, switched mode power supplies, telecommunications circuits, or electronic locking systems. EMC requirements for cab radios are more stringent than for other radio types due to proximity to safety-critical train systems.

**Battery Life:** Cab radios shall operate for a minimum of 1 hour on battery backup following train power failure (20% point-to-point, 5% group, 75% standby). General purpose and operational radios shall provide a minimum of 8 hours from a single charge (20% point-to-point, 5% or 60% group respectively, remainder standby).

**Display:** Displayed characters shall have a minimum height of 5 mm. Operational radio displays shall support at least 6 lines of 20 characters.

**Weight:** General purpose radios including battery shall not exceed 250 g. Operational radios including battery shall not exceed 800 g.

**Link Assurance Signal:** The link assurance tone shall be between 800 and 850 Hz, with a 1-second tone followed by a 2-second interval. This specification is fixed and not configurable.

### 7.2 Regulatory and Standards Constraints

**CENELEC Standard:** The driver MMI shall comply with the CENELEC Standard for the Driver-Machine Interface for ERTMS, including all mandatory audible and visual indications.

**UIC Fiche 651:** Cab radio equipment design shall make it possible to install within a cab complying with UIC fiche 651 (layout of cab equipment). Radio equipment shall not obstruct the driver's vision or hinder safe driving.

**UIC Fiche 641:** Where a driver safety device interface is implemented, it shall be capable of connection to a device complying with UIC fiche 641 (automatic vigilance devices for international traffic).

**ISO 9001:** All design, testing, and installation of EIRENE mobile radio equipment shall comply with ISO 9001 quality procedures.

**European Norms:** Equipment design shall comply with relevant European Norms for protection against physical hazards including pollution, chemical contaminants, flammable substances, biologically active substances, dust, sand, mould growth, and other environmental hazards.

**Network Interconnection:** Interconnection between national EIRENE networks shall comply with applicable open specifications. Interconnection is subject to bilateral agreement between network operators.

**International Border Crossing:** Automatic network selection shall not be used for international border crossings. Only manual or directed network selection is permitted at borders.

### 7.3 Operational Constraints

**Minimum Service Set:** The services defined in the EIRENE specification represent the minimum set for implementation within each UIC standard network. Railways may implement additional services but may not implement fewer than the mandatory minimum.

**National Customisation Limits:** National railways may apply stricter environmental or physical standards than those defined in EIRENE, but stricter national standards shall not prevent the use of EIRENE-compliant mobiles from other countries in that national network.

**Human-Machine Interface Consistency:** Where mandatory MMI requirements are not fully defined in the EIRENE specification, national solutions are permitted, but they must not conflict with the CENELEC Standard, particularly for actions required from drivers of trains crossing international borders.

**Shunting Mode Restrictions:** The functionality to enter or leave shunting mode shall not be available while there are ongoing calls involving the radio. Similarly, manual network selection shall not be available during calls.

**Emergency Group Deactivation:** Mobiles with railway emergency group subscriptions shall be prevented from deactivating those subscriptions while operational. This is a hard constraint that cannot be overridden by the user.

**Call Set-Up Time Measurement:** Call set-up times shall include the time required for any translation of functional numbers internal to the EIRENE network. Performance targets apply end-to-end.

---

## 8. ASSUMPTIONS & DEPENDENCIES

### 8.1 Assumptions

**GSM-R as the Underlying Technology:** It is assumed that the EIRENE system will be implemented on a GSM-R (Global System for Mobile Communications — Railway) network infrastructure. The frequency band, call types (VBS, VGCS), and network architecture referenced throughout the requirements are consistent with GSM-R. The specification does not mandate a specific technology but is written with GSM-R as the assumed bearer.

**ERTMS/ETCS Deployment:** It is assumed that ERTMS/ETCS Level 2 or Level 3 will be deployed on lines where the train control data bearer requirements apply. The EIRENE system is not required to support ERTMS/ETCS data communications on lines where only Level 1 (balise-based) ETCS is deployed.

**Bilateral Network Agreements:** It is assumed that bilateral agreements between national railway network operators will be in place to govern network interconnection, roaming arrangements, and the allocation of group call areas at network boundaries. The EIRENE specification does not itself establish these agreements.

**National Numbering Plan Coordination:** It is assumed that national railways will coordinate their numbering plans to ensure that functional numbers are unique across all EIRENE networks, including implementations on public networks. The EIRENE specification defines the structure of functional numbers but does not assign specific number ranges.

**Controller Equipment Procurement:** It is assumed that controller terminal equipment will be procured separately by each national railway and will be designed to interface with the EIRENE network. The detailed specification of controller equipment is outside the scope of EIRENE and is the responsibility of the railway operator.

**Train-Borne Recorder Availability:** It is assumed that train-borne recorders compliant with the standardised interface defined in the EIRENE specification will be available on rolling stock where emergency call recording is required. The EIRENE system provides the interface; the recorder itself is a separate system.

**Driver Safety Device Availability:** It is assumed that traction units equipped with a driver safety device (DSD) will have a DSD compliant with UIC fiche 641. The EIRENE cab radio provides the interface to this device; the DSD itself is outside the scope of EIRENE.

**Maintenance and Configuration Access:** It is assumed that network management and maintenance functions (including call barring configuration, group area definition, and shunting mode switching) will be accessible only to authorised personnel and will not be available to general users.

**Language Content:** It is assumed that the specific languages to be supported by each national railway will be determined during national procurement. The EIRENE specification mandates support for a minimum of ten languages but does not specify which languages must be included.

**Public Network Roaming Agreements:** Where EIRENE mobiles are permitted to use public GSM networks as a fallback, it is assumed that appropriate roaming agreements will be in place between the railway network operator and the public network operator. The ability to pre-empt public network calls for railway emergency purposes may be subject to special agreements that are outside the scope of EIRENE.

### 8.2 Dependencies

**CENELEC Standard for Driver-Machine Interface:** The mandatory MMI requirements for cab radios depend on the existence and currency of the CENELEC Standard for the Driver-Machine Interface for ERTMS. Any changes to this standard may require corresponding changes to the EIRENE cab radio specification.

**Summary of ERTMS Communications Requirements Document:** The detailed data communications requirements for ERTMS/ETCS are contained in a separate document (Summary of ERTMS Communications Requirements). The EIRENE functional specification is dependent on this document for the full definition of train control data bearer requirements.

**UIC Fiche 651 (Cab Equipment Layout):** The physical design requirements for cab radio installation depend on the current version of UIC fiche 651. Changes to this fiche may affect the physical installation requirements.

**UIC Fiche 641 (Automatic Vigilance Devices):** The driver safety device interface requirements depend on the current version of UIC fiche 641.

**National Frequency Allocation:** The ability to operate in the 900 MHz railway frequency bands depends on national frequency allocation decisions by regulatory authorities in each country. It is assumed that appropriate spectrum has been allocated for railway use in all countries where EIRENE will be deployed.

**ISO 9001 Certification:** Equipment manufacturers are required to hold ISO 9001 certification. Procurement processes must verify this certification as a condition of supply.

**Requirement 3.5.7 Technical Specification:** The requirement for cab radios entering a group call area to automatically join an ongoing call to all drivers in the same area (requirement 3.5.7) is noted as requiring further technical specification changes before field implementation can be achieved. This requirement is dependent on future technical work and cannot be implemented in the initial deployment.

**Group Call Area Definitions at Network Boundaries:** The allocation of pre-defined local areas for group and broadcast calls at network boundaries is dependent on bilateral agreements between adjacent network operators. These agreements must be in place before cross-border group call functionality can be fully operational.

---

*End of Business Requirements Document*

*This document is intended to provide sufficient business context for a requirements engineer to elicit the detailed functional and non-functional requirements for the EIRENE railway radio communication system. It should be read in conjunction with the UIC EIRENE programme documentation, the Summary of ERTMS Communications Requirements, and the relevant CENELEC and UIC standards referenced herein.*

ground_truth_requirements.label: FR

ground_truth_requirements.text: 4.1.2 Three distinct mobile radio types are required, based on the type of role they will perform and the environment in which they will operate, as follows: (I) a) Cab radio for use by the driver of a train and/or by other on-train systems, eg ERTMS/ETCS; b) General purpose radio for general use by railway personnel; c) Operational radio for use by railway personnel involved in train operations such as shunting and trackside maintenance. Note: It is possible that the General purpose and Operational radios may have a number of variants to meet railway requirements (eg handheld and vehicle mounted). 4.1.2 Three distinct mobile radio types are required, based on the type of role they will perform and the environment in which they will operate, as follows: (I) a) Cab radio for use by the driver of a train and/or by other on-train systems, eg ERTMS/ETCS; b) General purpose radio for general use by railway personnel; c) Operational radio for use by railway personnel involved in train operations such as shunting and trackside maintenance. Note: It is possible that the General purpose and Operational radios may have a number of variants to meet railway requirements (eg handheld and vehicle mounted).
ground_truth_requirements.label: FR

ground_truth_requirements.text: 4.3.1 This sub-section defines the core environmental and physical requirements for all EIRENE mobile equipment. The requirements provided in this section are augmented by those provided in later sections for each individual radio type, with each radio type being specified by the superset of the core plus specific requirements. (I) 4.3.1 This sub-section defines the core environmental and physical requirements for all EIRENE mobile equipment. (I)

ground_truth_requirements.text: 4.3.2 The categories of requirements defined for each type of mobile equipment are as follows: (I) 4.3.2 The categories of requirements defined for each type of mobile equipment are as follows: (I) climatic conditions (temperature, humidity, solar radiation, altitude, etc); physical conditions (flammability, contamination, physical protection, etc); mechanical conditions (shock and vibration); electrical conditions (power supply variation, battery life, overloading, etc); EMC (both emissions and immunity).

ground_truth_requirements.text: 5.2.1.2 The following functions will be provided: Driver call-related functions: 5.2.1.2 The following functions will be provided: Driver call-related functions: call controller: (M) primary controller; secondary controller; power supply controller; call other drivers in the area; (M) send Railway emergency call; (M) confirm receipt of Railway emergency call; (M) communicate with other drivers on same train; (M) call train staff; (M) call other authorised users; (M) receive incoming voice calls; (M) terminate calls; (M) receive text messages; (M) enter/leave shunting mode; (M) enter/leave direct mode; (O) monitor calls to other on-train users/devices; (M) forward calls/cancel call forwarding to/from driver handheld; (O) Other driver-related functions: powering up radio; (M) switch radio MMI on and off; (M) select language; (M) adjust loudspeaker volume; (M) select mobile radio network; (M) register and deregister train number; (M) register and deregister on-train users; (O) register and deregister stock number; (M) store/retrieve numbers and their details; (M) invoke supplementary services; (M) invoke tests; (M) Other Cab radio functions: automatic connection of incoming calls to appropriate on-train users or devices (conductor, public address system, data systems, etc); (M) automatic establishment of outgoing calls initiated by on-train users or devices; (M) automatic handling of calls of varying priorities; (M) send to the controller(s) a signal on activation of driver safety device; (M) transmit Railway emergency call event indication to train-borne recorder; (M) run-time diagnostics. (O)

brd_document:
# Business Requirements Document (BRD)

## Project: ERTMS/ETCS Onboard Train Control System
**Project ID:** 2007-ertms
**Document Status:** Draft — Pre-Requirements Elicitation
**Prepared by:** Business Analysis Team
**Target Audience:** Requirements Engineers, System Architects, Infrastructure Managers, Safety Authorities

---

## 1. PROBLEM STATEMENT

### 1.1 Background

European railways currently operate under a fragmented patchwork of national train protection and control systems. A train crossing from one country to another — or even from one national network to another within the same country — may encounter incompatible signalling, speed supervision, and movement authority systems. This incompatibility forces operators to either equip locomotives with multiple national systems (adding weight, cost, and maintenance burden) or to change traction units at national borders, introducing significant operational delays and cost.

The absence of a unified, interoperable train control standard creates the following measurable pain points:

- **Border crossing delays:** Locomotive changes or system reconfigurations at national borders can add 30–90 minutes per crossing, reducing the competitiveness of rail freight and passenger services against road and air transport.
- **Safety inconsistency:** National systems vary in their ability to enforce speed limits, movement authorities, and emergency braking. Accident investigations have repeatedly identified inadequate or absent automatic train protection as a contributing factor in serious incidents.
- **High onboard equipment cost:** Equipping a single locomotive with five or more national train protection systems can cost in excess of €500,000 per unit, with ongoing maintenance complexity.
- **Operational speed ceiling:** Many national systems were designed for speeds well below 300 km/h, creating a technical barrier to the deployment of high-speed services on cross-border corridors.
- **Driver workload:** Drivers operating across multiple national systems must be trained and certified on each system individually, increasing training costs and the risk of human error during system transitions.

### 1.2 The Core Problem

There is no single, standardised, interoperable European train control system capable of supervising train movements safely across all network types, speed regimes (up to 500 km/h), and operational scenarios — from high-speed full supervision to low-speed shunting — while also providing a managed, backward-compatible transition path from existing national systems.

The consequence is that European rail cannot realise its potential as a safe, efficient, and competitive transport mode without a common train control standard that can be deployed incrementally across the network.

---

## 2. BUSINESS OBJECTIVES & SUCCESS CRITERIA

### 2.1 Business Objectives

| Ref | Objective | Rationale |
|-----|-----------|-----------|
| BO-01 | Provide a single, interoperable train control system operable across all participating European networks | Eliminate border-crossing delays and multi-system equipment costs |
| BO-02 | Enforce safe train movement supervision automatically, reducing reliance on driver vigilance alone | Reduce the frequency and severity of train accidents attributable to human error or absent ATP |
| BO-03 | Support incremental deployment across network types (unfitted, partially fitted, fully fitted) without requiring simultaneous infrastructure upgrades | Allow phased investment by infrastructure managers |
| BO-04 | Provide a managed, supervised transition path from existing national train protection systems | Protect existing infrastructure investment while enabling migration |
| BO-05 | Reduce the onboard equipment footprint required for cross-border operation | Lower per-unit equipment and maintenance costs |
| BO-06 | Enable high-speed operation up to 500 km/h under full automatic supervision | Support next-generation high-speed rail deployment |
| BO-07 | Provide complete, tamper-evident onboard data recording to support accident investigation and driver performance assessment | Meet regulatory obligations and support operational improvement |

### 2.2 Success Criteria & KPIs

| KPI | Target | Source Rationale |
|-----|--------|-----------------|
| Maximum supervised train speed | 500 km/h | System must remain functional at this speed |
| Driver warning lead time before ETCS intervention | ≥ 5 seconds | Sufficient time for driver to react and avoid automatic brake application |
| Driver acknowledgement window for Full-to-Partial Supervision transition | ≤ 5 seconds before brake application | Ensures timely driver awareness of degraded supervision |
| Onboard data retention — accident investigation data | ≥ 24 hours, high granularity | Regulatory requirement for post-incident analysis |
| Onboard data retention — operational/driver performance data | ≥ 7 days | Operational assessment requirement |
| Train running number format | Maximum 8 numeric digits | Standardised identification across all networks |
| Speed display accuracy | Zero discrepancy between displayed speed and supervised speed | Safety-critical: driver must not be misled |
| RBC handover with dual radio | No performance penalty (no reduction in train spacing or speed) | Seamless cross-RBC-area operation |
| Brake application on unacknowledged level transition | Automatic, immediate | Safety enforcement |
| Emergency brake on train trip | Applied until train is stationary | Safety enforcement |
| Odometry error | Must be accounted for in all position and braking calculations | Safety margin requirement |
| National values persistence | Retained through onboard equipment power-off cycles | Operational continuity |

---

## 3. STAKEHOLDERS

### 3.1 Stakeholder Register

| Stakeholder | Role | Primary Need |
|-------------|------|-------------|
| **Train Driver** | End user of the onboard DMI; responsible for safe train operation | Clear, unambiguous information about permitted speed, movement authority, warnings, and system status; minimal cognitive burden during transitions |
| **Infrastructure Manager (IM)** | Owns and operates the trackside equipment (balises, loops, RBC, radio network) | Confidence that onboard equipment will correctly interpret and respond to trackside commands; ability to enforce movement authorities and emergency stops |
| **Train Operating Company (TOC) / Railway Undertaking (RU)** | Operates trains; responsible for fleet equipment and driver training | Reduced per-unit equipment cost; interoperability across multiple national networks; reliable, certifiable system |
| **Radio Block Centre (RBC) Operator** | Manages train movements in Level 2/3 areas via continuous radio communication | Accurate, timely train position reports; ability to issue, revoke, and modify movement authorities; emergency stop capability |
| **National Safety Authority (NSA)** | Regulatory body responsible for approving system deployment and certifying safety | Demonstrated compliance with safety requirements; complete audit trail; defined behaviour in all failure modes |
| **European Railway Agency (ERA) / Notified Body** | Defines and enforces the CCS TSI (Technical Specification for Interoperability) | Conformance to the ERTMS/ETCS specification; interoperability across member states |
| **Onboard System Integrator** | Integrates ETCS onboard equipment with traction unit (brakes, power, DMI, STMs) | Well-defined interfaces; clear isolation and failure behaviour; STM integration specification |
| **STM (Specific Transmission Module) Supplier** | Provides the national system interface module | Defined data exchange protocol between ETCS onboard and STM; ability to pass national system data to driver via DMI |
| **Accident Investigation Body** | Investigates incidents and accidents | Access to detailed, time-stamped onboard recordings via standardised output interfaces |
| **Fleet Maintenance Organisation** | Maintains onboard equipment | Self-test capability; clear failure indication; isolation procedure |

---

## 4. CURRENT STATE vs FUTURE STATE

### 4.1 Current State Limitations

| Dimension | Current State |
|-----------|--------------|
| **Interoperability** | Each national network operates its own train protection system (e.g., TVM, LZB, TPWS, BACC, etc.). Cross-border trains require multiple onboard systems, each independently certified and maintained. |
| **Speed supervision** | National systems impose varying speed supervision capabilities; many cannot support speeds above 300 km/h or provide continuous supervision. |
| **Movement authority** | Movement authorities are typically conveyed via lineside signals; automatic enforcement of end-of-authority is limited or absent on many networks. |
| **Driver information** | Drivers receive information from multiple independent displays, increasing cognitive load and the risk of misinterpretation. |
| **Shunting supervision** | Shunting movements are often unsupervised or supervised only by lineside signals, with no automatic speed enforcement. |
| **Data recording** | Recording standards vary by country; data formats are not standardised, complicating cross-border accident investigation. |
| **RBC / radio supervision** | Continuous radio-based supervision is absent on most networks; train position is not reported in real time to a central controller. |
| **Failure handling** | Failure modes and driver procedures vary by national system; there is no harmonised response to transmission failures or equipment faults. |
| **Multi-traction / tandem** | Handling of multiple traction units under a single control system is not standardised; isolation requirements vary. |

### 4.2 Future State Enabled by ERTMS/ETCS

| Dimension | Future State |
|-----------|-------------|
| **Interoperability** | A single onboard ETCS system, supplemented by STMs where required, enables operation across all ETCS application levels (0, 1, 2, 3) and national systems. A Level 3-equipped train can operate on Level 3, 2, 1, and 0 lines without additional onboard systems. |
| **Speed supervision** | Continuous, automatic speed supervision up to 500 km/h, with calculated braking curves based on train data and infrastructure data, enforced by automatic brake intervention. |
| **Movement authority** | Movement authorities are issued, tracked, and enforced digitally. End-of-authority is supervised automatically. Authorities can be revoked cooperatively. Time-outs on sections of the MA are enforced automatically. |
| **Driver information** | A single, standardised DMI provides all relevant information: current speed, permitted speed, braking curve distance, operational mode, level, warnings, and system messages — in the driver's selected language. |
| **Shunting supervision** | Shunting movements are supervised to a nationally defined ceiling speed. Train trip function is available for shunting movements passing a danger signal. |
| **Data recording** | All operationally and safety-relevant events are recorded onboard with UTC timestamps and position references, retained for defined periods, and exportable via standardised interfaces. |
| **RBC / radio supervision** | In Levels 2 and 3, continuous radio communication with the RBC provides real-time position reporting, dynamic movement authority management, and emergency stop capability for individual trains or all trains in an area. |
| **Failure handling** | Defined, harmonised failure responses (emergency brake, service brake, or proceed to end of MA) are configurable via national values. All failures are displayed on the DMI and, where possible, reported to the RBC. |
| **Multi-traction / tandem** | Multiple and tandem traction unit configurations are supported without requiring isolation of non-leading units; train trip and roll-away protection are suppressed on non-leading units. |

---

## 5. BUSINESS RULES

### 5.1 Application Level Rules

- **BR-L01:** A train equipped for a given ETCS application level shall be capable of operating on that level and all lower levels. A train equipped for Level 3 may operate on Levels 3, 2, 1, and 0. A train equipped for Level 2 may operate on Levels 2, 1, and 0. A train equipped for Level 1 may operate on Levels 1 and 0 only.
- **BR-L02:** When a train enters a line fitted with multiple ETCS levels, the onboard shall automatically select the highest level for which it is equipped, according to the priority order specified by the trackside.
- **BR-L03:** If a train enters a level transition for which it is not equipped, the onboard shall immediately apply the emergency brake.
- **BR-L04:** The current application level shall always be visible to the driver on the DMI.
- **BR-L05:** Level transitions requiring driver acknowledgement shall trigger a brake application if the driver fails to acknowledge in time. The brake shall be releasable upon subsequent acknowledgement.

### 5.2 Operational Mode Rules

- **BR-M01:** Any mode transition occurring while the train is moving shall occur automatically. Transitions while stationary may be automatic or manual as appropriate.
- **BR-M02:** If an automatic mode transition increases driver responsibility (except transitions to/from National Operation via STM), the system shall seek driver acknowledgement regardless of whether the train is moving or stationary.
- **BR-M03:** Transitions to/from National Operation (STM) shall always require driver acknowledgement.
- **BR-M04:** If a required acknowledgement is not given, the system shall apply the brakes.
- **BR-M05:** During any transition period between two operational modes, the supervision provided shall be at least as restrictive as the least restrictive of the two modes involved.
- **BR-M06:** Transfer to Shunting mode by driver selection shall only be permitted when the train is stationary.
- **BR-M07:** If operating under RBC control, transfer to Shunting requires explicit RBC permission, which shall be indicated to the driver.
- **BR-M08:** Automatic transfer to Shunting from Full or Partial Supervision is permitted only at speeds at or below the supervised shunting speed, and requires driver confirmation.
- **BR-M09:** Exit from Shunting mode shall only be possible when the train is stationary.
- **BR-M10:** Transfer from Full Supervision to Partial Supervision requiring acknowledgement shall be acknowledged within 5 seconds; failure to do so shall result in brake application.
- **BR-M11:** Full Supervision shall be entered automatically when a complete movement authority and all necessary information are received from the trackside.
- **BR-M12:** Unfitted operation shall be available either on trackside command or on driver selection at start-up.

### 5.3 Train Data Rules

- **BR-D01:** Train data entry shall be completed before the onboard ETCS permits any train movement.
- **BR-D02:** Manual entry or overwriting of train data by the driver shall only be permitted when the train is stationary.
- **BR-D03:** Previously stored train data shall be presented to the driver for confirmation at the start of each Data Entry session.
- **BR-D04:** Train data shall be retained in onboard equipment until the traction unit is taken out of service (except on transition to Shunting mode).
- **BR-D05:** Driver identification and Train Running Number may be changed during a journey.
- **BR-D06:** The Train Running Number shall be a maximum of 8 numeric digits.
- **BR-D07:** If the onboard fails to contact the RBC on awakening, the driver shall be prompted to enter RBC contact details manually.

### 5.4 Speed Supervision Rules

- **BR-S01:** The speed displayed to the driver on the DMI shall be identical to the speed used for supervision purposes; no discrepancy is permitted.
- **BR-S02:** The driver shall receive a warning at least 5 seconds before any ETCS brake intervention within a braking curve area.
- **BR-S03:** If the train exceeds the permitted ceiling speed by the harmonised margin, the onboard shall apply a brake intervention. The driver may release the brake once actual speed no longer exceeds the permitted speed.
- **BR-S04:** Braking curves shall be calculated using both train data and infrastructure data, accounting for target information but not the position of other vehicles on the track.
- **BR-S05:** Both an emergency braking curve and a service braking curve shall be calculated.
- **BR-S06:** Where the service brake is detected as insufficient, the emergency brake shall be applied to stop the train before the danger point.
- **BR-S07:** Release speed shall be calculated onboard or provided by the trackside; trackside-provided release speed takes priority.
- **BR-S08:** Speed increase to a higher speed level shall be governed by the rear end of the train; speed decrease to a lower level shall be governed by the front end of the train.
- **BR-S09:** Certain locations (e.g. tunnels) may be defined where speed increase is governed by the front of the train.

### 5.5 Movement Authority Rules

- **BR-A01:** If track data covering at least the full extent of the movement authority is not available onboard, the movement authority shall be rejected.
- **BR-A02:** Movement authority time-outs for defined sections shall be enforced onboard; expiry of a time-out shall shorten the movement authority accordingly.
- **BR-A03:** Movement authority revocation shall be cooperative between RBC and train. If the train cannot stop at the proposed new target, it shall reject the revocation and retain the original authority.
- **BR-A04:** Before a Full Supervision movement authority is issued, the trackside may request driver confirmation that the track ahead is clear.
- **BR-A05:** Before entering an occupied track section, driver acknowledgement shall be required.

### 5.6 Roll-Away and Reverse Movement Protection Rules

- **BR-R01:** The onboard shall continuously monitor the direction of movement against the permitted direction.
- **BR-R02:** After travelling a distance defined by a national value in an unauthorised direction, the emergency brake shall be applied automatically.
- **BR-R03:** Roll-away/reverse movement intervention shall be indicated on the DMI.
- **BR-R04:** The driver may release the emergency brake only after the traction unit has come to a standstill.
- **BR-R05:** Roll-away protection shall be active only on the leading traction unit in a multiple-unit consist.

### 5.7 Train Trip Rules

- **BR-T01:** When a traction unit passes a stop signal, the emergency brake shall be triggered automatically (train trip function).
- **BR-T02:** The emergency brake shall remain applied until the traction unit is stationary.
- **BR-T03:** The driver shall acknowledge the train trip condition before the emergency brake is released.
- **BR-T04:** After acknowledgement, the driver may move the train backwards for a distance defined by a national value.
- **BR-T05:** The train trip function shall be suppressed on non-leading traction units in multiple-unit and tandem configurations.
- **BR-T06:** The train trip function may be applied to shunting movements passing a signal showing "danger for shunting."

### 5.8 Override and Isolation Rules

- **BR-O01:** The override control (for passing a stop signal under authorisation) shall be protected against inadvertent operation.
- **BR-O02:** When the override is active and the train passes the stop signal, the train trip function shall be suppressed; actual speed shall remain displayed; a special indication shall be shown; the supervised speed shall not be displayed.
- **BR-O03:** Route suitability override shall only be possible when the train is stationary; after override, the movement authority shall be re-established.
- **BR-O04:** When the ETCS onboard is isolated, it shall be disconnected from the vehicle braking system and shall display only the isolation status — no other ETCS information.

### 5.9 National Values Rules

- **BR-N01:** National values received from the trackside shall apply to a defined geographic area.
- **BR-N02:** National values shall persist through onboard equipment power-off cycles.
- **BR-N03:** If no valid national values exist for the current location, harmonised default values permanently stored in the onboard equipment shall be used.

### 5.10 Data Recording Rules

- **BR-REC01:** All data entered, received, or indicated to the driver shall be recorded onboard, referenced to UTC and a geographic reference point.
- **BR-REC02:** Accident investigation data shall be retained for a minimum of 24 hours at high granularity.
- **BR-REC03:** Operational/driver performance data shall be retained for a minimum of 7 days.
- **BR-REC04:** Recorded data shall be exportable via standardised output interfaces.
- **BR-REC05:** The minimum recorded data set shall include: level and mode transitions, driver confirmations, supervision data, national system information, actual speed, service brake interventions, emergency brake interventions, train trip activations, override selections, route suitability overrides, isolation events, and all data entered/received/displayed.

### 5.11 Reversing Rules

- **BR-REV01:** Reversing shall only be possible from a single active cab that remains open throughout the procedure.
- **BR-REV02:** Reversing shall be supervised to speed and distance limits defined as national values, extendable by trackside instruction.
- **BR-REV03:** Once reversing commences, the existing movement authority shall be cancelled.
- **BR-REV04:** The driver shall not be required to re-confirm train data to use the Reversing function.

### 5.12 RBC Handover Rules

- **BR-RBC01:** Transition between RBC areas shall occur automatically without driver intervention.
- **BR-RBC02:** With two operational radios, RBC handover shall impose no performance penalty on train spacing or speed.
- **BR-RBC03:** With one operational radio, RBC handover shall remain possible but may result in a performance penalty.

### 5.13 Transmission Failure Rules

- **BR-F01:** In the event of a transmission failure, the onboard shall respond according to the applicable national value, selecting from: (a) immediate emergency brake with DMI indication; (b) immediate full service brake with DMI indication; or (c) proceed unrestricted to end of movement authority with DMI indication and driver notification.
- **BR-F02:** Equipment failures compromising safety supervision shall cause the onboard to immediately apply the brakes and bring the train to a stop.
- **BR-F03:** All failures shall be displayed on the DMI. Where possible, performance restrictions shall be reported to the RBC.

---

## 6. SCOPE

### 6.1 In Scope

| Area | Description |
|------|-------------|
| **ETCS Onboard Equipment** | All software and hardware functions of the ETCS trainborne equipment, including speed supervision, braking curve calculation, movement authority management, mode and level management, and DMI interface |
| **Driver-Machine Interface (DMI)** | All information displayed to and received from the driver, including speed, mode, level, warnings, text messages, and data entry screens |
| **Application Levels 0, 1, 2, 3** | Full specification of behaviour at each level and during transitions between levels |
| **All Operational Modes** | Full Supervision, Partial Supervision, Staff Responsible, On Sight, Unfitted, Train Trip, Post Trip, National (STM), Tandem, Multiple, Shunting, Stand By, Reversing |
| **STM Interface** | Data exchange between ETCS onboard and connected Specific Transmission Modules for national system compatibility |
| **RBC Communication Interface** | Train-to-trackside and trackside-to-train communication in Levels 2 and 3, including position reporting, movement authority exchange, emergency stop, and train data transmission |
| **Balise/Loop/Radio Reception** | Reception and processing of track-to-train information via all specified transmission media |
| **Onboard Data Recording** | Recording, retention, and export of all specified data categories |
| **Braking System Interface** | ETCS commands to the vehicle braking system (service brake, emergency brake, isolation) |
| **National Values Management** | Receipt, storage, persistence, and application of national values and default values |
| **Multi-traction and Tandem Support** | Behaviour of ETCS in multiple-unit and tandem traction configurations |
| **Pantograph, Power Supply, and Air Tightness Information** | Receipt and display of infrastructure-commanded traction and environmental information |
| **Plain Text and Fixed Text Messaging** | Receipt and display of track-to-train text messages in multiple languages |
| **Brake Inhibition Information** | Receipt and display of regenerative, eddy current, and magnetic shoe brake inhibition commands |
| **Train Integrity Reporting** | Transmission of train integrity information to trackside; manual driver confirmation |

### 6.2 Out of Scope

| Area | Rationale |
|------|-----------|
| **Trackside RBC Software** | The RBC is a separate system; this BRD covers only the onboard equipment and its interfaces |
| **Balise Encoder / Lineside Equipment** | Infrastructure equipment is outside the onboard system boundary |
| **National Train Protection Systems (other than STM interface)** | National systems are interfaced via STM; their internal logic is out of scope |
| **Traction Control Systems** | ETCS interfaces with but does not control traction; traction management is out of scope |
| **GSM-R / Radio Network Infrastructure** | The radio network is a separate infrastructure system; ETCS uses it but does not specify it |
| **Maintenance Management Systems** | Depot and fleet management systems are out of scope |
| **Passenger Information Systems** | No passenger-facing outputs are within scope |
| **Automatic Train Operation (ATO)** | Driverless or automated driving functions are not addressed in this specification |

---

## 7. CONSTRAINTS

### 7.1 Regulatory and Standards Constraints

- **C-REG01:** The system shall conform to the Control-Command and Signalling (CCS) Technical Specification for Interoperability (TSI) as published by the European Union Agency for Railways (ERA). All interfaces and behaviours shall be consistent with the applicable TSI version.
- **C-REG02:** The system shall be compatible with all existing national train control systems listed in the CCS TSI, neither interfering with nor being interfered with by those systems.
- **C-REG03:** The system shall be certifiable by a recognised Notified Body under the European railway interoperability framework.
- **C-REG04:** Safety integrity requirements shall be consistent with EN 50126 (RAMS), EN 50128 (software), and EN 50129 (safety case) standards applicable to railway control and protection systems.
- **C-REG05:** All data recording shall meet the requirements of applicable accident investigation regulations, including data retention periods and output interface standardisation.

### 7.2 Technical Constraints

- **C-TECH01:** The system shall be functional at train speeds from 0 to a maximum of 500 km/h.
- **C-TECH02:** The onboard equipment shall interface with the vehicle braking system to command service brake and emergency brake applications, and shall be capable of disconnecting from the braking system upon isolation.
- **C-TECH03:** National values shall be stored in non-volatile memory and shall survive power-off cycles.
- **C-TECH04:** Default national values shall be harmonised and permanently stored in all ETCS onboard equipment; they shall not be modifiable by operators or drivers.
- **C-TECH05:** The DMI character set shall support multiple European languages. Fixed text messages shall be rendered in the driver's selected language; plain text messages from trackside shall be displayed as received.
- **C-TECH06:** The onboard shall support connection of one or two operational radio units; behaviour shall be defined for both configurations, particularly during RBC handover.
- **C-TECH07:** The odometry system shall provide position and speed data with quantified error bounds; all supervision calculations shall account for odometry error.
- **C-TECH08:** The Train Running Number field shall accommodate a maximum of 8 numeric digits.
- **C-TECH09:** Standardised output interfaces shall be provided for extraction of recorded data to external media.

### 7.3 Operational Constraints

- **C-OPS01:** The onboard self-test at start-up shall be fully automatic and shall require no driver action.
- **C-OPS02:** Manual train data entry and overwriting shall only be possible when the train is stationary.
- **C-OPS03:** The Reversing function shall only be operable from a single active cab that remains open throughout the reversing procedure.
- **C-OPS04:** The override control for passing a stop signal shall be physically or logically protected against inadvertent activation.
- **C-OPS05:** Roll-away protection shall be automatically disabled on all non-leading traction units in a multiple-unit consist.
- **C-OPS06:** The train trip function shall be automatically suppressed on non-leading units in multiple and tandem configurations.

### 7.4 Interoperability Constraints

- **C-INT01:** An ETCS Level 3-equipped train shall be capable of operating on Level 3, 2, 1, and 0 lines. Level 2-equipped trains shall operate on Levels 2, 1, and 0. Level 1-equipped trains shall operate on Levels 1 and 0.
- **C-INT02:** It shall be possible to implement one or more ETCS application levels on a single line; the onboard shall select the highest level for which it is equipped, according to trackside priority.
- **C-INT03:** The system shall support STM connections for national system compatibility; the DMI shall be capable of displaying national system information, which may include replicating the national system's own display format.

---

## 8. ASSUMPTIONS & DEPENDENCIES

### 8.1 Assumptions

| Ref | Assumption |
|-----|-----------|
| **A-01** | The GSM-R (or equivalent) radio network infrastructure required for Level 2 and Level 3 operation is provided by the infrastructure manager and is available with sufficient coverage and reliability on lines where those levels are deployed. |
| **A-02** | Balise infrastructure (and optionally loops) required for Level 1 operation and for position reference in Levels 2 and 3 is installed and maintained by the infrastructure manager to the required specification. |
| **A-03** | The vehicle to which the ETCS onboard equipment is fitted provides compatible interfaces for braking system command (service brake and emergency brake), power supply, and physical mounting. |
| **A-04** | STMs for all national systems with which interoperability is required are available from qualified suppliers and conform to the ETCS STM interface specification. |
| **A-05** | The RBC systems deployed on Level 2 and Level 3 lines conform to the ERTMS/ETCS RBC specification and provide the required interfaces for movement authority management, emergency stop, and train data exchange. |
| **A-06** | Drivers operating ETCS-equipped trains will receive appropriate training on the ETCS DMI, operational modes, and required acknowledgement procedures before operating in revenue service. |
| **A-07** | A UTC time source is available to the onboard equipment for data recording purposes. |
| **A-08** | The infrastructure manager will provide accurate and up-to-date track description data (gradients, speed limits, balise locations, etc.) in the format required for onboard braking curve calculation. |
| **A-09** | National values for each network will be defined, validated, and loaded into the trackside transmission system by the relevant infrastructure manager prior to system commissioning. |
| **A-10** | The harmonised default national values have been agreed at a European level and are available for permanent storage in all onboard equipment prior to manufacture. |
| **A-11** | The vehicle odometry system provides speed and distance measurements with defined, bounded error characteristics that are known at the time of system integration. |

### 8.2 Dependencies

| Ref | Dependency | Owner | Impact if Not Met |
|-----|-----------|-------|-------------------|
| **D-01** | CCS TSI (current applicable version) must be formally published and stable before detailed system specification is finalised | ERA / European Commission | Specification instability; potential non-conformance |
| **D-02** | ETCS Subset specifications (particularly Subset-026 System Requirements Specification) must be available and consistent with this BRD | ERA | Detailed requirements cannot be derived without the subset documents |
| **D-03** | STM interface specification (Subset-035) must be agreed and published | ERA / STM suppliers | STM integration cannot be designed or tested |
| **D-04** | DMI specification (Subset-034) must be agreed and published | ERA | DMI design cannot proceed |
| **D-05** | RBC interface specification must be agreed between onboard and RBC suppliers | Infrastructure Manager / RBC supplier | Level 2/3 functionality cannot be integrated or tested |
| **D-06** | Vehicle brake interface specification must be provided by the rolling stock manufacturer | Rolling stock manufacturer | Brake command integration cannot be designed |
| **D-07** | National values for each target network must be provided by the relevant infrastructure manager before site acceptance testing | Infrastructure Manager | System cannot be validated for national network operation |
| **D-08** | Safety case framework (EN 50126/50128/50129) must be agreed with the relevant National Safety Authority before development commences | NSA / Project Safety Manager | Safety certification path is undefined |
| **D-09** | Odometry error model must be agreed with the vehicle manufacturer and validated against the track geometry of target networks | Rolling stock manufacturer / IM | Braking curve calculations cannot be validated |
| **D-10** | Standardised data recording output interface format must be agreed with accident investigation bodies and regulatory authorities | NSA / Accident Investigation Body | Recorded data may not be usable for investigation purposes |

---

*This document represents the business requirements baseline for the ERTMS/ETCS onboard train control system as understood prior to formal requirements elicitation. It is intended to guide requirements engineers in deriving detailed functional and non-functional requirements through structured workshops with the stakeholders identified in Section 3. All sections should be reviewed and validated with stakeholders before the requirements elicitation phase commences.*
